The course is an analytical study involving cases of retailing, stressing managerial functions, retail organization, location, layout, and policies, pricing brands, credits records purchasing, personnel administration, and the administration of retail store.

This course introduces the students to the principles of land surveying. They learn the Use of the engineer's compass, theodolite, level and plane table; linear and elevation measurements; surveying methods applied to problems in construction, traverse survey calculations of areas and contouring; trigonometric leveling, height, and distances, subtended barometric leveling, simple circular curve, survey for engineering projects.

This course continues to expose the students to principles of land surveying; They learn the Intersection and resection methods of calculations from given data; Tachometry system; Electromagnetic distance measurement methods; Use of GPS and other satellite --aided systems.

This course is an essential skill that is required for engineering students. It teaches students how to analyse data, present results and conclusions, and make recommendations in a logical, precise and accessible manner. The technical report writing style guide will be presented to assist the civil engineering students with their report writing.

Quantity surveying is primarily centred on construction and the management of the costs and budgets of large projects. From the moment a plan is drawn until a large construction project has been completed, a quantity surveyor is likely to be involved in a legal, technical and financial capacity, the students will be taught on some related legal matters aspect of the course. The students will be functions of a quantity concerned with the control of the cost on construction projects, the management and maintenance of the budget, valuations and any legal matters arising through the course of the project.

This course will be taken distinctly over a four-month period, during which students will be placed in engineering industries for exposure to engineering workplace and field experiences. Students will be introduced to managerial and standard operation procedures, engineering standards and codes of operations. Interns will be expected to work along with professional Engineers on sites and understand hands-on procedures and other significant details in the day-to-day responsibility of engineers. This is a monitoring course which students register for prior to their field assignment. A final report is expected of each student at the end of their program term along with a letter of recommendation from their sponsor. Extra fees may be attached to this course to facilitate the processing of their assignment. This will be set separately as Internship fees.

This course is an introductory concept for the analysis of structures. Axial forces, shear forces and bending moments in statically determinate structures such as beams, frames, and trusses due to applied loads are determined; deflection theory; methods for estimating deflections, influence lines, and moving loads are covered. Conjugate beams and graphical analysis of structures are introduced. An emphasis is placed on the application of basic analytical techniques.

This course deals with advanced techniques for the analysis of indeterminate structures, including Moment-area method, Virtual method, Force method, slope deflection method, and moment distribution method. Emphasis is placed on basic analytical techniques.

This course is an introduction to the description and characteristic of soil behavior as an engineering material. It includes the study of the physical properties of soil, such as the soil and water relationship. Altenburg's limit, soil permeability and seepage applications are discussed. Shear strength in soil, angle of friction and cohesion of conditions are introduced. Slip circles of soil is included.

This course introduces the key principles of geotechnical engineering includes investigating existing subsurface conditions and materials; determining their physical/mechanical and chemical properties, assessing risks posed by site conditions; designing earthworks and structure foundations; and monitoring site conditions; earthwork and foundation. Various factors such as soil consolidation and settlement of foundation are studied; time of settlement and methods of their evaluation are reviewed. Bearing capacities of soils for cases of shallow and deep foundations is introduced. Earth pressure theory is also considered.

This course Introduces the basic nature of fluids and instrumentation linked to fluid from an engineering perspective: Fluid static, hydrostatics pressure, flotation hydrometers, barometers, fluid dynamics without friction. Bernoulli's principle, venturi-meters, orifices, weirs notches. Loss of head, hydraulic structures and equipment, cavitation.

Introduction to the geological origins of construction materials and the effects of geological processes. General considerations on the use of materials in construction, required properties, selection, testing, design, and quality control of civil engineering materials. Element of design of ordinary Portland cement concrete, asphaltic concrete, masonry, steel, and wood construction are included; overview of composite and other materials in civil engineering.
This course is a generic lab to demonstrate civil engineering related functions: It starts with engineering mechanics loading, deflections, stress and principles of surveying.
This course is the continuation of civil engineering lab I. It covers topics such as soil mechanics, geotechnical and advance structural design.

This course shifts focus from structural analysis to design of structures. It starts with the design of structural members and connections. It shows the comparative strength of members depending on their composition. This first course investigates the properties of non-metal structures like concrete, wood or plastics, etc. Combined stresses, unsymmetrical bending, and plastic design theory are introduced. Design with concrete beans and wooden roof trusses are included. Environmental stresses and continuous frame designs are also included.

This course shifts focus from structural analysis to design of structures. It starts with the design of structural members and connections. It shows the comparative strength of members depending on their composition. This second course investigates the properties of metal structures like steel, aluminum or other metals, etc. Combined stresses, unsymmetrical bending, and plastic design theory are introduced. Design with steel girders and roof trusses are included. Settlement and temperature stresses, and continuous frame designs are also included.

This course introduces the students to specific engineering principles linked to the design of roads and highway, including design geometrics surveys, plans; Highway systems, planning, finance, contracts and administration.

This course continues with highway design principles covering, highway sub-grade structure, construction methods, and soil-stabilized roads. Highway based courses, asphalt pavement and Portland cement.
This course provides the opportunity for the student to develop the conceptual design of selected Project Work under the supervision of a Professor.
This course provides the opportunity for students to design the prototype of selected civil engineering project work under the supervision of a Professor.

This course covers the key principles environmental and sanitation engineering with emphasis on protection and management of the water systems. Topics include assessment of environmental quality; introduction to water treatment technologies; /Elements of design and operation/ of water treatment systems, including physical, chemical and biological treatment methods are discussed. Students are exposed to the e/ngineering aspects/ of collection, pumping, storage, and distribution of water for public, domestic, and industrial uses. Finally, students are exposed to various integrated water supply and pollution control design problems required to design creative solutions as a class project.

This course covers the key principles environmental and sanitation engineering with emphasis on management of the wastewater systems. Topics include assessment of environmental quality; basic understanding of collection of storms, sanitary and combined wastewater, and wastewater treatment technologies; /Elements of design and operation/ of wastewater treatment systems, including physical, chemical and biological treatment methods are discussed. Finally, students are exposed to various integrated wastewater systems and pollution control design problems required to design creative solutions as a class project.

This is the first of a two-part course which introduces students to practical specialized interest-based engineering topics and skills developments in exploring knowledge and solutions for gaps in the currently available scientific and engineering approach. It enhances students' ability to undertake Senior Project CENG 505.

This is the second of a two-part course which introduces students to the analysis and evaluation of new solutions for gaps found in existing knowledge and engineering solutions. It also promotes the need to realize ideals through physical designs, simulations and real engineering application, considering internal standards, and others engineering codes and regulations. This course helps students fulfil the requirement of ceng506.

This course covers the application of civil engineering fundamentals and analyses of construction activities revealing methods of improving quality value and at the same time reducing the cost of construction. Topics include: time value of money; depreciation of equipment; land clearing; ripping racks; tractor shovels; the theory of queues applied to trucks; batching of concrete; formwork, etc.

It is the first chemistry course designed to introduce the fundamental concepts of units and measurements, the scientific method, laws and theories of chemistry , including atomic structure and chemical bonding, the study of gas laws, acids, bases, salt, redox reactions, aqueous solutions, and stoichiometry (limiting reagents .actual yield ,theoretical, and percentage yield), periodic table and properties (trends)will be incorporated.

The course completes the fundamental concepts of laws and theories of chemistry. Its contents are tailored to provide the fundamental ideas of hybridization in chemical bonding (VBT), molecular geometry (MOT), gas laws, chemical kinetics, and chemical equilibrium, thermochemistry, entropy and free energy, acids, bases ,buffers and aqueous equilibria; thus calculations in hydrogen ion index, acid and base dissociations will be done (pH, pOH, pka, pKw).

It is an introductory organic chemistry course and as such important classes of organic compounds are studied with emphasis on the IUPAC nomenclature of main functional groups of the different classes of organic compounds in order to meet the requirements of organic chemistry II. It will highlight details carbon compounds; modern orbital and bonding theory; other theoretical aspects concerning structure of molecules and the relationship of structure and reactivity; study of simple saturated and unsaturated aliphatic compounds. Laboratory work includes identification of compounds and tests on functional groups. Qualitatively, it will present the chemistry of main classes of organic compounds, including their properties and reactivity.++++

This is a study of the important types of organic reaction with emphasis upon basic reaction mechanisms Optical activity of organic compounds. This course will proceed with the study of aromatic compounds, halides, phenols, ether, carboxylic acids and their derivatives; the carbonyl compound and nitrogen compounds. Laboratory work includes the reactions of different types of organic compounds and their identification by chemical tests.

The three environmental domains of the earth (Earth's atmosphere, water bodies, land) and the natural chemical processes and anthropogenic impacts. Human influences on each domain in relation to causes, effects, detection, mitigation, and remediation. Stratospheric ozone depletion, global climate change and energy, waste disposal, pollutants (organic and inorganic), fossil fuels, soil and water toxicity, and environmental regulation in Liberia. Laboratory activity will capture detection of pollutants in air and water and on land. Also includes some main topics from Environmental Mitigation Technology. Main ideas around water and wastewater treatment, solid hazardous waste; environmental toxicology, technology design for removal if pollutant from ground water and solids bioremediation; phytoremediation; role of environmental biotechnology in pollutants treatment.

This course Environmental Mitigation Technology emphasizes water and wastewater treatment, solid hazardous waste; environmental toxicology, technology design for removal of pollutants from groundwater and soils; bioremediation; phytoremediation; the role of ecological biotechnology in pollution abatement

This is a four (4) credit hour course and the first of two (2) inorganic courses. It is a required core course for students majoring in chemistry; an elective for biology and science education majors. This course deals primarily with fundamental structural quantum properties of the atom of elements and principally the theoretical and empirical aspects of the periodic table whereby a thorough survey of the properties of the elements in terms of groups, trends, and relationship are covered.

It entails the study of the activities and properties transition metal compounds in the Modern Periodic Table (MPT) and their relationship and usefulness in other chemical reaction process. Related theories of acids, bases, complexes, and ligands are entrenched. In addition, ligand and crystal field theories are included. Medicinal Inorganic Chemistry will be incorporated in this course.

This course consists of the theories and applications of spectroscopic techniques such as mass spectrometry (MS), Infrared (IR) Spectroscopy, Ultraviolet --Visible (UV-VIS) Spectroscopy and Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) with emphasis on their applications to structural determination.

The thrust of this course is to make available the fundamental concepts and applications in electrochemical reaction processes. This course will focus on the detailed explanation of electrochemical phenomena in relation to redox chemistry; understanding all electrochemical theories will therefore permit students to utilize such as models to better understand the construction and principles of operation of electrochemical devices.

Chemistry 305 is one set of an advanced organic chemistry course, with Chemistry 202 prerequisite. It is a core course for especially chemistry majors. Its content is devised to grasp knowledge on natural products of plant and animal origin their isolation, application and usefulness for medicinal purposes and as chemotherapy. Laboratory activities will be highly employed.

Introduction to thermodynamic feasibility, kinetic feasibility, chemical plant, and energy sources. Industrial consideration reaction, Evaluation, Selection, Feasibility, Management and Economics system approach, organization, analysis and control, research and development project evaluation or human relation in industry. Industrial aspect of organic and inorganic products, their chemistry (toxic, non-toxic). Limit test for heavy metals Pb, Ag, Hg, Fe and ash content. Introduction to Liberia Industries: Cement, soap, Paint, Match, natural and synthetic products rubber, petroleum refinery (oil and natural gas) and steel industry, in addition to these of high concern include logging and mining companies, distillery and confectionary, rubber latex processing.

This course forms an integral part of organic Chemistry as it is of primordial importance in the study of the structural arrangement of organic compounds in space (conformational analysis) ranging from linear, planar, Triligant, Quadriligant compounds and their derivatives coupled with four, six- coordinated metal complexes and also metal complexes with coordination numbers other than four or six.

The thrust of this course attracts the chemistry of the three environmental domains of the earth (Earth's atmosphere, water bodies, land) also the natural chemical processes and anthropogenic impacts. Emphasis will be placed on human influences on each domain as indicated above in relation to causes, effects, detection, mitigation, and remediation. This course encapsulates concepts in chemistry, and others. Major topics to be covered are thus; Stratospheric ozone depletion, global climate change and energy, waste disposal, pollutants (organic and inorganic), fossil fuels, soil and water toxicity, and environmental regulation in Liberia. Laboratory activity will capture detection of pollutants in air and water and on land.

This course is an introduction to the use of instruments in the measurement of quantities whose magnitude can be correlated with the concentration or total amount of a substance in solution. Instrumental analysis also investigates the use of scientific instruments to study systems. Typical topics included are spectroscopy, nuclear spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, crystallography, electrochemical analysis, thermal analysis, separations, and microscopy.

This course is an introduction to quantum chemistry with applications to elementary spectroscopy and kinetics. It specifically deals with units and measurement systems, basic thermodynamics, solution, surface and colloidal chemistry their concepts/principles and applications to biological systems and processes.

It is the second of the two physical chemistry courses; core course for chemistry major students and an elective for science education students. It is an extended study of physical chemistry I with emphasis on calculations involving thermodynamics (real and ideal systems) at room and above room temperature reactions (industrial processes), phase and phase equilibria calculations kinetics and advanced electrochemistry (electrochemical systems). Laboratory activities will be highly employed in this course

The treatment of the Theory of Modern Chemical Analysis; it will incorporate basic types of chemical analysis sample, sample types, sample collection techniques, sample preservation of solids , liquids and gaseous samples statistical methods of analysis in analytical chemistry; standard laboratory activities will be employed (titrimetry).

Course is an advanced analytical course for undergraduate chemistry majors; with Chemistry 401 and Chemistry 407 being pre-requisites. It entails instruction on conceptual principles and practice of modern methods of chemical analysis of substances via understanding light properties coupled with electromagnetic spectrum, Beer's law and the instrumentation and analytical techniques involved in the following: Mass spectroscopy (MS), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), flame emission spectroscopy (FES) chromatography and electro-gravimetry/coulometry etc. There will be an introduction to lasers in analysis (LA ICP-MS), LA ICP-OES.
