course_title: Human Anatomy and Physiology I (with Lab)
description: This course focuses on the structures of the human body and how these structures work together. It includes the gross and microscopic structure and function of the human body. Topics include body organization; homeostasis; cytology; histology; and the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous systems, and special senses. It demonstrates an in-depth understanding of the principles of anatomy and physiology and their interrelationship. The course involves laboratory work which may include the dissection of preserved specimens, microscopic study, physiological experiments, computer simulations, and multimedia.

course_title: Organic Chemistry with Biochemistry for Health Sciences

description: This course introduces the basic concepts of food and nutrition to highlight ways that students can integrate good nutrition into their lifestyles. Principles of digestion and absorption, the function of nutrients, lifecycle nutritive needs, disease prevention, diet modifications, and weight management are covered. Practical application of these principles to the students' lives is emphasized.

This course is designed to discuss the causes, treatment, and preventive measures of selected tropical and communicable diseases and diseases of public health importance and epidemic potential in tropical settings. For nursing students This course is designed to discuss the causes, treatment, and preventive measures of selected tropical and communicable diseases and diseases of public health importance and epidemic potential in tropical settings. It will focus on infectious disease identification, control, and management with a closer examination of those endemic within Liberia. Examination of the shifting multi-level, multi-modal burden of disease. Principles of outbreak documentation and notification will also be covered. This clinical course will enable learners to demonstrate using the nursing process to assess client populations with suspected or confirmed exposure to tropical and communicable diseases. Planning primary, secondary, and tertiary communicable disease interventions and evaluation of interventions will be the focus of this clinical course. For Publich Health students:...
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

This course discusses the 8 elements of primary health care; emphasis is placed on community mobilization, participation in their health care, and immunization. References to Liberia's Expanded Programs on Immunization and the immunization policies will be made. Fieldwork activities include visits to various levels of the Liberian Health Care Systems starting with the community level up to the level of the regional hospital to observe activities at each level. Here, students observe and participate in immunization sessions.

This course is designed to introduce students to how society views critical health issues. The course will specifically deal with issues of stigmatization in the context of HIV/AIDS and mental health as well as other determinants of health in the broader context of health and wellness in resource-poor and underdeveloped communities. This is a general requirement for all students in the College of Health Sciences.

course_title: Human Anatomy and Physiology II (with Lab)
description: As the sequel to chsc102 Human Anatomy and Physiology I, this course provides students with an understanding of the role and function of the digestive, urinary, reproductive, nervous, and hormonal systems as well as structure and function of the eye, nose, ear, sinuses, and mouth of the human body. It introduces common human disease processes. Laboratory components include anatomical studies using microscopy and dissection and the study of physiological concepts via experimentation.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

This course provides an overview of epidemiologic methods used in research studies that address disease patterns in community and clinic-based populations. It is designed to introduce students to the basic theories and skills of epidemiologic principles. Using a broad multi-disciplinary perspective, the course examines selected health topics that have great effects on individual and group behaviors and their implications in epidemiological studies of population health.

course_title: Emergency and Crisis Management in Healthcare
description: This course provides students with strategies and methodologies on how to manage a crisis in a variety of healthcare settings. Emphasis is placed on how to communicate with the public as well as preparedness strategies, emergency planning, and assessment of hazards and resources. Studies include in-depth planning and analytical framework, hazard/vulnerability analysis, and management.

This course focuses on financial decision making in the modern corporation. The basic issues include capital budgeting/corporate investment, capital structure, corporate sources of funding, dividend policy and corporate contingent claims, and financial risk management. Course concepts are integrated into the standard theories of risk and return, valuation of assets, and market structure (i.e., the concepts developed in the Financial Management course, will be heavily utilized).

This course allows graduating senior students of accounting to carry out independent academic research in his/her profession on any approved topic in accounting but under the close supervision of an assigned professor from the department. Note: Accounting Internship to be done during the vacation after the student shall have completed the Senior year, Semester II.

This course serves as the foundation for students' research endeavors, guiding them through the process of developing a comprehensive research proposal. Students will learn key elements of research design, including formulating research questions, conducting literature reviews, and selecting appropriate methodologies. By the end of the course, each student will produce a well-structured proposal that demonstrates their ability to conceptualize and plan a scholarly project.

Building upon the skills acquired in coba431, this course focuses on the implementation and completion of the research project. Students will carry out their proposed studies, analyze findings, and present their results in a formal research report. Emphasis is placed on critical thinking, data interpretation, and academic writing, culminating in a final project that showcases their ability to conduct and communicate original research effectively.

The course provides experiential learning opportunities for senior students in economics. In addition, it provides practical and hands-on experience for students wishing to aspire as future economists. Each student is required to prepare a comprehensive and detailed written report on his or her assigned internship experience with the institution he or she was placed.

This course seeks to introduce and orient the student who has no or minimal knowledge and skills in Computer to the general basic concepts that concerns the various devices and tools in Information Technology and the Internet. It will also develop student's computer literacy, keyboarding skills from hands-on experiences, basic skills in file management utilities, word processing, spreadsheets, and graphical presentations in the Windows environment. The student will learn to appreciate how these devices and tools are being used and their impact on the socio-economic life of the community. With these skills he would be able to search for information concerning the various courses he would be taking, submit assignments to faculty online, participate in academic discussions through the Internet, World Wide Web resource, and the like.
TODO...

Overview of the course involves topics such as Logic and conditionals, String manipulation, Loops, recursion, Round-off error and precision, 1-D arrays, plotting with MATLAB, Abstraction and functions, Randomness, Multidimensional arrays, Sorting, searching, root finding, Data abstraction, structures and user-defined types, Data I/O, file processing, Memory allocation, Vectorization and Debugging and profiling.

Problem solving and programming are essential skills for Computer professionals. Understanding how a computer is instructed to accomplish tasks lead to an appreciation of the underlying concepts of the Computer discipline i.e. algorithms, flowcharts, pseudo-codes, etc. Students will learn the importance of algorithms in the problem-solving process, the process of converting an algorithm to flowchart and machine-executable code, the properties of good algorithms, algorithms for solving simple problems. The use of programming languages to illustrate simple programming constructs such as sequential statements, selection statements, repetitions etc. will be emphasised. The objective of the course is to help students to think about a problem in a logical manner and decide how to process each part using standard control structures.

In this course, we will learn the mechanics of editing and compiling programs in C++. We will begin with a discussion of the essential elements of C++ programming: variables, loops, expressions, functions, and string class. Then, we will cover the basics of object-oriented programming: classes, inheritance, templates, exceptions, and file manipulation. We will then review function and class templates and the classes that perform output and input of characters to/from files. This course will also cover namespaces, exception handling, and preprocessor directives. In the last part of the course, we will learn some slightly more sophisticated programming techniques that deal with data structures such as linked lists and binary trees.

Principles of Software Engineering and Reusing and Extending Code, Review of Fundamentals of Procedural Programming, Data Abstraction, Information Hiding and Encapsulation, Constructors, destructors, and object creation, Name space and reference, Class Methods, Methods Overloading, Inheritance, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Polymorphism, Abstract Classes, Abstract Methods, Exceptions, Exception Handling, Templates.

Introduction to the background of operating system*, Operating System Structure, Process Concepts,* The process concept, Program versus Process, Process creation, Process control block, Process table, Shell, Operation on Processes, Kernel/kernel mode, System calls, Types of systems calls, Interrupts, Interrupt processing, Types of Interrupts Memory Management, Virtual Memory, Process Management, Threads and Multitasking, Concurrent Processes, Inter-process communication.

Introduction to computer networks, Network Protocols, Data Communication, Transmission Media and Transmission Technologies, Data Encoding Techniques, Multiplexing FDM, TDM, DWDM, Network structure: Routing in switched networks, internet Protocols architecture, Data Link Control Protocols, Network Security, Confidentiality with Conventional Encryption, Message Authentication and Hash Functions and Public-Key Encryption and Digital Signatures

This course focuses on the study of data and its storage with an emphasis on relational database technology and exposure to NoSQL. Students will learn the entire process of database development from data gathering to querying a fully normalized relational database. Basic database concepts, Database architecture, DB Design Life Cycle, Schema Architecture, Conceptual, Logical and Physical Database Modelling and Design, Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD), Enhanced ERD, Relational Data Model, Mapping ERD to Relational Model, Functional Dependencies and Normalization, Relational Algebra and Structured Query Language

The course is an advance of phys332. The first part of the course covers digital design, with an emphasis on how digital circuits and systems are implemented. The following topics are studied: Number systems, arithmetic operations, Boolean algebra, logic gates, simplification methods, combinational logic circuits, sequential circuits, synchronous and asynchronous circuits, flip flops, counters, shift registers, memory elements, memory technology, state machines, arithmetic circuits and introduction to programmable logic. The second part of the course covers basic designs and concepts of simple computers. The following topics are studied: History, computer design on different levels, design hierarchy, algorithmic state machines, programming model, instruction format, addressing modes, CPU organization, data path (arithmetic/logic unit), control unit, microprogramming, RISC, CISC, I/O systems, interrupt, busses, memory hierarchy, cache, virtual memory.

This course seeks to introduce and orient the student who has no or minimal knowledge and skills in Computer to the general basic concepts that concerns the various devices and tools in Information Technology and the Internet. It will also develop student's computer literacy, keyboarding skills from hands-on experiences, basic skills in file management utilities, word processing, spreadsheets, and graphical presentations in the Windows environment. The student will learn to appreciate how these devices and tools are being used and their impact on the socio-economic life of the community. With these skills he would be able to search for information concerning the various courses he would be taking, submit assignments to faculty online, participate in academic discussions through the Internet, World Wide Web resource, and the like.

The Computer Engineering course description involving MATLAB covers various aspects of computer engineering practice and research, focusing on computer hardware, robotics, and embedded systems. The course aims to introduce students to the practical applications of MATLAB in the field of computer engineering. Here is a breakdown of the key components covered in the course description.

This course teaches the various theoretical approaches or methodologies in software development. It further provides the opportunity for students to create their own software projects that meet the standards in software engineering. Students will be required to choose their desired design, programming language, methods, tools and other techniques in software development. The finish software products are evaluated by the assigned panelist.

This course is an introduction to computer programming that aims to equip students with fundamental programming and to provide them with the basic knowledge and skills in Java programming including its concepts. Topics include data types, basis Java commands, control structures, arrays, object-oriented design, abstraction, encapsulation, algorithms, documentation, testing, and debugging.

The course is designed to teach the fundamentals of engineering operating systems such as virtual memory, kernel and user mode, system calls, threads, context switches, interrupts, inter-process communication, coordination of concurrent activities and the interface between software and hardware. The course further advances to the various concepts of advance operating system to include but not limited to architectural driven and application driven operating systems.

This course teaches object-oriented programming to those who have learnt basic programming concepts and are ready to learn in-depth programming. It focuses on object-oriented programming using Java. The course is design to provide students with in depth knowledge of the different Object-Oriented programming concepts such as classes, objects, inheritance, encapsulation, polymorphism, arrays, strings, overloading, overriding, Functions / Methods / Actions, interfaces and data structure with File I/O / Recursion. Student will study how these concepts can be implemented in Java. Additional topics such as exception handling, building GUIs and Applets will also be taught.

This course lays the foundation for structuring the thoughts, approaches to programming a computer. It also introduces the tools, methodology employ in computing. The course will also introduce a specific programming language as a spring board for students to use for understanding the structure of other programming languages. The focus of the course is to provide orientation for the students to regenerate their creativity in computer programming. This course will give the students a basic knowledge and skills in databases such as database system foundations, relational algebra and data model, schema.

Systems analysis and design is a crucial discipline in the field of information technology that focuses on creating efficient and effective information systems to meet organizational needs. This course provides students with the knowledge and skills required to analyze, design, implement, and evaluate information systems. Topics include determining business requirements, documenting organizational processes, analyzing information flows, and reengineering/designing information systems.

This course seeks to lay bare the components of a computer, explain their individual functions, show the interconnectivity in creating a system. The course also takes into account the behavior of a computer from the stand point an engineer and as seen by the user. The course also illustrates how the computer gets power procedurally leading to a fully functional system. It encompasses the Organization and Architecture with emphases on the study of the internal working of a computer.

This provides the student with a comprehensive introduction to the design of databases and the use of database management systems for applications. It covers the relational model, relational algebra, and SQL, the standard language for creating, querying, and modifying relational and object-relational databases. Students will learn about XML data, including the XML languages XPath, XQuery, and XSLT. The UML approach to database design will be covered, as well as rational design principles based on functional dependencies and normal forms. Other issues important to database designers and users, such as indexes, views, transactions, authorization, integrity constraints, and triggers will be covered.

This course provides the interaction between law, policy, and technology as they relate to the evolving controversies over control of the Internet. In addition, there will be an in -- depth treatment of privacy and the notion of transparency, regulations and technologies that govern the use of information, as well as access to information. The course will focus mainly in legal and regulation over the Internet, law and electronic surveillance, profiling, data mining, policy aware web, and different technologies for anonymity and transparency.

This course will provide the students a fundamental in web programming and development that includes Internet and World Wide Web Concepts, development of websites and webpages, dynamic and interactive web-based capabilities development, client-side technologies including the privacy and security concerns in the Internet. In addition, it will also include a basic server-side technologies concept but will mainly focus on web programming with the use of modern tools and languages in web development.

This course will teach the students about different techniques for the design and analysis of efficient algorithms, emphasizing methods useful in practice. It includes sorting techniques such as search trees, heaps, and hashing; divide -- and -- conquer; dynamic programming, greedy algorithms; amortized analysis; graph algorithms; and shortest paths. In addition, it also includes advanced topics such as network flow, computational geometry, number -- theoretic algorithms, polynomial and matrix calculations, caching, and parallel computing.

which students will be placed in engineering industries for exposure to engineering workplace and field experiences. Students will be introduced to managerial and standard operation procedures, engineering standards and codes of operations. Interns will be expected to work along with professional Engineers on sites and understand hands-on procedures and other significant details in the day-to-day responsibility of engineers. Interns will be expected to prepare and present reports on their experiences from the industry.

The course “Introduction to Artificial Intelligence” provides students with a foundational understanding of the principles, techniques, and applications of artificial intelligence (AI). Students will explore the history of AI, its key concepts, and various approaches used in AI systems. The course covers topics such as machine learning, neural networks, natural language processing, computer vision, and robotics. Through lectures, hands-on projects, and case studies, students will gain practical experience in implementing AI algorithms and developing AI applications. Additionally, ethical considerations and societal impacts of AI will be discussed to help students understand the broader implications of AI technology.

This course introduces students to the concepts, issues, design, implementation, standard applications of multimedia and current technologies of multimedia systems. The course explains the technologies underlying digital images, videos and audio content. This includes various compression techniques, standards, and the issues to deliver multimedia content over the Internet. Issues such as the effective representation, processing, and retrieval of multimedia data must be addressed.

The course will recap on the knowledge from introduction to databases, and expand on the building of database by introduction new futures. Such futures must include the techniques in recording information, storing it, making it very difficult if not impossible to make unauthorized changes, or cheat let alone hack in said record system. The course must finally deal with the technology cryptography.

The “Introduction to Python Programming” course is designed to provide students with a foundational understanding of programming using the Python language. The course covers core programming concepts such as data structures, conditionals, loops, variables, and functions. Students will learn how to write fully-functional Python programs by utilizing commonly used data structures, creating custom functions, and performing file input/output operations. The course includes hands-on coding exercises to reinforce learning and practical application of the concepts taught.

This course provides the students an overview of cloud computing and its concepts. It also provides broad knowledge or virtualization, distributed computing, utility computing, networking, cloud computing latest technologies and the security issues involve in using this technology, the course includes the different cloud computing security techniques that are used today in mitigating and avoiding the risks associated with these technologies.

The course will cover topics such as network theorems to Alternating Current (AC) Networks: network topology. Graph or network. Trees: node voltage and current equations. Two port networks: interconnection of networks, application of interconnection rules, loaded two ports, reciprocity and symmetry. Multiport networks: network equations. n-terminal networks. Two port devices: the gyrator. Laboratory work I: circuit practice, electromagnetism and the magnetic circuit, circuit laws and bridge circuits. Thermionic Emission, Semiconductor Device, Junction Transistor Fabrication

This course will provides the students an advanced topic in databases such as the Entity Relational (E-R) model, the three database models, representation and evaluation of relationship, relational database model, functional dependencies, multi-valued and joint dependency, normalization theory, concurrency control in relational databases, object-oriented data models, database language Structured Query Language (SQL), constraints and triggers in Structured Query Language (SQL), system aspects of Structured Query Language (SQL), Object-oriented query languages and Extensible Markup Language (XML) databases.

This course covers the fundamentals of computer network programming, client-server programming, concepts of computer network programming including the RPC procedure call, Common Object Request Broker Architecture (CORBA), multicasts, and broadcasts, overview of computer network theory and practice from a systems perspective: network infrastructure, local area network (LAN) protocols, wide area network (WAN) protocols, switching technologies, Internet Protocol (IP), Transmission Control Protocol (TCP), network security, and network configuration, design, and performance, Element of information security, security issues and control, and cryptography methods of information security.

Introduction to embedded systems hardware needs. Interrupts basics Interrupt Service Routines (ISR). Survey of software architectures. Inter task communication. Message queue, mailboxes and pipes. Timer functions. Events interrupt routines in a Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) environment. Embedded system software design using a Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) hard real-time and soft real-time system principles, Task division, need of interrupt routines, shared data. Embedded software development tools. Debugging techniques.

This course will provides a knowledge and skills to students about hardware and software required to be able to communicate over a network, mathematical skills such as number systems, application and meaning of bandwidth, comparison of OSI model and TCP/IP model, properties and standards associated with copper and optical media used in networks, installation of simple wireless LAN, different topologies and physical issues associated with cabling common LANs. In addition, it will also cover the fundamentals of Ethernet media access, collision detection, concepts of switching, comparison of collision and broadcast domains, IP addressing, packet switching, and routing protocols.

The course will cover WAN configurations and technologies, internal and external component of a router, proper connections of router fast Ethernet, serial WAN, and console ports, router configurations, and management of system image and device configuration files. In addition, it also includes topics such as static and default routes, characteristics of routing protocols, distance vector routing protocols, ICMP operations, and IOS software.

The course will give an overview and concepts about logical systems design; arithmetic units; conventional and micro-programmed control; stores; input/output devices and programming, memory organization. pipeline and vector processing, multiprocessors; bus structures; cases studies in computer architecture ,combinational circuit design using Medium Scale Integration/Large Scale Integration (MSI/LSI) and programmable logic modules, iterative and tree networks, sequential circuit design and implementation, algorithmic state machine design, asynchronous and pulse mode circuit design, hardware description language and synthesis, Micro- program control design, testing of digital systems and introduction to hardware-software co-design.

This course introduces students to the analysis and evaluation of new solutions for gaps found in existing knowledge and engineering solutions. It also promotes the need to realize ideals through physical designs, simulations and real engineering application, considering internal standards, and others engineering codes and regulations. This course helps students fulfil the requirement of csen505.

This course covers state-of-the-art topics in mobile computing. The course introduces students to the fundamentals of mobile computing, how mobile computing works, what mobile computing involves, and the different applications that mobile computing offers. The course also involves mobile communication, mobile hardware, and mobile software. Communication issues such as ad hoc networks and infrastructure networks as well as communication properties and protocols will be addressed. The course emphasis on the major technologies that support mobile computing, and the basic understanding of the role mobile computing plays in our day to day activities.

The course will introduce topics such as compilers and translators, lexical and syntactic analysis: top-down and bottom up parsing techniques, internal form of source programs, semantic analysis, symbol tables, error detection and recovery, code generation and optimization, type checking and static analysis, Algorithms and implementation techniques for type-checking code generation and optimization. In addition, students will design and implement translators, static analysis, type-checking and optimization.

This course will allow and gives time to student to develop their system design project. System and documentation are included in this course and it is recommended to have a grouping on the projects. Each group needs to present their finished system design project including its documentation and be ready and prepared for the defense before a group of panelists. In addition, each group is required to choose their adviser.

course_title: Introduction to Early Childhood Education and Development
description: This course is an introductory level course designed to introduce teacher-aspirants to the philosophical foundations of early learning; the Liberian early childhood development and education context, including relevant Ministry of Education Liberia frameworks, policies, and recommended practices; inclusive early childhood learning environments; and play-based approaches to learning.

course_title: Content-Specific Methods for Early Childhood Math
description: This course focuses on the content and process of mathematics instruction in the early childhood grades including mathematics teaching principles, mathematical tools, and assessment of student progress within the content learning trajectories of Quantity and Accelerated Mathematics Plan (amp); Number Recognition; Counting; Comparing, Ordering and Estimating; Adding and Subtracting; Composition, Decomposition, Place Value and Multi-Digit Arithmetic; Spatial Thinking; Shapes; and Measurement.

This course emphasizes on inclusion, diversity, and representational literature for the Liberian student population served. The course is designed to enrich students' knowledge so that they can be able to respond to literature topics personally. That is, they will be very judgmental in analyzing any piece of literary work. That includes prose, poetry, and drama. The course is delivered using a combination of instructional approaches including lecture, assigned readings, class discussion, small-group inquiry, cooperative learning, peer teaching, and analytical and reflective writing.

This course presents current theory and research on speech development, language acquisition, and language learning that should guide speaking and listening activities in the early childhood classroom, with an emphasis on the five components of language. Issues of equity related to multilingual students and students with disabilities are also addressed.

course_title: Practicum in Early Childhood Education I
description: This course is a practical experience in an early childhood educational setting that includes working directly with young children; emphasis is on Liberia's national teacher standards, early learning standards, and programmatic planning for ages 3 to 6.

course_title: Natural and Life Sciences in Early Childhood Education
description: This course covers methods and materials for effectively teaching natural and life sciences in the early childhood grades including evidence-based practices for teaching basic science concepts and the importance of inquiry-based approaches in the teaching of nature and living systems. This includes hands-on exploration and application of Liberia National Early Childhood Development (ECD) natural and life sciences curriculum.

course_title: Practicum in Early Childhood Education II
description: This course is a practical experience in an early childhood educational setting that includes working directly with young children; emphasis is on Liberia's national teacher standards, early learning standards, and programmatic planning for ages 3 to 6.

course_title: Social Sciences in Early Childhood Education
description: This course introduces the students to the basic knowledge of the Social Sciences which includes sociology, anthropology, political science, history, economics, demography, and geography. It enhances students' awareness of the current social, political, and economic affairs of their society and aims to expose students to the basic concepts of scientific methods. The Liberian early childhood curriculum on social sciences will be explored.

course_title: Advanced Practices and Processes for Teaching Early Childhood Math
description: This course covers pedagogical issues and perspectives of teaching culturally relevant and inclusive mathematics in the early childhood grades through research-based practices and the processes of Problem Solving, Communication, Connections, Reasoning and Proof, and Representation.

This practicum offers hands-on experiences in applying literacy instruction strategies in real-time early childhood school settings. Teacher-aspirants will design, implement, and evaluate literacy-focused lessons, emphasizing reading, writing, and communication skills. Guided by mentorship and reflective practices, students will address diverse children's needs and explore evidence-based approaches to literacy development. The Liberian early childhood curriculum aspects focusing on literacy will be examined carefully.

This advanced seminar explores current research, trends, and issues in early childhood education. Teacher-aspirants will engage in in-depth discussions, critical analysis, and collaborative problem-solving related to topics such as ethical behavior, creative teaching, curriculum design, portfolio development, family engagement, the Liberian Teacher code of conduct and other policy implications. The course emphasizes reflective practice and the application of theory to real-world challenges. This seminar fosters leadership, innovation, and a deeper understanding of best practices in the field of early childhood education.

This course is designed to expose students to experiential-inquiry-concept-based teaching and learning that reflects current approaches to learning in early childhood school settings. It will explore various methods, skills, and strategies such as Montessori, High Scope and other evidence--based approaches used in teaching and learning in early years. The course emphasizes an integrated approach to the Liberian early childhood curriculum as it relates to contents and pedagogy. Furthermore, students will examine the Liberia early childhood thematic curriculum and be able to access locally available materials to enhance teaching in the classroom. Fieldwork required.

course_title: Internship - Teaching in Early Childhood School Settings
description: This course provides a supervised, hands-on teaching experience in early childhood school settings. Teacher-aspirants will apply theoretical knowledge to practice by planning, delivering, assessing developmentally appropriate lessons for young children in preschool and holistically implementing the Liberian early childhood curriculum. Emphasis is placed on creating impactful contents, classroom management, fostering inclusive learning environments, building professional relationships with children and their families, as well as colleagues and communities. Regular reflection, mentorship, and feedback will support the development of teaching skills and professional growth.

Econ 102, principle of Economics, is an introductory course in Microeconomic. It studies the individual unit of the economy, such as the theory of the consumer and that of the firm. It also treats in its subject matter the determination of price in a free market economy, the principle of supply and demand analysis. It further discusses concepts in economics such as scarcity, choice, opportunity cost and the basic problem of resource allocation are reviewed along with the concepts of demand, supply, and equilibrium price. The study of the firm, market structure and types of business organization are introduced.

This course introduces the students to the study of national economy. Major topics include the nature and scope of macroeconomics, public vs. private sectors, the circular flow of income and expenditure, national income accounting and the uses of national income and output statistics. The concepts of unemployment, inflation, taxation, macroeconomic policy, money, banking, international trade and public finance are explored.

course_title: Quantitative Techniques for Business and Economics
description: This course introduces the students to the use and appreciation of mathematical techniques for business and economic analysis. Topics include overview of the nature and scope of mathematics in business and economics analysis, mathematical symbols, limits, continuity, techniques and functions of several variables; derivative and the derivation of marginal concepts, growth rates and elasticity. The course also treats maximization, minimization and constrained optimization problems. Simple techniques of integration and its application to business and economics are discussed alongside matrix algebra.

course_title: Quantitative Techniques for Business and Economics
description: The principal aim of this course is to introduce students to how mathematics can be used to sharpen and clarify business and economic analysis. By the end of the course, successful students will be comfortable with the basic mathematical methods, which are indispensable for a proper understanding of business and economics, and will have some facility at tackling economic problems using a mathematical frame work. Students with knowledge in simple calculus will find the course manageable, interesting and challenging, as well as helpful in understanding other Economics, Business and Finance courses. Topics include concept of limit, differentiation by first principle and rules of differentiation, partial and implicit differentiation, higher order differentiation. Optimizing mathematical functions, increasing and decreasing functions and prove of concavity and convexity. It also introduces Multivariable functions and applications. Economic Application of Graphs and Equations, use of Derivative in Economics, Calculus of multivariable functions in Economics, Determinants and Matrices and their use in Economics and Comparative Static and concave programming.

The course focuses on price and allocation theory with emphasis on the techniques and methods of analysis. Principles of optimization, the theory of consumer behavior, and the firm and factor market are analyzed.

In continuation of econ301, the course starts with production and cost theory and continues with the analysis of market structure and price in terms of equilibrium of the business firm and consumer demand in markets of varying degrees of competition. Programming and activity analysis, welfare economics and general equilibrium analysis are also explored.

This course deals with the concepts of economic model, national income and basic model of income determination with extension from simple closed economy to four-sector economy model. The theories of consumption such as absolute income hypothesis, relative income hypothesis, permanent income hypothesis and the life cycle income hypothesis; theories of investment and the concepts of multiplier and accelerator are studied alongside the labor market, money and prices.

The course explores deeper understanding of the money and product markets using the IS-LM framework. It also discusses fiscal and monetary policy frameworks and extends to the Keynesian vs. monetarist views on unemployment and inflation. The foreign sector and the international monetary system are also highlighted.

The course deepens the students understanding and application of mathematical techniques in economic analysis. Topics include comparative statics and dynamics, utility functions, price discrimination, and indifference curves application. It continues with constrained optimization, utility maximizations and minimization, differential, integral and optimization techniques as well as matrices with their respective applications in economics.

This course introduces the students to the importance and process of academic research. Major topics to be covered include the nature and importance of research, application of the principles of science to social phenomena, the problems of research in West Africa, and the research process as well as essential parts of a research paper.

The course is an empirical socio-economic analysis of the features and problems of the Liberian economy, covering such aspects as population, national account, internal trade, external trade and payments, public finance, banking, prices, agriculture, mining, industry, transport and communication. A study of the evolution, formulation and implementation of development plans of Liberia, regional cooperation, a general comparison of the features and problems of Liberia with those of other West African countries are explored.

course_title: Statistics for Business and Economics I
description: This course introduces students to statistical techniques employed in business and economic research. Topics include the definition, types and uses of statistics. The distinctions between descriptive and analytical statistics are highlighted. The process of collecting statistical data for business and economic research and analysis; census, population and sample, pilot surveys, questionnaires, and secondary sources of data. It continues with frequency distribution and diagrammatic representation of statistical data, measures of central tendency and measures of dispersion, regression and correlation and index numbers.

course_title: Statistics for Business and Economics II
description: The course is a continuation of econ313. It covers the following topics: The use of probability theory in business and economics research, construction of statistical models for business and economic research, especially the use of statistical tools and techniques such as sample designs and sampling distributions, point and interval estimation, confidence interval, hypothesis testing, chi-square tests of independence, analysis of variance, and the use of extrapolating methods such as moving average and exponential smoothing (time series).

This course focuses on government revenues and expenditure. Topics include principles of taxation, incidence of taxation, budget and government accounting, government debt management, and government revenue and expenditure of developing economies.

This course applies microeconomic analysis to specific business decisions. Emphasis is on managerial decision-making upon the basis of comparisons of worth of alternative courses of action with respect to their cost; tools for decision-making; elements of financial accounting; capital budgeting; economics of production; inventory management; application of classical optimization techniques of profit maximization; linear programming and project evaluation, exchange; social and private costs and benefits; theory of distribution of wages, rent, profits and interest; matrix applications in the IS-LM framework, welfare function and Pareto optimum.

The course explores economic growth theories such as Harrod-Domar, Solow and the neo-classical growth models etc. It covers problems and policies of domestic development, problems and policies of underdeveloped and developed countries as well as third world emerging economies. Studies are varying: Methods for growth, population, pollution, control problems, United Nations agencies of assistance and foreign aid. This section looks at principles and concepts of development with interest in theories of development; diverse economic structures and common characteristics of developing nations; economic growth and development models.

An introduction to the analytical and quantitative methods used in economics. Alternative forecasting methodologies for economic time series are analyzed and discussed. The ordinary least-square techniques and the assumptions underlying it are developed. Methods designed to detect and correct for the violation of these assumptions are examined the foci of the course are: (1) the development of time-series (ARIMA) models and their application to forecasting, (2) the use of standard econometric models for forecasting, and (3) evaluation and comparison of these methods and the conditions under which each is the appropriate methodology. Special emphasis is given to the practical application of the procedures discussed through the use of computer exercises.

An introduction to the analytical and quantitative methods used in economics. Alternative forecasting methodologies for economic time series are analyzed and discussed. The ordinary least-square techniques and the assumptions underlying it are developed. Methods designed to detect and correct for the violation of these assumptions are examined. The foci of the course are: (1) the development of time-series (ARIMA = autoregressive integrated moving average) models and their application to forecasting, (2) the use of standard econometric models for forecasting, and (3) evaluation and comparison of these methods and the conditions under which each is the appropriate methodology. Special emphasis is given to the practical application of the procedures discussed through the use of computer exercises.

The course defines monetary policy, its objectives and techniques at the disposal of government for its implementation. Theories of demand for money by classical and Keynesian schools, Baumol's inventory theoretical approaches to money, Friedman reformation or restatement of quantity theory of money (QTM), the wealth theory, theories of the rate of interest, and role of money in the Macroeconomic model are extensively discussed. Conflicts, trade-offs and monetary coordination, the IS-LM framework and the effectiveness of monetary policy in economic stabilization; international adjustment and liquidity, and monetary policy in Liberia are highlighted.

The course highlights what industrial economics is all about. The nature of the firm, ownership and control problem, theories of the firm- marginalist, behavioral and managerial; perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly, monopolistic competition, company finance; investment theory, research and development; advertising; performance. The course explores the dimensions and determinant of market structure; concentration, merger and acquisition; integration, economies of scale; condition of entry; market power warfare, pricing and business practices. Others are public policy and regulation, industrial development structure and policy in Liberia -a sample of industry study.

This course is a linguistic-based course which emphasizes creative reading, correct, clear and eﬀective writing and speaking skills. The course further assists student-teachers in phonetic instruction, phonetic awareness (phonemes, graphemes and morphemes) and phonics in a chronological order.

The course is divided into two: Part One covers the fundamentals of literature and literary genres. The focus is on defining literary terms, types/genres, the use of language and the devices of language, plot, setting, narrative techniques, character and characterization. Part Two examines each of the genres, poetry, prose and drama. In general, the course studies at least one classic from Shakespeare. The course also focuses on each genre of Literature by examining relevant African and non-African works of art.

This course will delve into the study of how speech sounds are produced by the human vocal organs, with a particular focus on the concept of "stress," which refers to the emphasis placed on specific syllables within a word or utterance, allowing students to learn how to identify and produce different stress patterns in various languages, often utilizing the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) for transcription and analysis. Key aspects covered in the course include: basic phonetics, phonology, phonemes, IPA transcription, stress, types of stress, stress patterns, and stress in connected speech, stress in different languages, and practical applications to improve intonation and pronunciation.

course_title: Introduction to Creative Writing and Short Story (Prose)
description: This course offers students interested in creative writing the opportunity to develop their interest and acquire more knowledge and skill in the art of literary writing. The course will be taught through the creative discussion of the elements of literary writing, especially in terms of their roles and uses in the artistic ordering of thought and experience, and in terms of their relevance to the African experience.

This course is a critical study of the contributions made by African writers and writers of African descent. It is designed to develop a sensitive and comprehensive response to African Literature and to train alert and critical readers. Emphasis is placed on the tale, folklore, short stories, the poetry, the novel and drama as they contributed to the development of society. Special eﬀort is made to introduce works of Liberian authors. It is hoped that the students will be encouraged to try their own hands at creative writing. A large selection of African novels will be required for background reading. Selections by authors of Black Africa or African novels will be required for background reading. Selections by authors of Black Africa or African South of the Sahara will be read.

This course examines how language is used in real-world contexts by analyzing spoken and written communication beyond the sentence level to understand how meaning is constructed through social, cultural, and situational factors. Students engage different theoretical approaches and methods for analyzing discourse data and interpreting significance within specific contexts, including practical work with authentic language samples. Core topics include language in use, major discourse-analysis frameworks, analysis of spoken and written discourse, data collection and analysis, discourse features such as coherence, cohesion, speech acts, conversational structure and genre, transcription techniques, power and social context, and ethical considerations in discourse analysis.

course_title: Literary Genres, Classification, Forms, Conventions and Techniques
description: This is an intensive and extensive course focusing on the genres of literature, basically their forms, conventions and techniques. It studies a variety of English and African poems to introduce students to the major poetic forms, conventions and techniques -- didactic, courtly, heroic, mockheroic, pastoral elegy, lyric, ballad, ode, sonnet, dramatic monologue, narrative poetry, confessional poetry, imagery, symbolism etc. It also attempts a survey of the classiﬁcations and techniques of the novel, considering the conventions, advantages and disadvantages of each form. Types of narration: ﬁrst person, epistolary, stream of consciousness, omniscient narrative, untrustworthy narrator, historic, allegory, hero, anti-hero shall be examined. The drama genre focuses on classical tragedy, modern heroic tragedy, morality play, farce, history play, romantic comedy of manners, social criticism, Marxist drama, theatre of the absurd, poetic drama, inﬂuences of sage type of audience and the use of mythology in drama.

This course on sociolinguistics focuses on history, ideologies, methodology, concepts and application of sociolinguistics. It considers the relationship between language and society with a focus on the attitude toward language varieties and social dialects. For example, the geographic, social and functional varieties of English as well as other language varieties in the Liberian context and how such variations construct and are in turn constructed by identity and culture will be explored. We will therefore consider some of the educational, political, and social repercussions of these sociolinguistics facts. The course also offers an opportunity to focus on key principles of second language learning. Individual differences in second language learning (general learning ability, phonetic coding ability, grammatical sensitivity, inductive learning ability, associative memory), Grammatical competence and communicative competence in second language learners -- the learner's inter-language and its description. *NB:* Students will be required to read extensively on sociolinguistics, do personal research on concept, theories, and sociolinguistic situations in Liberia and other countries, and make group or individual presentations in class
Same course as engl405...

course_title: Elements of English Language (Lexis, Semantics, Syntax, and Pragmatics)
description: The course emphasizes the different types of English grammatical structures and their usage including the advance phonetic learning. It exposes learners to the different types of phrases and their syntactic functions (i.e. the noun phrase, the verb phrase, adverb phrase, the adjectival phrase, the prepositional phrase). Sentence type and their communicative functions, (i.e. declarative, interrogative, imperative and exclamatory sentences with functions such as statements, questions, requests, directives, apologies, wishes, invitations, offers etc.). Simple, compound and complex sentences.

The course emphasizes a detailed study of word-formation process and the internal structure of words in English. Words and morphemes, inﬂectional morphology (including the identiﬁcation of lexical and grammatical categories). Word-formation process in English derivation, compounding invention, echoism, clipping, acronym, branding and back-formation.

Introduction to Poetry is a one-semester course of three credit units. It is designed for English Language students and others in related departments who are required to take language and literature courses as electives. The course has fifteen units which cover almost all the introductory information a student would need to understand in poetry as a genre of literature. This requisite information includes: the nature of poetry as literature, definitions, uses, elements, techniques and devices of poetry and, finally, how to criticize, appreciate, and compose a poem.

course_title: Survey of English and American Literature
description: The course deals with American Literature from its origin in the puritan culture of New English to contemporary American ear. The sequence of topics is as follows; the puritan mind, 18th-century Rationalism and the American Revolution, American Romanticism, the Genteel Tradition; the emergence of modern American Literature following the Civil war, the Authentic American Voice of Western Realism of the 19th Century and Recent Trends in Fiction and poetry.

The course explores the valuable canon of Liberian literature recorded by literary history, both in Liberia and outside. Accordingly, the course studies a chronological record of Liberia's pastoral, folk literature, with its folk songs, proverbs, and folk tales, known since the 1800s. These include earlier African narratives in English expression dating back to the 1800s to contemporary Liberian literary works.

course_title: Methods of Teaching English and Literature in Secondary Schools
description: Explorations will lead to developing a point of view in teaching English and Literature. Focus also on use of MOE content standards in developing curriculum and for instruction, strategies to promote the integration of teaching literature and syntactic rules of standardized English and reading and writing for meaning, the use of socio-cultural resources, unit and lesson planning using Backward Design, assessment and evaluation of teaching and learning in the Junior and secondary levels.

This seminar course explores teaching methods in English language and literature through critical reading, research, and discussion. Students analyze classic and contemporary literary works, examine various genres and themes, and engage in stimulating discussions on literary and linguistic issues. Emphasis is placed on developing analytical writing, oral presentation skills, and a deeper appreciation of English and literature.

This course is designed to develop students' appreciation of the literature of the diverse cultures and people in the world as an important part of their overall educational experiences. In particular, the course aims at developing students' critical thinking abilities, their understanding and celebrating of diverse cultures and experiences as a tool for independent assessment of human issues. It also should help in students' examination of their own ethical and intellectual growth as they come to understand the diversity of historical, political and psychological issues through literature. Reading the works of writers in the African diaspora -- in the Caribbean and the Americas -will be a central part of this course.

This course is a senior course for student teachers specializing in English and Literature. It delves into testing practical application of concepts and skills learned in their field of concentration, particularly English and Literature. It involves field work: observing mentors, practical teaching by student teachers, planning, and compiling of teaching experiences.

course_title: 18th Century Literature and Early English Novels
description: The three genres of literary writing -- poetry, drama and fiction-of the period are considered. Particular attention is paid to the works of John Dryden, Jonathan Swift, Alexander Pope, Samuel Johnson, Thomas Wyatt through to Donne (poetry): William Congreve, Oliver Goldsmith and R.S. Sheridan, William Shakespeare (drama): Jonathan Swift, Samuel Johnson (fiction). The aim is to introduce students to the influence of “satire” and “comedy” in the literature of England from 1660 -- 1700. Attempt will be made to relate the artist to his/her socio-cultural setting, especially to the importance of politics and development of cities in creating a new aesthetic awareness. Emphasis is also on detailed analysis and appraisal of the themes and conventions of the early English novel. Representative works will be those of Defoe, Richardson, Fielding, Smidler, Stern. Bronte, Scott and the Gothic novel.

In this course, students will continue learning from engl102 by being introduced to the kind of prose they will deal with in their academic career and the connection between reading and writing academic prose. Reading selections will become opportunities for further in-class dialogue, making thematic inter-textual connections, discourse analysis, reasoning and writing across disciplines. The course emphasizes the various forms of essay writing at the standards required for a university level student, and techniques of writing prose logically and coherently. Attention will be given to appropriate language use and technical guidelines associated with kinds of writing.

The course analyzes selected works by gender theorists and creative writers from Africa, the West, and other traditions that examine psychological, historical, and cultural factors shaping gender identities, with focus on feminism. It studies conventional stereotypes of women in works by male and female writers and the changing image of women in African literature within historical, economic, and cultural contexts. Topics include the idealization of women in chivalric and courtly traditions, portrayals of women as temptress or object of desire, the bourgeois “bad woman,” woman as mother and moral center of the home, earth-mother imagery, misogyny, and early feminist representations. Readings include African folklore and traditional literature, with selected medieval and Renaissance works. The course further addresses gender and sexuality in literature, feminist theories, intersectionality with race, ethnicity, class and age, feminist social movements, and methods for analyzing how gender roles and sexual norms are constructed and deconstructed in literary texts.

The course traces Liberia's history before the founding of the country, explores the administration of governance, and concludes with developments up to 1944. Topics include: the people of Liberia before the 19th Century, state structure in pre-state Liberia, the coming of independence in 1847, the resurrection, the wars of 1857, the imperialist pressures and responses, the interior policy and its development, the True Whig Party the emergency of one-party oligarchy, and the League of Nations' Inquiry. The course concludes with end of Edwin Barclay's rule and ascendancy of William V.S. Tubman

This course is designed to introduce students to Liberia's historical developments following the ascendancy of President Tubman. Accordingly, the rise of Tubman with his “Open Door” and “Closed Door” policies, accompanied by his 27 years of leadership, is studied. The course further highlights the Liberian economy and foreign policy; the rise of the emerging social, economic and political order of the 1970s; events of political upheavals of the 1970s and 1980s followed by the Liberian civil crisis, international interventions and political transitions since the end of the crisis in 2002. The course further studies contemporary issues such as governance and decentralization, the structure and mandate of integrity institutions, as well as current historical events.

The course is designed to examine in depth the colonial period in Africa. The course studies the European colonization of Africa and analyses the colonial domination on Africa, examining the contributory factors for the colonization of Africa and the strategies that were used by the imperial interlopers to achieve their objectives. The course further examines the colonial policies of the British, the French, the German and the Portuguese; the impact of European colonization on Africa and the rise of African resistance and independence movements.

This course explores available materials on the History of West Africa from the Middle Ages up to the 19th Century. Students are required to demonstrate the ability to prepare research documents in the area to arrive at original conclusions based on their individual assessment of the materials. The course covers the History of the Early Sahara, Western Sudan States such as Karnem Bornu Empire, Hausa State, Oyo Kingdom, Benin Kingdom and the Ashanti Kingdom.

The course studies Europe from the Renaissance to the end of the Thirty Years War with the Peace of Westphalia. The course emphasizes intellectual development and social changes, and investigates the reasons for the expansion of the continent, stressing the growth of particular state formation. European expeditions and Europe's trade with Asia and Africa are studied. The course further explores the Age of Renaissance, the Reformation and the role of the Church in the affairs of the state. The course further traces the rise or emergence of modern Europe following the “Peace of Westphalia” of 1648 and concludes with the two world wars fought by European nations.

The course presents an intensive analysis of the history of Africa from across all regions. Beginning with a survey of Africa's archaeological finds, the course explores major historical themes of East Africa, North Africa, Southern Africa and Central Africa. The course concludes with a reflection of major historical themes of contemporary West Africa.

This course explores the relationship between film, music, art and politics in the twentieth century Africa with a special emphasis on Liberia. Artistic productions and consumption is considered in the context of various major political shifts, from the experience of colonialism, the struggle against Apartheid and pre and post-war Liberia, for example. Each week addresses a different theme and image from the arts in an attempt to introduce students to the various dynamics that shape African cultures, societies and governments. Additionally, this course seeks to engage students in a debate about how popular films, art and music not only reflect but also influence how peoples construct historical imaginations and memory. While much of the images of Africa have previously been the product of Hollywood and European films, this course will introduce Nollywood and burgeoning African film industry as an African alternative to how films depict, and people understand, their history. How western perceptions and understanding of African art have shifted will also be discussed throughout the course

This course covers the pre-history of Africa, tracing the emergence of the earliest human species to the organization of the ancient African civilizations. The course explores themes such perspectives, sources of African History, Africa as the cradle of human civilization as well as the emergence of human species which proves Africa as the birthplace of mankind. The hub of the course centers on a critical review of ancient African civilizations of North and East Africa. Focus is placed on linguistic classification, political and religious culture in early African kingdoms of Egypt, the Great Nubia and the Kush, the Aksum of Ethiopia and Carthage.

course_title: Slavery: An African and Inter-continental Perspective
description: The course explores the history of slavery across various continent of the world. Major themes include the slavery in early Middle Ages of Europe, the Byzantine Empire and the Crusader sates, the Indian Ocean and Asia. Key attention will be given the study of the Trans-Saharan Slave Trade, the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade, and slavery in the Americas and the Caribbean.

The course examines sources of African History and explores facts relating to archaeological findings across Africa in places like: Olduvai Gorge and Laetoli (Tanzania), Valley of the Kings (Egypt), Gedi Ruins/Koobi Fora (Kenya), Sterkfontein Caves/Blombos Cave (South Africa), Meroe (Sudan), Nok (Nigeria), Laas Gaal (Somalia), Kola Forest and Salty Sea (Liberia).

The course examines the challenges of African growth at both economic and political levels, considering the impact of colonial legacy, neo-imperialism, neo-imperial economic interest, conditioned foreign aid as crucial factors for underdevelopment of post-independence Africa. Attention will be devoted to other constraints to Africa's growth and development such as Africa's unfavorable endowments, geographical challenges, HIV/AIDS, demographic impact, political uncertainty, conflicts, weak institutional capacity, low savings, mono-cultural economies and lack of industrial and technological capacities.

The course is a survey of the history of the various civilizations of the world, tracing the earliest human civilization to civilizations of the Fertile Crescent. Key world civilizations which form the themes of the course the Indian and Chinese civilizations, the Ancient Greek civilization, the Roman Civilization and the rise of Christianity, the rise of Islam and the Muslim empires, emerging Europe and the Byzantine Empire, Europe in the Middle Ages, the Age of Enlightenment and the Revolutions, the World Wars and the aftermaths, the Cold War and postwar challenges, and the changing global patterns.

The course is a study of the discovery and colonization of America, the role of independence and major events in the United States and the Latin American region. Key themes of the history of the United States include but not limited to the early indigenous arrival and indigenous culture; causes and consequences of the American Revolutionary War; the War of 1812 as well as the Civil War; the first and second “Great Awakenings”; the “Monroe Doctrine”, the “Manifest Destiny”, and the United States in world affairs. The course further highlights events of the history of Latin America such as the colonization of Latin America, decolonization and the struggle for independence of Latin American states, and post-colonial Latin America

The course fundamentally pertains to what has been described as the “arts and science of writing history” or “the history of history”. Its essence is to expose students to issues like concepts, definition, nature, scope and value of history as well as the relationship of history to other social science disciplines.

course_title: Methods of Teaching History in Secondary Schools
description: The course focuses mainly on the pedagogical instruction of history subjects among high school teachers, whereby they will be exposed to rudiments of how best to electively impart the knowledge or lessons of history to their pupils. Understandably, college education departments focus on producing teachers, which explains why they must be equipped with teaching techniques that should help to achieve desired results in class with their pupils. To this end, student-teachers will be exposed to chronological (periodization -- dates and events) and thematic (diachronic and synchronic) approaches of teaching history as well as how to merge both and create a well-structured technique of teaching history-related subjects to eliminate many of the disadvantages of both approaches and utilize the advantage of each.

course_title: Urbanization of Contemporary Africa; Space, Identity and Politics
description: Though the course will focus mainly on the contemporary era, our explorations will consider African cities in historical perspective. It begins with a brief history of urbanism on the continent to lay the groundwork for an examination of colonial legacies and the strategic role that African cities have played in globalization and empire, past and present. This will help us to locate urban Africans in today's most recent era of globalization. Then, considering a wide- range of contexts across the continent (but mainly of sub-Saharan Africa) from a variety of disciplinary perspectives, we will delve into some cross-cutting contemporary themes in the study of African cities related to: infrastructure and planning, economies and livelihoods, internal migration, youth, development and urbanization; and politics and identities---including negotiations around issues of religion and gender. Insights gained will be used to reflect on notions of the city, citizenship, national development and globalization.

course_title: Running While Others Walk; African Perspectives on Development
description: Throughout the history of modern Africa, Africans have specified their desired future development, and identified the major obstacles in achieving it. Debates about development have intensified in the post-colonial period, especially as African countries have replaced the leaders installed at independence. Amidst the general critique of the imposition of external values and rules, Africans have differed, sometimes sharply, on priorities, process, and programs. While for some the challenge is to catch up with development elsewhere, for others it is essential to leap ahead, to set the pace, to initiate a radical social, economic, and political transformation. Students will review, compare, and analyze major contributions of African thinkers and political figures on development trends, patterns and growth of the continent.

The course introduces students to the skills and techniques of writing academic research. It explores the development of a research problem, setting research objectives and questions, conducting literature review, using appropriate research methods and design, collecting, analyzing and interpreting data. Students will be required to write a comprehensive research paper that corresponds to academic research requirements.

The course seeks to provide the students with knowledge, skills and attitudes needed to become successful entrepreneurs; promote positive attitudes among the undergraduate students at the University towards entrepreneurship and business, and foster an enterprising spirit and self-confidence among them. It helps the students develop an entrepreneurial spiritand skills that are necessary for building an entrepreneurial foundation for the nation. A combination of these will serve as a catalyst for sustainable private sector growth in Liberia, play a major role in the fight against the hurdles of youth unemployment and poverty, and also promote personal development. The course is taught on a modular basis from module one through three covering topics like understanding entrepreneurship and the concept of work and business, becoming an entrepreneur, and scanning the environment for business opportunities.

The course continues from eedu301, and seeks to enhance the skills of the students on practical entrepreneurial activities borrowing from the knowledge gained from eedu301. There are two additional modules to be covered with topics such as business research and developing a business plan, as well as starting and operating a successful business.

The course introduces basic circuit analysis techniques and major circuit theorems including Ohm's and Kirchhoff's laws, V I laws for R-L-C passive elements, common signal models like thevenin's and Norton's theorems, circuit transformations (Y-Delta and Delta-Y transformations), the transient and steady-state responses of R-L-C circuits, phasors applications in electric circuits, sinusoidal analysis and complex powers, introduction to polyphase and magnetically coupled circuits, and applications of electric softwares such as PSPICE and Simulink that are used circuit simulations during practical sections are also introduced to the students.

Intro to electric networks, passive elements (R, L, C) in the frequency domain, first order circuits (RL and RC) circuits, Natural responses of RC and RL circuits, step responses of RL and RC circuits, a general solution for step and natural responses, applications of first order circuits, second order circuits (RLC), introduction to the natural response of a parallel circuit, the forms of the natural response of a parallel RLC circuit, the step response of a parallel RLC circuit. The natural response of series RLC circuit, Step response of series RLC circuit, applications of second order circuits. Laplace transformation in circuit analysis, the Fourier transformation in circuit analysis, two ports networks.

The workshop Introduces the first Aid procedures, the general rules and regulations of electrical engineering and the basic electrical tools indicating their functions, organization of the maintenance and troubleshooting table, introducing the various electrical components and materials with their identification methods. Types of maintenances. Later introduce the measurement of basic electrical quantities (resistance, capacitors, coils, current, voltage, and power, testing of semiconductor devices). Building of simple electric circuits, half-wave and full-wave bridge rectifiers, stabilizers, voltage regulators. Conclude the course by building an amplifier using transistors and ICs, series and parallel circuits (RC, RL, and RLC circuits. Lectures on general troubleshooting and maintenance procedures will be conducted. The logbook filling procedure.

This course covers two major topics on DC machines and polyphase circuits. The following topics are introduced under the DC machines chapter: Magnetic circuits and induction, principle of electromagnetic conversion, basic armature reaction and commutation of DC machines, operating characteristics of DC generators, speed control, starting and breaking of DC motors, losses, induction motors (constructional features of three-phase induction motors, principle of operation and torque development, characteristics of three-phase induction motors, single phase induction motors, types of single phase induction motors, synchronous machines, principle of motor operation, testing and maintenance of DC machines. Polyphase circuits, theory of polyphase induction motors.

course_title: Electrical Safety, maintenance and Troubleshooting Procedures
description: This course introduces the hazards of electricity and electrical safety equipment. Safety procedures and methods and the grounding of electrical equipment are later discussed; regulatory and legal safety requirements and standards are explained. The accident prevention, accident investigation, rescue and first aid are systematically presented. Safety management and organizational structure are properly taught. Procedures of maintenance and troubleshooting are outlined in the course.

course_title: Electrical / Electronic Testing Method and reliability
description: This course is intended for students majoring in electrical engineering and related engineering disciplines. It can assist graduate students and practicing engineers who wish to improve their knowledge on reliability and maintainability of engineering systems. The course is to introduce students to reliability engineering so that engineering techniques could be applied to prevent or reduce frequency of failures of the system. Also, ways of identifying and correcting causes of the failures. Students will also be informed about diagnosis of engineering failures, application of statistical analysis, risk analysis and quality control. Furthermore, reliability-centered maintenance (RCM) concept will be explained to students for maximizing the performance and increasing the reliability of systems. Topics to be covered include Introduction to reliability; Elementary reliability theory; Indices of reliability and failure rate models. Failure distribution reliability function; Mean time to failure; Mean time between failures; Reliability of systems; serial configuration, parallel configuration, and redundancy. Determination of reliability of electronic systems; Power systems reliability; Factors affecting power systems reliability. System maintainability; Analysis of down time. Repair-Time distribution; Exponential Repair-Time; Reliability concept in preventive maintenance; Methods of improving systems reliability

This course introduces student to measurements associated with electrical engineering phenomena. It emphasizes the linkage between instruments, physical laws and the calibrations of instruments. This is a two-part lab course. It covers transducer, digital and analogue testing instruments, curve tracers, recorders; also, measurement of voltages, currents, power, temperature, displacements. This course further introduces student to measurements associated with electrical engineering phenomena. It emphasizes the linkage between instruments, physical laws and the calibrations of instruments. This is a two-part lab course. It covers power and digital measurements: magnetic ratio and multiple ratio measurements; data acquisition, conversion and interfacing; data logging switches and displays; EM field measurements.

The course discusses the general overview of power system technologies, tools and services, power generation, transmission, and distribution of electric power.
