One-line diagram of electrical power systems per unit quantities. Electrical transmission systems types (HVDC, HVAC), effects of increase voltage, distribution systems (feeders, distributors, service mains), types of distribution system (radial and ring main system). Comparison of overhead line (OHL) and underground cables (UGC). It also covers the analysis of interconnected systems with load flow studies, fault finding and calculations using symmetrical components, electric circuit protection devices. Analysis of protecting and relaying of power system protection, using computation techniques. Fourier transforms of signal power and energy spectra representations. Sinusoidal sources and their complex representation using phasors. Power factor correction and grounding techniques.

This course will be taken distinctly over a three-month period, during which students will be placed in engineering industries for exposure to engineering workplace and field experiences. Students will be introduced to managerial and standard operation procedures, engineering standards and codes of operations. Interns will be expected to work along with professional Engineers on sites and understand hands-on procedures and other significant details in the day-to-day responsibility of engineers. Interns will be expected to prepare and present reports on their experiences from the industry.

Basic communications theory which starts with the description of simple communication network; types of communication systems, atmospheric layers, the electromagnetic and radio frequency spectrums, transmission modes and media, the dB theory. Basic transmission and reception principles and operations, modulation systems (AM, FM, PuM, Digital), multiple access methods (FDMA, TDMA, CDMA), spread spectrum techniques and telephone signaling systems.

current using an extension of the classical Newtonian model. The course also describes the behavior of moving electrical charges and their interaction with magnetic and electric fields and mechanical phenomena. The course looks at the application s as how to employ the effects of changing electric and magnetic fields along with the forces and motions these fields induce on objects with electric charges.

The steady magnetic field theory that includes some laws and theorems of the magnetic field (Biot-Savart law, Ampere circuital law, The curl and Stoke theorems, magnetic flux and magnetic flux density, the scalar and vector magnetic potentials, steady magnetic field laws), magnetic forces, materials and inductance, time-varying fields, Maxwell's equations, transmission lines, the uniform plane wave, plane wave reflection and dispersion, guided waves, optical fibers, electromagnetic radiation of electric energy and antennas.

Introduction to AC machines, principle of AC generation, transformers, synchronous generators and alternators, rotating magnetic fields, synchronous motors, construction and theory of polyphase induction motors, commutator machines, synchronous convertors and rectifiers. Industrial procedures and operating principles of basic electromechanical machines. Fractional horsepower, Electric machines specifications and ratings. Methods of troubleshooting and overhauling of electric machines with AC motors laboratory exercises emphasizing measurement techniques.

Introduction to various technical facilities in the workshop including mechanical and electrical equipment. Concepts in electrical safety, safety regulations, earthing concepts, electric shocks and treatment. Use of tools used by electricians, wiring regulations, types of cables and electric accessories including switches, plugs, circuit breakers, fuses etc., symbols for electrical wiring schematics e.g. switches, lamps, sockets etc., drawing and practice in simple house wring and testing methods, wiring schemes of two-way and three-way circuits and ringing circuits, voltage and current measurements. Electric soldering and soldering tools; soldering methods and skills, PCB designing, transferring a circuit to PCB, etching, drilling and soldering component on PCB Testing.

Introduction to control systems, aims and classification of control systems. Discrete data and sequential control systems. System models covering mechanical, electrical, fluid, and thermal systems. Transient and steady state responses. Force-voltage and force-current analogies. Dynamic responses with the introduction of the transfer function, representation of block diagrams, and the introduction of signal flow graphs. Poles and zeros, time and frequency domain responses. Nyquist and Bode plots, Stability and the performance criteria. Root locus method of analysis of control systems. Basic control actions and controller characteristics.

The course introduces the basic concepts required to understand optical communication systems and networks. Theory, applications, and design principles are emphasized. The principles of wavelength division multiplexed (WDM) systems, RF photonic systems and passive optical networks (PONs) are presented. The characteristics and limitations of system components (laser diodes, external modulators, optical fiber, optical amplifiers, optical receivers) and factors affecting the performance of both analogue and digital transmission systems are studied.

Introduces various definitions that in use in analog electronics, thermionic valves (vacuum diodes, triodes, tetrodes, pentodes, basic semiconductor theory which includes modeling the atom, conductor and insulator properties, intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors, and the P-N junction diode, transistor circuits, basic integrated circuits theory, linear amplifiers and oscillators, feedback and frequency response, comparing the transistor and the triode amplifier circuits, operational and power amplifiers circuits. Filters and tuned amplifiers, multistage amplifiers used in electronics circuits. Signal generators, electronic troubleshooting and maintenance of analog equipment.

The course introduces the concepts of signals and systems abstraction with applications on mass, spring, tank and cell phone systems where the hierarchical representations of signal components' systems are analyzed. Discrete-time (DT) systems and their systems representations, advantages and disadvantages of DT systems, the difference equation and the block diagram. Recipe and constraints of block diagrams. Feedback, poles, and fundamental modes (feedback, cyclic signal paths and modes, geometric growth and the second order systems, partial fractions, poles and complex poles). Continuous time (CT) systems with their differential representation of various signals and systems. Evaluation of operators' expressions, elementary building blocks of DT and CT systems, impulse response of acyclic CT systems, convergent and divergent poles. The Z-transformations and the inverse Z-transformations with their applications to signals and systems. Digital signal generation, a stablemulti-vibrator's signal generator, crystal oscillators and phase-locked loops (PLL) systems.

The course includes study of nature of power system, overview of technologies and services, high voltage DC transmission, medium voltage DC transmission with siplink, flexible AC transmission systems, power transmission lines, transformer, voltage regulation and stability, solar power solutions, transmission lines faults, grid access solution for decentralized power generation: power flow in power systems: and protection and maintenance and hybrid system.

Principles of Hydroelectric energy (Hydropower), components of a hydroelectric power station, layout of hydropower station, layout of thermal power station, layout of a nuclear power station, method of load study and load balancing of community areas, Freehand sketching and electrical diagram of electrification of a given community. Domestic and industrial electrical installations, power generation and hybrid electric systems. Safety rules, standards, and regulations when working under power systems.

Introduction to the architecture of a microcontroller, Explanation of how a microprocessor works and of its components (ALU, registers, stack, command decoder, program counter, Identification of hardware components on the experiment card, Introduction to the design of an Intel 8085,Introduction to the memory system for a microprocessor, memory mapping , memory address decoding. Introduction to the various microcomputer buses, Read-out of data on address, control and data buses, Introduction to the instruction set of the CPU, Writing programs for simple computing operations, introduction to programmable integrated circuit, application of programming in electrical/electronic circuits. Writing your own assembler, micro C and micro basic programs.

course_title: Electrical Senior Design Project I (Conceptual design)
description: This course begins with the selection of an approved topic; later develop a framework for carrying out the project that will be beneficial to the University, to Liberia, and the World at large. At least a 10-page of document summarize the project. This conceptual aspect of the project. Selected Project Work under the supervision of a Professor

Using teams, students design an electric project that primarily involves the communication, control and power systems. Design teams are advised by a faculty or engineering practitioner. Lectures cover supplemental technical background specific to the project as well as cross-disciplinary aspects of the project management development, and constructability. Integrated project design with further development of student communication skills and the prototype is built and presented at the end of the course.

Frequency domain response, stability and the frequency domain, control system design (compensators and controllers, modes of control, velocity feedback), discrete time systems (systems, digital signal processing, approximation of differentiation and integration, sampling theorem, zero-order hold), the Z-transform (time delay, sampling and the Z-transform, properties of the Z-transform, the inverse Z-transform, pulse transfer function, relationships between the S-plane and the Z-plane, tests for stability in the Z-domain, steady-state error and frequency response of discrete time systems), Computer control systems [computer control, digital implementation of continuous time controllers, digital controller design, conversion of discrete controller transfer functions into programs, choice of sampling interval, the microprocessor in the control loop, programmable logic controllers (PLC)], state system models (matrix notation and terminology, state space model, relationships between state equations and transfer functions, solutions to state equations, and state diagrams).

This course covers definition of high voltage, need for generation of high voltages; advantages of high voltage engineering; applications of high voltage engineering; high voltage insulation. It also deals with high voltage testing and measurement; specimens and electrodes; measurement of vbr at 50 hz, high voltage; test equipment for oil and other insulation; surge insulation testing, destructive testing, nondestructive testing; high voltage technique in bio-medical engineering; and over voltage transients, lighting protection of high voltage equipment; lighting effect on buildings; insulation coordination; introduction to insulation co-ordination for protection; basic definitions of terms; factors on which basic insulation levels depend; impulse characteristics of electric equipment; harmonics and unbalanced loading in transformer.

This course covers characteristics of power electronics devices, converters, power supplies, cyclo converters, DC drives, electric motors, microcomputer control, protective projects and computer simulations: and relaying, projects and computer simulations.

Shift-keying techniques (FSK, BPSK, QPSK, and DSPK), Quadrature amplitude modulation, Multiple access systems (FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, SSMA), radio and television engineering, intro to network topology and types of networks, the GSM network, The WWW and its protocols. Satellite communications, fiber optics communications, microwave radio, radar, sonar systems, laser and maser technologies. Linear systems and filters. General safety rules and regulations for communications systems.

This is the first of a two-part course which introduces students to practical specialized interest-based engineering topics and skills developments in exploring knowledge and solutions for gaps in the currently available scientific and engineering approach. It enhances students' ability to undertake Senior Project EENG 505.

Student will prepare a paper on a selected topic and present it to the panel of faculties under the supervision of both the respective chairs of the Departments and the Dean/CET. Required research methodologies must observed during the entire writing process. The seminar final paper must contain 40-50 pages and include all the required drawings of the seminar topic. The writing standard should observe the international paper writing requirements that will be given by the CET or the chairs of the respective Departments. Introduction to field theory, review of the series and vectors (operations on vectors, dot and vector products, triple products), Complex differential operators (the gradient, the divergence, the curl, the Laplacian), Spherical and cylindrical coordinates and the relations between them. Wave propagation in the radio frequency spectrum, the experimental law of Coulomb, the vector form of Coulomb's law, the electric field intensity and its applications, the vector field and the magnetic field.

This is the digital electronics course starting with the operations and conversion of binary numbers to their decimal equivalents and vice-versa, also operations with octal and hexadecimal numbers, coding and error detecting codes. Logic gates and their various combinations in electronics circuits. Digital devices and their data sheets. Designing and implementing combinational logic circuits (truth table, Boolean algebra and the simplification of logic expressions, Boolean analysis of logic circuits, the standard SOP and the standard POS forms, mapping a standard and a non-standard SOP expressions, De-Morgan theorems, Karnaugh map and its simplification, determination of a minimum SOP expression from the map, combination logic circuits and devices, Binary coded decimals (BCD), Digital codes (Gray code, excess-3 code, alphanumeric codes, American standard code for information interchange, A/D and D/A converters circuits and their applications in electronics, logic testing devices. Cyclic codes, Programmable logic devices, memory devices and systems. Flip-flops, Counters and shift registers. Principle of digital signal generation, transmission, reception, and interfacing.

This elective language package requires students to complete one of fren101, gleb101, chin101, or span101 where the program uses the 101 language elective group. The selected course satisfies the package requirement and carries the specific language content and progression of the chosen option.

This elective language package requires students to complete one of fren102, gleb102, chin102, or span102 where the program uses the 102 language elective group. The selected course satisfies the package requirement and carries the specific language content and progression of the chosen option.

This elective language package requires students to complete one of fren201, gleb201, chin201, or span201 where the program uses the 201 language elective group. The selected course satisfies the package requirement and carries the specific language content and progression of the chosen option.

This elective language package requires students to complete one of fren202, gleb202, chin202, or span202 where the program uses the 202 language elective group. The selected course satisfies the package requirement and carries the specific language content and progression of the chosen option.
The graduating senior may select any departmental course ie., ceng404, ceng501, ceng502, ceng504 or ceng507 as technical elective.

Freshman English I is a fundamental English course required of students in all degree programs of the university and pre-requisite to all credit-bearing English courses. The course provides instructions in two main parts -- the fundamentals of grammar and basic communication. Part I covers different parts of speech and their problem areas, sentence types and their syntactic functions, subject-verb agreement, and the use of tenses. Part II dwells on elements of basic communication, emphasizing listening, basic writing and speaking abilities as well as focusing on the reading and discussion of short prose selections intended to develop the skills in both oral and written communication. In summary, the course embodies six core components: 1) the eight parts of speech and their problem areas, 2) types of sentences and their syntactic functions, 3) subject-verb agreement, 4) correct use of tenses, 5) use of appropriate vocabulary and 6) effective reading and writing skills.

English 102 is required of students in all programs and prerequisite to all credit-bearing English courses at the University. The course helps to reinforce the skills acquired in English 101 by providing instruction in the writing of original composition; description/narration and in reading composition, enabling students to meet the requirements of college education. The course is built around three main sections. Section I discusses the different types of phrases and clauses. Section II considers the correct usage of these phrases and clauses in developing four types of compositions -- descriptive, narrative, expository and argumentative. The final section focuses on vocabulary development and styles, grammatical accuracy, and fluency in compositions, proofreading techniques.

This course introduces students to various types of dramatic literature, from the ancient Greek dramas to contemporary Eastern and Western Theatre. Its purpose is to familiarize students with not only the history and theoretical aspects of theatre, but also its practical manifestations through playwriting, acting, directing, stage production and theatre management.

Grammar and Composition (engl201) is an English grammar and composition course required of students in all degree programs of the university. The course provides instructions in two main parts -- The English Grammar and Composition. Part I covers elements of the English grammar which build on the subject-verb agreement (concord), passive constructions, question tags, and the use of tenses already offered in Freshman English I. Part II dwells on the essential English composition skills such as mechanics of writing, paragraphing and paragraph development techniques, advanced composition, speech writing and the long essay.

The course is divided into two: Part One covers the fundamentals of literature and literary genres. The focus is on defining literary terms, types/genres, the use of language and the devices of language, plot, setting, narrative techniques, character, and characterization. Part Two examines each of the genres, poetry, prose, and drama. In general, the course studies at least one classic from Shakespeare. The course also focuses on each genre of Literature by examining relevant African and non-African works of art.

This course makes use of lectures and workshops and debates to help students develop written and oral communication skills for preparing and presenting technical and other specialized reports. It covers such areas as basic communication skills, communication graphics, resume/c.v, application letters, research skills, methodologies, research proposals, collaborative work and oral presentations, job interviews, debate and public speaking skills.

course_title: Introduction to Creative Writing and the Short Story
description: This course offers students interested in creative writing the opportunity to develop their interest and acquire more knowledge and skill in the art of literary writing. The course will be taught through the creative discussion of the elements of literary writing, especially in terms of their roles and uses in the artistic ordering of thought and experience, and in terms of their relevance to the African experience.

Introduces students to the principles of phonetic description and taxonomy, illustrated with practical examples and language laboratory exercises from a variety of languages, particularly those likely to be of interest to the teaching of English in Liberia. Emphasis will be on the scope of phonetics and phonology, phoneme theory, classification and description of sounds, phonological processes, among others. The course will also expose students to the suprasegmental features of the English language. Special focus will be on syllable structure, morphophonemics, stress (both at the word and sentence level), intonation, among others

This is a course in the principles and practice of discourse analysis. Emphasis is placed on practical analysis and the study and description of relevant textual materials. Specific attention will be given to the analysis of language and literacy events in classrooms as teachers and students engage in face-to-face encounters with each other and construct meaning and take social action using language.

This course concentrates on the analysis and study of poetry and its forms. Readings include work from diverse cultural contexts, including, for example, poems by women, African Americans, other minorities, and non-Western writers. The course emphasizes discussion and student presentation of poetry analysis.

The course emphasizes the participation of students in theatre production as directors, actors, designers, technical directors, stage and house managers, and crew members with emphasis on acting and evaluated by the supervising teacher. Effective students' contribution at critical meetings, before and after the production, is encouraged.

This course introduces students to the basic elements of prose fiction. Emphasis shall be on the major features/characteristics of the prose fiction genre. The students shall be introduced to the genre and sub-genres of prose. Basic literary texts of prose fiction from Africa and other continents shall be introduced to highlight character, characterization, narrative techniques, fiction, non-fiction, faction, allegory, fable, foreshadowing, suspense, conflict, etc.

The course emphasizes a detailed study of word-formation process and the internal structure of words in English. Words and morphemes, inflectional morphology (including the identification of lexical and grammatical categories). Word-formation process in English derivation, compounding invention, echoism, clipping, acronym, branding and back-formation.

The course presents the psycholinguistics account of language and the relationship between language and the mind. Topics to be covered include language acquisition and language learning; language, thinking and cognition; language and the brain, language localization, linguistic performance and behavior production and comprehension; language impairment

The course covers both African oral literature and the early development of written literature. The origins and historical background of African traditional literature will receive special focus. This is because students will need to be immersed in an interdisciplinary approach in their way of dealing with the large variety of literature coming from the continent. The course examines the different genres of literature. That is, such as the short story, poetry, drama, and the novel, to provide students with a general knowledge about African literature in its various forms.

course_title: Survey of English and American Literature
description: Survey of English and American literature studies the diverse range of texts produced in the U. K and the U.S., through which their history and culture are constructed. The course examines a range of English and American writing produced between the end of the First World War and the dawn of the 21st century. It looks at some major English and American writers, but also considers the evolving path of English and American literary history in the twentieth century, its relationship to the social upheavals of the times, as well as to the aesthetic and generic development of English and American art and writing. It also examines novels, short stories, poetry, and plays, and considers the changing fate of these forms in the age of modernism and postmodernism. Some of the fundamental issues of twentieth-century British and American life (wars in Europe, the civil rights movement, second-wave feminism, the triumph of late capitalism, urbanism and its discontents, religion and secularism, etc.) will be explored. Also, a wide variety of literary forms and styles: from the modernist novella and the postwar realist novel, through the experimentalism of Native American writing to genre fiction, theatre, painting, and film.

The course examines the historical background of West Indian Literature, firmly placing the vision of the writers within the backdrop of ancestral enslavement, cultural dislocation, racial prejudices, and social alienation. Detailed critical examination is made of selected texts. Attention will be paid to such peculiarities as the quest for self-identity, “the muletto” style, and the condition of the exile. The overall intention is to identify the nuances and texture of African-American and Caribbean literature while placing it within the wider framework of established Anglophone literature.

course_title: Approaches to Literary Theory and Criticism
description: The course provides students with a working knowledge of the critical concepts and approaches in literary theory and criticism from the impressionistic criticism and deconstruction. The course clarifies and consolidates the practice of criticism and helps the student to identify specific schools of literary analysis and theories, in terms of peculiar tenets, proponents, and techniques of textual disclosure. It explores the various application of literary theories to textual and practical criticism.

This course on sociolinguistics focuses on history, ideologies, methodology, concepts and application of sociolinguistics. It considers the relationship between language and society with a focus on the attitude toward language varieties and social dialects. For example, the geographic, social and functional varieties of English as well as other language varieties in the Liberian context and how such variations construct and are in turn constructed by identity. The course also delves into some of the educational, political, and social repercussions of these sociolinguistics facts. It also offers an opportunity for understanding key principles of second language learning. Individual differences in second language learning (general learning ability, phonetic coding ability, grammatical sensitivity, inductive learning ability, associative memory), grammatical competence and communicative competence in second language learners -- the learner's inter-language and its description.

This course emphasizes African - American Literatures from the 18th -20th century and from both historical and literary perspective. Emphasis is placed on the 20th century. Genres include the novel, short story, poetry, and drama. Texts are explored from the perspectives of not only race, but also in the larger context of American literary, artistic and cultural themes and movements. It focuses on the exploration of the emergence of a distinctly black modernist and post-modernist literary discourse, often in response to and in conversation with contemporaneous Anglo-American literary movements and trends. It also investigates some of the following: African American writers' engagement with the “Wright School of Social Protest”; the evolution of the Black Arts/Black Aesthetic Movements of the 1960s and 1970s; the emergence of black feminist and womanist literature, criticism, and theory in the 1970s and 1980s; and the so-called “third renaissance” of the 1990s and 2000s.

The course is a descriptive survey of the major genres of traditional African oral art and performance. It also examines the lyrics, myths, folktales, legend, epic, proverbs, praise-poetry, and ritual dramatic forms. These and stylistic techniques are evaluated in order to demonstrate the nature and scope of traditional African creative imagination.

The course will examine, among others, the theoretical assertion that variable gender identities are a result of psychological, historical and cultural factors that impact on society by analyzing the works of some gender theorists as well as creative writers from Africa, the West and other traditions that are relevant to the subject. The latter part of the course focuses the conventional stereotypes of women and male writers and examines the changing image of women as reflected in literature and so relating to historical, economic, and cultural circumstances. Issues to be discussed include the idealization of the woman in the chivalric and courtly traditions versus the portrayal of woman as Eve, temptress and object of attire; the “bad” woman in the bourgeoisie novel versus woman as mother and soul of the home, earth mother, misogyny and portrayal of the early feminist; woman in African literature. Works to be critically analyzed from the feminist perspective will be chosen from African folklore and traditional literature, selections of medieval and Renaissance poetry.

course_title: Literary Genre, Classification, Forms, Convention and Techniques
description: This is an intensive and extensive course focusing on the genres of literature, basically their forms, conventions and techniques. It studies a variety of English and African poems to introduce students to the major poetic forms, conventions and techniques -- didactic, courtly, heroic, mock-heroic, pastoral elegy, lyric, ballad, ode, sonnet, dramatic monologue, narrative poetry, confessional poetry, imagery, symbolism etc. It also attempt a survey of the classifications and techniques of the novel taking into account the conventions, advantages and disadvantages of each form. Types of narration: first person, epistolary, stream of consciousness, omniscient narrative, untrustworthy narrator, historic, allegory, hero, anti-hero shall be examined. The drama genre focuses on classical tragedy, modern heroic tragedy, morality play, farce, history play, romantic comedy of manners, social criticism, Marxist drama, theatre of the absurd, poetic drama, influences of sage type an audience and the use of mythology in drama.
It focuses on utterance meaning, as distinct from sentence meaning, on the socio-cultural and linguistic rules that determine correct interpretation of terms in the real world.

Liberian literature has a valuable canon, a cultural asset to be preserved, organized, and recorded by literary history, both in Liberia and the world. It contains a chronological record of Liberia's pastoral, folk literature, with its folk songs, proverbs, folk tales, known since the 1800s. There are writers, genres, and species, from poetry to drama, important to be taught for the ongoing development of Liberia's literary history. In this regard, earlier African narratives in English expression written by Africans such as Guanya Pau, by Joseph Walters, 1891. We find written poetry dating back to the 1800s; ‘Leaves from Love's Garden,' written by Edwin Barclay, 1836-1961; ‘Echoes of the Valley,' edited by Roland T. Dempster, 1947; ‘Poems of Liberia,' collected by A. Doris Banks Henries, 1963. We find Liberia's novelists, Bai T. Moore, Henry B. Cole, Wilton S. Sankawulo; short story writers, such as Robert H. Brown, Patricia Jabbeh Wesley. Dramatists also occupy a notable position: Lester Parker, Kona Khasu, Peter Ballah, Kerkura Kamara, and Womi Neal have all contributed by their work to the development of a literary genre in Liberia.

Examines the syntactic treatment of topics of relevance and currency in English Syntax. Recommended for inclusion are pronominalization, complement structures, case marking, thematic roles, negation, grammatical categories (e.g. tense, aspect, etc, ‘theta' marking, etc.

This course makes use of lectures and workshops and debates to help students develop written and oral communication skills for preparing and presenting technical and other specialized reports. It covers such areas as basic communication skills, communication graphics, resume/c.v, application letters, research skills, methodologies, research proposals, collaborative work and oral presentations, job interviews, debate and public speaking skills. The course concentrates on representative plays selected to introduce the students to all the various types of drama written by master playwrights on histories, tragedies, comedies, and romances. The course will examine critically the contents of these selected palys as well as their structure, language and principles underlying them. References will be made to Elizabethan drama in general and dramatic practices in England as appropriate for comparative basis for the study.

Is a course on the basic concepts in study of stylistics analysis of literary and non-literary texts with focus on differentiations, mode, field, tenor, etc. Seminar papers (at least two) analyzing the language and style of a cross-section of major authors are crucial.

This course is designed to enable students appreciate the literature of the diverse cultures and people in the world as an important part of their overall educational experiences. In particular, the course aims at developing students' critical thinking process, the understanding and celebrating of diverse cultures and experiences as a tool for independent assessment of human issues. It also should help in molding and forming the characters of the students morally and intellectually. Readings the works of writers in the African diaspora will be a central part of this course.

course_title: 18th Century Literature and Early English Novel
description: Attention is paid to the works of John Dryden, Jonathan Swift, Alexander Pope, Samuel Johnson (poetry): William Congreve, Oliver Goldsmith and R.S. Sheridan (drama): Jonathan Swift, Samuel Johnson (fiction). The aim is to introduce English Single Honors students to the influence of “satire” and “comedy” in the literature of England from 1660 -- 1700. Attempt will be made to relate the artist to his socio-cultural setting, especially to the importance of politics and city civilization creating a new aesthetic awareness.

This course explores the English Language use vis a vis its structure, history and evolution the English language. It will also delve to the contemporary dynamics of the language such as new word creations across cultures and regions, grammar, vocabulary and pronunciations. This course will expose students to the socio-political and cultural factors influencing modern usage of the English language. Factors like artificial intelligence, digital humanities, online content creation, and their impact on the modern English language, focusing on their practical application and linguistic features.

In the course students will continue learning from engl102 by being introduced to the kind of prose they will deal with in their academic career and the connection between reading and writing academic prose. Reading selections will become opportunities for further in-class dialogue, making thematic inter-textual connections, discourse analysis, reasoning and writing across disciplines. The course emphasizes the various forms of essay writing at the standards required for a university level student and techniques of writing prose logically and coherently. Attention will be given to appropriate language use and technical guidelines associated with kinds of writing.
A preparatory course for the Project/Long Essay, the course introduces students to the basic methods and tools of research.

The student's maturity in the study of English Language and Literature in English should be reflected in this course. Under the direction and supervision of a qualified faculty, each student is expected to focus on a research topic of their interest. Such a topic is to be decided upon with the assistance of the supervisor, who oversees the writing. The length of the research must be between 6,000 and 8,000 words.

This is a course that provides overview of the world to foster environmental awareness of population, resources and environmental degradation. Topics of environmental health with sources, routes, media, and health outcomes associated with biological, chemical and physical agents in the environment are discussed. It includes study of the effects of agents on disease, water quality, air quality, food safety, and land resources, current legal frameworks, and polices associated with environmental issues. The course enhances students' responsibility as agents of change.

course_title: Elective Social Science (History / Philosophy / Psychology / Sociology)
description: This elective package requires students to complete one of hist101, phil101, psyc101, or soci101 where the program uses the Freshman Semester I social science elective group. The selected course satisfies the package requirement and carries the course-specific content, prerequisite chain, and departmental ownership of the chosen option.

course_title: Elective Social Science (History / Philosophy / Psychology / Sociology)
description: This elective package requires students to complete one of hist102, phil102, psyc102, or soci102 where the program uses the Freshman Semester II social science elective group. The selected course satisfies the package requirement and carries the course-specific content, prerequisite chain, and departmental ownership of the chosen option.

This course introduces strategies and practices to develop key early literacy skills in young learners. Teacher-aspirants will explore phonemic awareness, phonics, vocabulary development, fluency, and comprehension techniques to foster a strong literacy foundation. Through hands-on activities, lesson planning, and assessment tools, teacher-aspirants will learn to create engaging, developmentally appropriate instructions that support diverse learners in early years to master essential writing, speaking, reading, and listening skills.

course_title: Principles and Foundations of Education in Early Years
description: This course discusses the core principles and philosophical underpinnings that shape early years education. Teacher-aspirants will examine the historical and contemporary theories of learning and development, including the works of Montessori, Piaget, Vygotsky, Bowlby, Bronfenbrenner, and other influential educators in child learning and development. Students will develop their individual teaching philosophies that will guide them throughout their studies. Emphasis will be placed on understanding how these philosophies inform teaching practices, curriculum design, and the creation of nurturing, child-centered learning environments.

This course will expose teacher-aspirants to major concepts in biological and chemical sciences. Biological science will place emphasis on the scientific concept of life, characteristics of living things, the function of biological micro molecules as building blocks of cells, the structure and function of cellular components, prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, classification of living things, pollution, microorganisms, plants, and systems of the human body, evolution principle, and common adaptation. The chemical science portion of the course will address matter, its properties, and states, mixture, atoms and molecules, and the periodic table.

This course is designed to help students acquire general knowledge in Earth and Physical sciences. Earth science contents will place emphases on the solar system, the earth and its composition, energy, natural resources, water cycle, and global environmental challenges. The physical science contents will provide students with general knowledge on system of measurement, motion and force, work, machine, and wave.

This course explores the physical, social, mental, and emotional development of the young child with implications for learning. The course provides the broad overview of the development process underlying child and adolescent development. An overview of human development across the lifespan, including the consideration of change in one's physical, intellectual, personality and social domains and the influence of contexts of development (e.g., the family, school, peers, and culture) within each domain. Also, the course equips students with specific skills related to child and adolescent psychology.

course_title: Sociology of Education and Working with Families and Communities
description: Family-school-community partnership is a shared responsibility and reciprocal process that is geared towards active support for the child's holistic development. Over time, parent and community involvement has always been a cornerstone of public schools in the form of Parents-Teachers Association (PTA), however, greater recognition and support of the importance of these collaborative efforts is needed. Hence, this course will explore theories and best practices in joining families, communities, and schools in promoting children's learning, development, and success in school; strengths and needs of families in a diverse and multicultural society. The course emphasized teachers' roles in concert with other disciplines in supporting families and building partnerships, and connection with community resources.

This course focuses specifically on understanding infants, toddlers, and adolescents' developmental needs through carefully sequenced modules on topics such as children's physical and emotional health, nutrition, safety, and issues of neglect and abuse, case studies, and clinical observations etc. The course also focuses on the developmental milestones of typical and atypical traits among children from Birth -- 18 years, and development of intervention strategies for atypical behaviors. Students will demonstrate comprehensive knowledge of typical and atypical behavioral traits associated with development. Teacher-aspirants will explore basic principles and practices as well as culturally relevant strategies for intervention with parents and children themselves. Students will review governmental and social policies for promoting healthy, safe, and nurturing environments for children and families. This course will underscore the use of the Infant/Toddler Environmental Rating Scale (ITERS). (Fieldwork required)

This course provides an engaging exploration of creative expression through music, visual arts, and performance. Teacher-aspirants will learn foundational techniques, foster creativity, and develop skills in these artistic disciplines. Through hands-on projects, collaborative activities, and critical analysis, students will gain an understanding of the cultural and emotional impact of the arts while creating their own original works. Ideal for aspiring artists and educators looking to integrate creative arts into learning environments.

course_title: Early Years Program Organization and Management
description: The course is designed to equip students with knowledge and skills of what is entailed to start-off (establish) and run a successful early years' school program. Hence, the course will explore administration, personnel, physical plant, financial planning and management, licensure, and curriculum, etc. in establishing and maintaining a successful early years' program. Furthermore, the course will expose students to other possible career pursuit in early years' education.

course_title: Team Building and Mental Conditioning of Players; Planning Pre- Season Program
description: This course discusses reading the game, methods of coaching i.e. Observe and identify the technical and tactical demands of the game as they relate to the principles of the game. To prepare coaches to better recognize problems and solutions as they occur in games up to 7v7. Organize, develop and implement appropriate training sessions and offer correct technical and tactical guidance to players. Develop a better understanding of the responsibilities of coaching.

This course focuses on leadership skills, the ability to teach players how to do something, patience and the ability to remain calm in stressful situations, the ability to monitor your own performance and that of your colleagues, excellent verbal communication skills, knowledge of teaching and the ability to design courses, to be thorough and pay attention to detail, the ability to use your initiative, the management skills and motivational skills for success.

This course organizes appropriate practices to improve a player's basic technique and decision, making ability to organize appropriate practices to improve a player's basic attacking and defensive awareness, integrate development practices throughout a training session, and demonstrate effective communication skills to facilitate a player's appropriate involvement within the practices course. It also include technique and Skill Practices 1 and 2, Basic Playing System Practices, attacking awareness practices, defensive awareness practices, set play awareness practices ,set play awareness in attack and defense, goalkeeper awareness practices and candidates practical evaluation and feedback.

course_title: Team Preparation for a Match and Development of Playing Styles
description: This course is to educate the coaches in the tactical and advanced technical requirements of team football and team management. The coaches will also be exposed to the demands of high-level performance through understanding of the physiological and psychological needs of the players. The course is designed to educate coaches in the advanced techniques and skills of the game and to develop an understanding

This course is designed to provide an intermediate level of coaching football (soccer) information, fundamentals, principles and management enablers for anyone who would coach and / or instruct intermediate football (soccer) skills, it is also design to provide entry-level information, fundamentals, principles, and management enablers for anyone who would coach and / or instruct basic Football (Soccer) skill

This course is designed to emphasize the importance of nutrition on the enhancement of performance and on the prevention of diseases, such as cardiovascular disease and obesity. It also focuses on Specific applications of nutrition and sport will also be examined. An investigation of the immediate assistance administered in the case of injury or sudden illness before the arrival of trained medical personnel. This course includes certification in Sport Safety first aid and CPR etc.

This course discuses construction of attack, the play of the libero/ screening ,midfielder in attack development of midfield play Wing play, strikers play, attacking play -- ways to the goal counter attack, attacking play against compact defense, attacking play at set pieces -- corner-kicks and free-kicks, defense playing with a back four, defense playing with a back three, the art of midfield defending, forward players -- defending responsibilities, defending against wing play and crosses.

This course introduces students to information needed to successfully program for sport-oriented organizations. Students will chose and/or develop programs or events, flyers advertising the events, budgets, and help to run the events on or off campus. Students will be required to work at events outside of class time.

course_title: Introduction to Game Systems and Tactics
description: The course is designed to educate coaches in the advanced techniques and skills of the game and to develop an understanding of individual, group and team tactics, inherent in the playing of the game. The physical and mental requirements of players are also addressed. The development and use of possession.it also includes, individual attacking play, combination play, crossing the ball, scoring from crosses, goal scoring, defending skills: Individual and combined and team defending skills

course_title: Effective Communication and Organizing Individual Training and Coaching Sessions
description: The course is designed to educate coaches how to organize, direct and conduct youth coaching practices and is targeted mainly to develop the fundamental techniques, skill and understanding of youth players. It also includes teachings skills in dribbling passing and supporting play, controlling the ball, shooting, heading, defending, goalkeeping, attacking play, creative football, small-sided games.

This is capstone course for coaching that applied all necessary knowledge, skills and methods of coaching to the experience through direct observation and participation with a sport team. It is structured as a class combined with site coaching experience. The student will be instructed theoretically by an instructor and supervised practically by a head coach of an assigned team.

This course is designed to provide semi-professional level information, fundamentals, principles, and management enablers for anyone who would coach and / or instruct semi-professional Football (Soccer) skills.

This course is designed to provide semi-professional level information, fundamentals, principles, and management enablers for anyone who would coach and / or instruct semi-professional Football (Soccer) skills

This course is designed to provide an intermediate level of coaching football (soccer) information, fundamentals, principles and management enablers for anyone who would coach and / or instruct intermediate football (soccer) skills
This course is a senior seminar, which examines the current trends and issues in the sport industry. Extensive research of current texts and journal articles is required.

course_title: The Goalkeepers Psychological Approach to Training and Improve Technique to Game Awareness
description: This course Organize a development programs for goalkeepers, organize and implement advanced goalkeeper fitness sessions, organize and implement appropriate practices to improve a keeper's technique and decision-making ability, integrate field players and keeper skills. It also includes principles of goalkeeper coaching to provide candidates with the knowledge and competency to organize goalkeeper related practical and theory sessions to the youth level, understand the role of the goalkeeper and required skills conduct goalkeeper related warm up and stretching sessions. Organize a periodic training program for all ages, coach basic goalkeeper techniques analyses and correct problems in technique, coach game awareness and practical warm up and stretching routines technique.

This course aims to provide basic knowledge to learners. It prepares learners to handle simple everyday interactions that they will encounter in francophone countries as well as to introduce to some particularities. The course enable learners to link physical or abstract things to their names and acquaint themselves with their different names.
This course aims at expanding learners' knowledge of French-speaking countries while developing their French vocabulary and pronunciation.

This course takes learners beyond the basic situations encountered in the other courses. Learners will expand their knowledge of French through hands-on approach and gain more independence by responding to job-related inquiries and interacting with their colleagues.

course_title: La Francophonie (people, places, travels and events)
description: This course aims at sharpening learners French as they discover the French-speaking world and improve their language skills as they journey through the Worlds of French.
This course aims at enabling learners to discover traditions and festivals in the French-speaking world and apply French language structures effectively.

This course aims at enabling learners to discover the francophone media: cinema and television, print media and radio programs. Learners will be able to discuss genres and programs of interest through hands-on approach.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

French 201 is the first semester course of elementary French. The aim of the course is to build skills in speaking, understanding, writing and reading French through class activities, practice, and study. A variety of class activities are designed to help students acquire knowledge of the basic French vocabulary, foundational grammar concepts and structures along with the ability to use what they have learned in situations of communication. In this course, there is a possibility of using a multimedia approach that incorporates texts, songs, videos, and the internet and helps students acquire linguistic as well as cultural competence. By the end of this course, students will be able to create with the language to talk about familiar topics related to daily life and to express personal meaning.

This course is a continuation and expansion of some of the key grammatical structures, vocabulary, and expressions from French 101. It is designed to improve students’ French language proficiency in speaking, reading, and writing with emphasis on oral proficiency. The aim is to advance greater familiarity and knowledge of the French language.

This course is based on a textbook that enhances the main skills of listening comprehension, reading, writing and oral skills. French for Specialization I covers the following grammatical content: present tense, the main rules concerning nouns and adjectives, possessive pronouns, numbers, negation, prepositions, demonstrative, relative and personal pronoun.

This course focuses on the basic skills in French: Listening comprehension, reading, writing and oral skills. French for Specialization II covers the following grammatical content: present tense, past tense (passé composé/imparfait), future, conditionnel, imperative, indirect speech, systematization of the subjunctive, comparison and conditional clauses.

course_title: Literary theory and criticism in French Literature
description: This course delves into the exploration of various literary theories and critical approaches applied to French literature. It likely covers different movements, key figures, and methodologies that have shaped the interpretation and analysis of French literary works throughout history. Students might study structuralism, post-structuralism, psychoanalysis, feminism, and other theoretical frameworks within the context of French literature.

This course offers an exploration of the rich tradition and diverse styles of French poetry, from classic to contemporary works. Students delve into key movements, poetic forms, and influential poets, enhancing their understanding of French language, culture, and literary expression. Through close reading, analysis, and discussion, participants develop critical thinking skills and deepen their appreciation for the beauty and complexity of French verse.

This course provides a comprehensive overview of French grammar and vocabulary, designed to help learners develop a strong foundation in the language. Through a combination of lectures, exercises, and practice sessions, students will explore essential grammar rules, verb conjugations, sentence structure, and common vocabulary used in everyday communication. Emphasis will be placed on building practical language skills for effective reading, writing, speaking, and listening in French. By the end of the course, students will have acquired the necessary tools to navigate various linguistic contexts with confidence and fluency

This course aims at enhancing learners' comprehension of the complexities of the French language. Through thorough study, students will delve into intricate grammatical structures, nuanced vocabulary usage, and idiomatic expressions. Topics may include advanced verb conjugations, subjunctive mood, relative pronouns, and stylistic elements. The focus will be on practical application through exercises, discussions, and written assignments to improve learners' proficiency in French.

This course delves into the intricate nuances of the French language, focusing on its stylistic elements and composition techniques. Students typically explore various literary works, analyzing linguistic structures, rhetorical devices, and cultural contexts to enhance their understanding and mastery of French writing. The course often emphasizes practical writing exercises to develop students' composition skills, allowing them to express themselves effectively and creatively in French.

This course is designed to develop language skills specifically tailored for business contexts within the French-speaking world. The course covers vocabulary, grammar, and cultural nuances essential for effective communication in various professional settings. Topics include business etiquette, correspondence, presentations, negotiations, and cross-cultural communication strategies. Students will engage in practical exercises, role-plays, and case studies to enhance their ability to communicate confidently and effectively in French within the business environment.

This course will enable learners to delve into the complexities of French sounds, with a focus on accurate pronunciation, intonation, and rhythm. Through various exercises and practice, students will enhance your ability to produce authentic French sounds, including vowels, consonants, and liaisons. Additionally, learners will learn about phonetic symbols and patterns to improve your listening comprehension and speaking skills. This course is essential for anyone striving for fluency and precision in spoken French.

This course provides an in-depth exploration of semantics and pragmatics within the context of the French language. It is designed for students who have an intermediate to advanced proficiency in French and wish to deepen their understanding of meaning in language.

This course explores different facets of French history, art, literature, philosophy, politics, cuisine, and social customs. It delves into France's rich cultural heritage, examining how historical events and societal norms have influenced modern French identity. Topics may include the French Revolution, Enlightenment thinkers, impressionist art, existentialist philosophy, culinary traditions, and contemporary social issues.

This course delves into the intricate world of French phonology and syntax, providing students with a comprehensive understanding of the sound system and sentence structure of the French language. Through a combination of theoretical study, practical exercises, and authentic language examples, students will develop the skills necessary to comprehend and produce spoken and written French with accuracy and fluency.

This course focuses on improving learners written French language skills. The course uses a structured curriculum to help students enhance your grammar, expand their vocabulary, and develop a better understanding of sentence structure. With an emphasis on practical application, the course includes various writing assignments, from formal compositions to informal reflections, to help learners express their thoughts and ideas clearly and accurately. Cultural insights and feedback are also provided to enrich learners learning experience and help them communicate effectively in different situations.

The course aims to help students become proficient in translating between French and English, with a focus on grammar, vocabulary, and cultural nuances. Topics may include idiomatic expressions, specialized terminology, and practical translation techniques. Students will participate in exercises, readings, and projects to improve their skills in both written and oral translation.

course_title: Introduction to Francophone African Drama and Prose
description: This course is a comprehensive exploration of the rich literary traditions of African authors who write in French. It delves into various themes, styles, and historical contexts of Francophone African literature, focusing particularly on drama and prose forms. Students will analyze works by prominent writers from different regions of Africa, examining their cultural, social, and political significance within the context of colonialism, post-colonialism, and globalization. Through readings, discussions, and critical analysis, students will gain a deeper understanding of the diverse voices and narratives shaping contemporary African literature.

This course explores the rich literary traditions of French-speaking Africa and the Caribbean, examining themes such as colonialism, identity, diaspora, and cultural hybridity through the lens of literature. Students will study works by authors such as Aimé Césaire, Léopold Sédar Senghor, Maryse Condé, and many others, gaining insight into the historical, social, and political contexts that shape these diverse literary landscapes.

This course explores the evolution of French political and social thought, examining key thinkers and their ideas from different historical periods. Topics may include Rousseau's social contract theory, Montesquieu's separation of powers, existentialism, feminism, post-colonialism, and contemporary debates in French political philosophy.

French dialectology is the study of regional variations and dialects within the French language. It examines the linguistic differences, including phonological, grammatical, and lexical aspects, across different regions where French is spoken. This course typically delves into the historical, social, and cultural factors that have shaped these linguistic variations, offering insights into the rich diversity of the French language. Students might explore various dialects spoken in France, Canada, Africa, and other francophone regions, as well as the impact of globalization and standardization on these dialects.

This is a thorough course created to provide educators with the necessary skills and knowledge to effectively teach French as a second language. The course covers theoretical frameworks and practical applications, focusing on various aspects of language teaching such as curriculum development, lesson planning, instructional strategies, assessment techniques, and classroom management. Emphasis is placed on communicative approaches to language teaching, integrating cultural components into instruction, and utilizing technology to enhance learning outcomes. By the end of the course, participants will be prepared to design engaging and culturally relevant French language lessons that cater to diverse learner needs and promote proficiency in both oral and written communication.

Francophone literature encompasses literary works created in French-speaking regions worldwide, not limited to France. It explores the diverse literary traditions of Africa, the Caribbean, Canada, and other regions, examining unique themes, styles, and cultural contexts. Students typically analyze a range of texts, such as novels, poems, plays, and essays, to gain a deeper understanding of the Francophone literary landscape and its importance.

This is a course designed to help learners master the sounds of the French language. Through a combination of theoretical explanations and practical exercises, students will develop their ability to pronounce French words accurately and improve their overall speaking proficiency. Topics covered may include pronunciation of vowels, consonants, liaison, intonation, and rhythm. By the end of the course, students should feel more confident in their French pronunciation and be better equipped to communicate effectively in French-speaking environments

This course explores various aspects of French culture, including literature, art, film, history, language, and society. It delves into the rich heritage of France, from its iconic landmarks like the Eiffel Tower to its renowned artists like Monet and Renoir. Students might analyze literary works by authors such as Victor Hugo or delve into the impact of French cinema on global culture. Discussions may also cover topics like cuisine, fashion, philosophy, and current events, providing a comprehensive understanding of French culture and its significance in the world.

This course typically covers methodologies and techniques used in academic research within the French context. The course includes topics such as qualitative and quantitative research methods, data collection and analysis, literature review techniques, and ethical considerations specific to research conducted in French-speaking countries or within the French academic tradition. Additionally, the course may explore French-language research resources and scholarly communication norms.

The internship for students pursuing a BA in French Studies provides an opportunity to apply language skills and cultural knowledge in a professional setting. Students will gain practical insight into fields such as translation, interpretation, cultural exchange, education, or international business through hands-on experience. The internship typically lasts a semester or a summer, and students may be placed in various organizations, including multinational corporations, non-profit organizations, or government agencies. Responsibilities may include assisting with translation tasks, conducting research, organizing cultural events, or supporting communication initiatives. The internship aims to enhance students' linguistic proficiency, cultural competence, and professional development within the context of French language and culture.

This is an advanced course focusing on refining French language skills to achieve higher proficiency levels. It typically includes intensive practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing, with an emphasis on advanced grammar, vocabulary expansion, and cultural nuances. Students can expect to engage in discussions, debates, and analysis of complex texts to enhance their linguistic and communicative abilities in French.

This Capstone Project involves integrating language skills, cultural knowledge, and critical thinking acquired throughout your French studies. It includes a research component, presentation, or creative project, demonstrating proficiency and understanding of French language and culture. Under the direction and supervision of a qualified faculty, each student is expected to focus on a research topic of their interest. Such a topic is to be decided upon with the assistance of the supervisor, who oversees the writing. Topics range from literature analysis to sociopolitical issues in French-speaking countries.

This course will provide an understanding of the basic concepts; growth and development of human (nature and nurture), descriptive analysis of basic theories, patterns and stages of human physical, social, emotional, moral, intellectual, cognitive, and personality. Students will learn the development of human from conception till birth and through elder adulthood in a multicultural context, growth and development of human throughout life span. Understanding human behavior through their biological make-up will help students to identify their patient's problem and dealing with the problem appropriately. The course will be taught from perspective of cognitive science and behavioral systems as well as sociological, cultural, life-span develop and comparative approaches. It will also focus on normative development; developmental disorders are used to elucidate normative developmental and adaptive processes in language, cognition and behavioral self-regulation that will serve to introduce students to behaviors and concepts relevant to clinical practice with both children and adults. (psyc101)

This course provides an understanding of the basic concepts; development and history of guidance, roles and functions of counselor, and basic guidance services will be explored. Students learn about what is to be accomplished in the counseling process, principles, techniques, types of counseling and the steps involved. In this course, students will be able to share confidentiality rules with the client, explain what cannot be kept confidential and other ethical issues in counseling (gced102)

The course is to enable the students to perform psychodynamic and cognitive behavioral psychotherapy under supervision, and to apply psychotherapeutic methods based in relevant theories and treatment. It covers the scientific and philosophical origins, fundamental principles, and treatment implications of the primary systems of psychotherapy. This will enable the students to conduct therapy taking into account a societal perspective on the client, and ethical and legal considerations. The students are also to actively and critically reflect on their own role and the applicability of the method for solving different problems.

This course will focus on working with children, adolescents and adults suffering from grief and loss. Students will learn best methods of working with and talking to their patient about loss and how to provide therapeutic interventions. Students will study attachment theory. Students will examine many models of grief and loss as well as learn accompanying effective interventions to use with clients in mental health counseling and therapy sessions. It will also focus on tools and criteria for comprehensive assessments of clients experiencing various types of grief reactions. Students will study grief therapies and learn when to refer clients for counseling. Other important study in this course will include; counseling in groups, grief as a social phenomenon, assessing at-risk bereaved, funeral rituals, and much more. Issues of the counselor's own grief and burn-out syndromes will also be addressed. Special situations of grief and loss and mourning will also be explored, such as sudden death, violent death, suicide, child death, aged death and more. Interventions for working with special situations will also be presented (gced102, educ203).

This course provides the preparation needed to assist in the promotion of students' vocational and career development. The course will explore career development theories and appropriate assessment strategies that can be used to evaluate a student's academic, career, and vocational interest as well as the decision-making processes in making career choices. Skills in interpreting data to determine an individual's talents, strengths and needs, and frameworks to enable recognizing uniqueness in cultures, values and traditions and their connection with career choices and decision-making will be included. Course focus will include exploration of the central role of entrepreneurship and self-employment in the Liberian economy and the implication of this knowledge for vocational and career counseling.

This course examines the issues surrounding psychological and emotional crisis, with an emphasis on trauma. The course will be organized in modules focusing on: (1) Crisis situations that can arise for children, adolescents and teachers in a school setting pertaining to issues such as loss due to death, violence, suicide, and separation such as in divorce; the impact of these events on the learner, educators, and school environment; (2) Triggers of abuse including the structural causes of gender- based and domestic violence and (3) Roles and responsibilities of the school guidance counselor during crises, disasters, and other trauma-causing events and the possible interventions and prevention approaches that can be implemented. The course reviews techniques and exercises that will better equip the students in conflict resolution and mediation with school-aged individuals, teachers, principals and families.

This course will teach students how to use art in therapeutic process to help clients heal and grow. The course will use a variety of art forms, such as dance movement, drawing, music, poetry, drama, and painting and so on. The course will provide students with alternate approaches to conventional models of school and clinical counseling programs. The use of the arts and music in assessing and interpreting clients' strength and needs, and in recognizing uniqueness in cultures, languages, values, backgrounds and abilities will be explored. African literary works and images reflected in the arts and media will also be analyzed for representation of historical, economic, and cultural factors and the creative opportunities they offer in guidance and counseling.

This course will be based on studying abnormal human behavior. It will focus on development of abnormal human behavior, patterns and characteristics which will include the major mental and personality disorders, types of personality disorders, with emphasis on the symptom and/or life circumstances and events described in the various diagnostic categories. In this course, students will learn how to diagnosis patient with abnormal behavior, provide treatment and helping such an individual to overcome their problem. Students will also learn how to make use of Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM) in comparing the changes in the old and latest version of the manual (gced202, gced206, educ202).

The course will be based on domestic violence (DV) / intimate partner violence (IPV). This course presents updated information about domestic violence, including information about various types of abuse, scope of the problem, victims of abuse, perpetrators, role of health care providers, and victim resources. Risk factors and information about various populations including, spouses, children, adolescents and the elderly will be presented. Profile information on both victims and perpetrators of domestic violence will be provided. Theories that explain violence will be discussed. Issues relevant to assessment including signs and symptoms, specific injuries and patterns of injury known to be associated with abuse, screening methods and tools, and counseling roles will be discussed. Students from Liberia will be provided with Internet resources to obtain information on laws and domestic violence county-by-county. (Field experience required)

course_title: Theories and Techniques in Counseling and Psychotherapy
description: This course will be based on the use of theories and techniques in counseling. The course will focus on the study and analysis of the major affective, cognitive, and behavioral theories and therapeutic approaches to individual counseling. Emphasis will be laid on historical perspectives and practical application. It will also focus on the development and effective use of skills and techniques on the basic process of individual counseling. Students will learn different types of approaches used in counseling and how to apply them in counseling. Students will also examine how counselor's self-awareness, self-reflection and self-care impact both treatment and establishing a strong therapeutic alliance with their patient.

The course presents theories and perspectives on personality. The goal of this course is to explain and evaluate major concepts in personality. Some of the topics will include trait, psychodynamic, behavioral, and humanistic theories. Methods of personality research and relevant findings will also be introduced. The School Adjustment and Mental Health Counseling program trains mental health professionals and counselors to work within the school culture and in clinical settings serving the mental health needs of students and their families. The use of evidence-based counseling strategies when working with children and adolescents in school settings will be emphasized. A variety of topics/stressors experienced by youth, such as violence, trauma, death, divorce, poverty, neglect and abuse, alcohol and substance abuse will be explored. The critical role of the school counselor with respect to general educators, special educators, specialists, administrators, and parents will be examined. An introduction to the juvenile justice system will be provided. A systematic approach aimed at identifying effective strategies for partnering with families and community services will be emphasized. The course will also address the needs of families with children who have learning disorders, medical conditions, and physical disabilities. The needs of families with gifted children, foster children, and children in kinship care will also be studied.

This course covers the media which incorporates technology supports counseling. The information and communication technology (ICT) plays a vital role in distance counseling (DC) setup. It does not only work for client/patient support services, but also to prepare, design and deliver activities on time. Also, it is used by learners for their necessity and enhancement of their skills. Counseling is the part of the support services to distance client/patient. Clients are heterogeneous in character and belong to different geographical, economic, cultural, and religious background; therefore, they need support services differently. In relation to different programmes it demands different types of support services to provide on time services to clients, therefore media and technology is the best method to be adapted in distance counseling across the globe. The detailed analysis of media and their use in counseling services will be discussed in this course.

The primary focus of this course is on diagnosis, treatment and prevention of substance abuse disorders (drug and alcohol dependence and addiction). The effective use of interviewing skills and various screening and assessment tools will be presented to help students assess the severity of abuse and to develop an initial treatment plan. Treatment settings, effective interventions used with chemically dependent clients and comorbidity issues (the interface between addiction and psychopathology) will be discussed. Substance use as relating to issues of diversity will be examined. Other addictions such as gambling, gaming, sex, anorexia/bulimia will also be studied. Pharmacological, physiological, and medical aspects of addictions along with current evidence-based research and treatment care models will be reviewed. It will also examine the role of trauma in addiction and mental health issues. (Field experience required)

This course will provide an explanation on the issues relevant to the role and function of school counselor including the use of data in planning, implementing, and evaluating a comprehensive school counseling program that is linked to school improvement plans and current education reform issues. Emphasis will be placed on developing competencies in the knowledge, use, and interpretation of individual and group approaches for assessment and evaluation in school settings.
