It will also provide school counselors with a combination of theory and practice for implementing a comprehensive school counseling program that is consistent with national and county developmental models to advance academic, personal/social, and career opportunities for all children in a pluralistic society. The history of school counseling, development of school counseling, and contemporary roles and functions of school counselors will be emphasized. Curriculum development and instruction, educational planning, and consultation and collaboration skills will be introduced. Practical strategies for planning, managing, and evaluating a comprehensive school program will be present utilizing national and county models (Field experience required).

course_title: Family and Group Counseling and Psychotherapy
description: This course focuses on Family and Group Counseling. This course provides students with an opportunity to learn about the processes of group counseling by participating in experiential activities that demonstrate the basic principles of group counseling. Students will examine the various types of groups and the different techniques appropriate to each. The course also teaches students to understand the specific roles of group leaders and members. Emphasis will be placed on the ethical guidelines related to group work. Topics to be treated include family, types of family, role of parents and children in a home, group therapy, a more in-depth examination of group dynamics, facilitation techniques, group process, methods for selecting group members, counseling theories for group application, and meeting the needs of population-specific groups (e.g., domestic violence survivors, addiction, etc.) as well as types of groups (e.g., psycho-educational). (Field experience required)

The course focuses on ethical, legal, philosophical, and sociological issues involved in the practice of professional school counseling. The primary roles of the school counselor will be presented in relationship to elementary through high school settings. School Counseling is designed to present school counselors-in-training with an impression of the various rudiments that makes up counseling professional role. Some of the topics for discussion in this course will include consultation, professional and ethical responsibilities, career guidance, culturally appropriate assessment, and the methods of intervention. In addition, students will be introduced to the components for developing and managing their own school counseling programs. This course will combine a mixture of theory with exposure to practitioners in the field and related areas to increase the students' understanding of the role and function of the professional school counselor in secondary school (gced301, gced302, gced303, gced306, educ302).

This course will emphasize presentation and discussion of field/research in guidance and counseling. It will also emphasize on theoretical and practical criticism of relevant studies in the field of guidance and counseling which was learnt in the classroom to live experiences. It is designed to prepare counseling students to respond effectively in critical situations within counseling settings. The course work will introduce students to theory and professional expectations of counselors in crisis situations, suicide assessment, substance abuse, anger management, among others. It will also introduce students to the theory and role of consultation in schools, and other counseling settings. Current issues and trends in the field of school counseling and others will be emphasized and integrated throughout the course.

This course will be based on societal changes and trends, human roles, societal subgroups, social norms and interactions and differing lifestyles. It will also focus on socio-cultural characteristics and concerns of subgroups and the application of multicultural counseling. The course will also examine salient client population-specific issues related to the life experiences and world view of the culturally different client and how such experiences impact on the counseling relationship and therapeutic process. Underlying values and assumptions associated with widely used traditional counseling interventions and their appropriateness will also be explored. Traditional and non-traditional culturally competent counseling approaches will also be discussed. The course is to heighten student's critical consciousness and foster a social justice orientation. (Field experience required)

The practicum field experience provides the opportunity to integrate theory and practice. Students will be placed in a variety of agencies where counselors are needed such as schools, hospital programs, courts, prison compounds, Gender and Social Security Protection, Non-governmental Organization etcetera. The field exercise will take place during the Fourth year of the program. The students must have completed a minimum of 125 credit hours course both in class and site with supervision by a licensed professional in the field. Students will meet weekly on campus with a faculty clinical supervisor to discuss their experiences in a seminar format with other graduate students/interns. All first year and third year courses are prerequisite for practicum and internship students. Students must have at least 3.0 grade.

The course will be based on counseling assessment. It will explore the use of psychological testing (test batteries), including purposes, methods and procedures in using the psychological testing. An emphasis will be laid on analysis, evaluation and administration of various educational and psychological instruments. Students will also learn the assessment of interest, career, personality, intelligent, achievement, and aptitude. In addition, students will learn about the underpinnings of assessment as well as statistical analysis of such assessment, including basic concepts like measures of central tendency, validity, reliability, norm and criterion referenced tests. It will also cover, administration, scoring and interpreting test results which will also be considered along with the writing of assessment reports.

The purpose of this course is to describe methods of organization and administration in counseling. It will include the study of various components in a comprehensive, developmental school counseling program. It will include understanding professional roles and functions, historical and future trends, accountability and professional organizations. It will include a systematic analysis of the entire range of services provided in schools as well as those provided in the industry and community. It is also designed to equip students with necessary knowledge and skill relative to developing and organizing programs. School leadership, program development, universal change, student/family diversity, special populations, legal and ethical considerations, consultation, collaboration, use of technical resources, accountability and practical organizational and time management strategies will be examined and discussed.

This course is an introduction to drawing for engineering students especially those without previous drawing or drafting experience. It covers the fundamentals of drafting using the manual system approach as compared to computer-aided drawing. It covers 2D drawing and views, lettering, dimensional diagrams, free sketching and the use of various drawing scales.

The course is a follow up to geng201. It starts by introducing students to computer aided drawing using AutoCAD software. This first series reviews basic concepts of GEN201, using the computer drawing process, such as 2D drawings, lettering formats, dimensional diagrams, scaling, sketching and geometrical constructions logic, orthographic projections, isometric drawing, sectional views and dimensioning. Details are added using examples from civil, electrical or mechanical engineering design process.

This course covers an introduction to engineering education and the engineering profession; engineering ethics, and basic concepts and tools are presented. It provides an over view of the engineering problem solving and design process. Techniques and methods used in defining and solving engineering problems are reviewed also design methodologies including analysis, synthesis and creative thinking are presented. Finally, the students are provided an opportunity to complete an engineering design project.

This course emphasizes computer aided design and simulation of electrical circuits and system. It introduces computer- aided design techniques using MatLab, Simulink or PSpice software. Students develop various electrical, mechanical, civil and cybersecurity system models and apply computer aided design techniques to solve them.

This course is an upgrade from PHYS 101. It stresses applications of physical principles in the fields of industry and engineering technology, precision measurement, properties of matter-stress and strain, Young modulus , hydrostatics and hydraulics, laws of mechanics, introduction to vectors analysis, resultant, conditions of equilibrium of forces, statics of structures, trusses; reactions friction, center of gravity, moment of inertial, dynamics, force and motion, rotary motion, energy and momentum, conservation laws.

This course continues on the foundation of phys201. It covers topics such as: Matter, Heat and Temperature (Temperature scales and conversions); Gas laws and general gas equations are learnt by students. Heat and heat measurement, heat energy, internal energy, reversibility and irreversibility, heat and energy relationship. Heat Capacity, specific heat capacity, Thermal expansion. Laws of thermodynamics and entropy, Heat flow in matters (Convection, Conduction and Radiation); Waves and electromagnetic energy. Students will be introduce to the study of electromagnetic phenomena; including electrostatics; capacitors; resistors and direct-current circuits; magnetism and magnetic induction; RC, LR, and LRC circuits; waves; electromagnetic waves; and radiation; They will then be introduced to properties and propagations of sound, wave motion, light,, electromagnetic spectrum, the nature of light, speed, emission, absorption of light, reflection, refraction, and diffraction of light.

The course Engineering Mechanics, Statics is the fundamental of engineering mechanics which deals with forces acting on rigid bodies at rest covering coplanar and non-coplanar forces, concurrent and concurrent forces, friction forces, centroid and moment of inertia. The course focus on finding forces for a variety of force systems, as well as analysing forces acting on bodies to find the reacting forces supporting those bodies. Students will develop critical thinking skills necessary to formulate appropriate approaches to problem solutions.
This course is a continuation of geng301. Principal topics are kinematics and kinetics of particles and rigid bodies of finite size in motion. Techniques of vector mathematics are employed

This course covers an overview of engineering management and economic. It focuses on practical analytical skills needed by all engineers to ensure that their various designed interventions are economically prudent and environmentally friendly (ex: elements of projects management, cost benefit analysis, quantity analysis, financial analysis, etc.) ...elements of entrepreneurship training will be included.

Strength of Materials is the foundation for Engineering design courses. The course covers material behavior, stresses, strains and deformations with simple applications in engineering designs. Topics include elastic and elastic-plastic behavior, plane stress and strain; constitutive relationships, principal stress and strain; failure criteria; bending and shearing stresses in beams; Mohr's circle; Euler buckling; short and long columns; torsion of solid and hollow circular sections. Introduction to statically indeterminacy and simple redundant structures.

This course introduces the students to the fundamentals of materials Science as a functional area in Material Science and Engineering. It is a three-hour credit course which primarily discusses the principles, concepts and basic problems of material structure, processing and application. It covers topics such as Introduction to Material science and Engineering, Atomic Structure, Inter atomic Bonding and Structure of Crystalline Solids, Properties of Materials, Heat treatment of metals etc.

This is the first of a two-part course which covers the study of the first and second laws of thermodynamics and their application to energy transformations during various processes. Property relations are studied for pure substances, ideal gases, mixture of ideal gases, and atmospheric air. Steam power cycles, refrigeration cycles, spark-ignition and compression-ignition engines, and turbine cycles are evaluated to determine performance parameters and energy efficiencies.

This course starts with an introduction to electrical engineering world and to the understanding of currents, voltage, resistance and power; introduction to safety regulations and rules. It continues with the study of electrical passive components with emphasis on their applications to in AC and DC networks. Transformers, simple R. C and R.L.C circuits, Blue print reading and wire colour coding for domestic and industrial installations. Introduction to semi-conductor theory, devices and circuits. Electrical experiments in the laboratory.

This course introduces students who are proficient with 2D commands in the AutoCAD software to the concept and methods of 3D modelling. The training guide provides thorough grounding in the fundamentals of 3D and explores the main features of 3D Modelling workspace in the AutoCad software. Topic include 3D Foundation, Simple Solids, Working with the user Coordinate System, Creating Solids and Surfaces from 2D Objects, Modifying in 3D Space, Refining the View, Point Clouds, Visualization; Working Drawings of engineering design from 3D Models.

The course introduces the language of research, ethical principles and challenges, and the elements of the research process within quantitative, qualitative, and mixed methods approaches. Participants will use these theoretical underpinnings to begin to critically review literature relevant to their field or interests and determine how research findings are useful in informing their understanding of their environment (work, social, local, global). Students will learn how to identify problems to study, develop hypotheses and research questions, specify independent and dependent variables, check for the validity and reliability of studies and design research projects. You will be exposed to the broad range of designs used in communication research from laboratory and field experiments, surveys, content analysis, focus groups and in-depth interviewing.

involved with capital investments for materials, equipment and labor. The techniques of economic analysis may be used to assist in determining the best alternative. It introduces economic terminologies; concept of basic cash flow diagrams, economic factors and their use, nominal and effective interest rates, continuous compounding, the use of multiple economic factors in engineering, present-worth and capitalized cost evaluation, replacement analysis and e-bonds; inflation and cost estimation, capital rationing under budget constraints; and engineering decision making for large capital investment.

This is an introductory course in Geography with emphasis on understanding Geography as a science. The scope of the course covers the need and purpose of Geography, Geography as the study of area differentiation. The course also deals with the laws, description and explanation of the principle of geography. Students will also learn the dualism of physical and human geography, regional constraints, systematic procedures, challenges and opportunities in geography.

This course is a continuation of Geog. 101. It explores in details scientific method and its application in Geography. It therefore deals with the structuring of geographic relationships, formulation, classification, resource appraisal, location and uses of land, regional geography, theory of regions, and contemporary trends in geography. Principles and Methods of Map Analysis I. The course is designed to introduce students to the principles and methods of map making and the skills for interpreting features and extracting information from maps. The course focuses on concepts and procedures used in discovering and applying relationships within and among maps. Emphasis is laid on fundamental principles, concepts, techniques and methods of cartography. Thus, the course will include practical sessions on map interpretation and measurements. The course further intends to introduce students to analysis of elementary Land Surveying which covers introduction to surveying, process and types of surveying, and classification of surveying because of purpose and types, and because of errors.

course_title: Principles and Methods of Map Analysis I
description: The course is designed to introduce students to the principles and methods of map making and the skills for interpreting features and extracting information from maps. The course focuses on concepts and procedures used in discovering and applying relationships within and among maps. Emphasis is laid on fundamental principles, concepts, techniques and methods of cartography. Thus, the course will include practical sessions on map interpretation and measurements. Students will be introduced to the Global Positioning System (GPS) and its practical use as modern equipment for data collection.

This course is designed to improve the students' knowledge on the various types of agricultural practices in Africa and the world at large. It also gives brief idea on irrigation agriculture, bush fallowing, plantation agriculture, fruit and pastoral farming, which include methods and problems facing agriculture in Africa. It also provides insight on how agriculture has become the backbone for economic development.

course_title: Principles and Methods of Statistical Geography for Teachers
description: This course covers the statistical analysis of geographic data and techniques for geographic analysis. No prior statistical knowledge is assumed, and basic statistical concepts are covered. This is an introductory course in the fundamental techniques of geographical data acquisition, analysis, interpretation and presentation. This course provides students a common foundation in both physical and human geography research techniques. It further provides grounding for further research-based courses, particularly 306 Education statistics.

course_title: Principles and Methods of Map Analysis II
description: This course is the continuation of geog201: Principles and Methods of Map Analysis I. The course is designed to introduce students to the principles and methods of map making and the skills for interpreting features and extracting information from maps. The course focuses on concepts and procedures used in discovering and applying relationships within and among maps. Emphasis is laid on fundamental principles, concepts, techniques and methods of cartography. Thus, the course will include practical sessions on map interpretation and measurements. The course further intends to introduce students to analysis of elementary Land Surveying which covers introduction to surveying, process and types of surveying, and classification of surveying because of purpose and types, and because of errors.

This course explores the science of geomorphology, the study of Earth's landforms and the processes that shape them. Students will investigate the physical and chemical processes that create and modify landscapes over time. Through a combination of lectures, laboratory work, field trips, and project-based learning, students will gain a comprehensive understanding of the dynamic interactions between the Earth's surface and the environmental forces acting upon it. Topics such as Introduction to Geomorphology, Tectonic Processes, Weathering and Erosion, Fluvial Processes, Glacial Geomorphology, Aeolian Processes, Coastal Geomorphology, Karst Landscapes, Landform Analysis and Mapping, and Human Impact on Landscapes will be explored.

This course is designed to acquaint students with the physical features of Africa, including the human aspects of geography. As physical features varied from within regions and from regions to the activities of the inhabitants who imprint on the land.

This course focuses on the dynamics of population issues, population concept such as over population, under population, optimum population and population density. It considers the advantages and disadvantages of each concept. The course also recognizes the positive and negative roles that migration play. It further deals with the side effect of population growth and some recommended policy actions.

course_title: Environmental Geography and Ecology for Teachers
description: This course is designed to give students a better knowledge and understanding about their environment. It deals with environmental interaction, spheres of environment, the ecosystem, environmental resources and environmental hazard such as soil erosion, desert encroachment, flooding, and control mechanism of the environmental hazard. Additionally, it deals with the various types of environmental problems such as air, land and water pollution, their effects and control.

The purpose of this course is to provide an integrated introduction to climatology and meteorology. The course makes extensive use of maps and graphs. It emphasises the understanding of the elements of climate and the characteristics of the atmosphere such as temperature, precipitation, speed of wind and direction, humidity, radiation, sunshine, cloudiness and evaporation.

This course focuses on the dynamics of population issues, population concept such as over population, under population, optimum population and population density. It considers the advantages and disadvantages of each concept. The course also recognizes the positive and negative roles that migration plays. It further deals with the side effect of population growth and some recommended policy actions.

This course examines the importance of space in geography, forms of interactions, relationship and explanation in geography, location and land-use patterns, spatial diffusion processes and their impact on land/man relationships.

This is an introductory aspect of Geographic information system designed in getting maps into the computer. It involves the use of a powerful set of tools for storing and retrieving at will, transforming and displaying spatial data from the real world for a particular set of purpose. It is a database system.

This course focuses primarily on the dynamics of urban settlement in Liberia and the World as a whole. It also deals with course of high population growth in many urban areas in the world. Additionally, it deals with increase problems of urbanization which includes high demand for goods and services, social amenities, social vices or crime, high cost of living, environmental pollution, unemployment, traffic and housing congestion and so on.

course_title: Methods of Teaching Geography in Secondary Schools
description: The course focuses mainly on the pedagogical instruction of Geography subjects among high school teachers, whereby they will be exposed to rudiments of how best to electively impart the knowledge or lessons of Geography to their pupils. Understandably, college education departments focus on producing teachers, which explains why they must be equipped with teaching techniques that should help to achieve desired results in class with their pupils. To this end, student-teachers will be exposed to geography methodologies and techniques of conveying the subject to students for better understanding.

This seminar course engages students in the exploration of geographical topics through research, discussions, and presentations. Students analyze contemporary global and environmental issues, including climate change, urbanization, and sustainable development. Emphasis is placed on critical thinking, geographic inquiry, and the real-world application of geographic concepts.

This course studies in detail both physical features of Liberia and the various land use patterns that are related to these physical features. Its physical features are varied from regions to regions, even within regions. It is necessary for students to study these differences as they relate to the activities of the inhabitants of a particular region.

This course provides students with hands-on experience in geography-related field teaching, such as urban planning, environmental management, or GIS mapping. Through supervised internships, students apply geographic concepts in the classroom through teaching and presentations, develop analytical and problem-solving skills, and explore career opportunities. The course includes mentor guidance, reflective discussions, and a final presentation or report on the internship experience.

course_title: Economic Geography of Liberia for Teachers
description: This course studies the trend of economic development in Liberia. It will aid students to identify and locate natural resources such as diamond, gold, iron ore etc. The economic values of other commodities such as rubber, coffee and cocoa and the impact of mining activities in different parts of the country will also be highlighted. Also mentioned in this course is the importance of transportation means such as roads, railways, rivers and the likes to the economic growth of the country.

Glebo 101 introduces students to basic structures of the Glebo language. The aim is to help students develop interest and appreciation of the Glebo language and culture. It helps in achieving written and oral proficiency in Glebo.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

This course is designed to give first year students an in-depth look at the major themes and events in Liberian history and historiography. It examines the time period from the early population migration during the 15th century through the colonial and independence eras until the end of the cold War and the beginning of the Civil War in 1990. The course focuses on inter-sectoral cooperation, conflict resolution within the written record over time as well as Liberia's place within regional and world history. It presents a survey of Liberian history past to present. It traces the development and growth of Liberia's political system, major institutions, and its role in African and world affairs. It also explores the social, cultural, religious and major events that shaped how the people live today. Students are provided the opportunity to learn to critically discuss the relevance of these issues.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

This course is designed to give students an in-depth look at the major themes and events in World History. It covers important events, including the development of Western, African, and classical Chinese and Indian civilizations while focusing on the role of religion, technology, and global ideologies of the last three thousand years of human history.

This course is designed to help students develop the skills necessary for higher mathematics courses. It aims to foster appreciation of the logical structure of mathematics and to help students think critically and analytically. Topics include Real Number System, Polynomials, Laws of Exponents, Radicals, Linear Equations, Inequalities, Quadratic Equations, Functions, Linear Equations, Matrices, Determinants, Sequences and Series. A review of the operations of Polynomial, Fractions and Radicals and Special Products and Factoring is also included.

This course is primarily intended to prepare the students to gain knowledge into how mathematics is applied in the real world and to provide them proper background in mathematics and other physical sciences. It aims to create in the students an appreciation of the logical structure of mathematics and to develop a feeling of mathematical thought and processes that would lead them to acquire a skill that will be of inestimable value in their future work. This course covers trigonometric and circular functions, logarithms, identities, equations, and solution of right and oblique triangles.

This course is designed to develop the necessary math skills and understanding for entry into a calculus course at the university; with emphasis on those elementary functions which are of fundamental interest in calculus. These include functions and relations, polynomial functions, rational functions, exponential and logarithmic functions and the trigonometric functions. Special attention to graphing.

Calculus I is traditionally the first calculus level course taken by students in chemistry, mathematics, physics, and engineering. For some students, it is the only exposure they receive to the important mathematical concepts of the derivative and the integral. For others who will take additional mathematics, Calculus I provides the essential foundation for advanced mathematics. For most, it offers the first glimpse of the real power and excitement of mathematics. The topics covered in this course include: concepts of function, limits and continuity, differentiation of all functions, fundamental theorem of calculus, rates, approximations and extrema problems and applications of the derivative.

This course is the continuation of math201 and it focuses on integral calculus. It deals with definite and indefinite integrals and their properties. Topics include definite integrals and areas, the Fundamental Theorems of Calculus, substitution, integration by parts, other methods of integration, numerical techniques, computation of volumes, arc length, average of a function, applications (to physics, engineering, and probability), separable differential equations, exponential growth, infinite series, and Taylor series.

This is an introductory course to linear algebra and their applications. It covers matrix theory and linear algebra, emphasizing topics that are useful in other disciplines. Linear algebra is a branch of mathematics that studies systems of linear equations and the properties of matrices. The concepts of linear algebra are extremely useful in physics, economics and social sciences, natural sciences, and engineering. Topics included are: fundamental theory of matrices, determinants and curve fitting, vector spaces, orthogonality and least squares, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and linear transformations.

This is an introductory course designed to improve and strengthen the computational skills of prospective teachers with the basic concepts of arithmetic, number theory, set theory and symbolic logic. Topics include logic, sets, numeration systems, arithmetic in non-decimal systems, system of integers, place value, Roman numerals, number theory, laws of exponents, scientific notation, and Mathematical reasoning. Additional topics include an introduction to algebraic concepts such as relations and functions. There will be a strong emphasis on critical thinking, problem-solving, understanding concepts and their application.

This course provides the fundamental principles of Mathematics necessary in understanding the concepts of Mathematical Analysis. It covers in depth knowledge in Analytic Geometry. Its foremost objective is to help the students develop a sound understanding of the fundamental concepts of Analytic Geometry and a thorough appreciation of its applications. Topics include Cartesian plane, distance and divisions of segments, slopes, straight line, vectors in a plane, the conic sections, and the transformation of coordinates and curve sketching.

This course studies elementary functions: Linear, absolute value, polynomial, rational, square root, exponential, and logarithmic functions. The course reinforces and extends the student's numerical algebraic and graphical understanding of equations and inequalities, while preparing the student for the study of calculus by emphasizing the concept of function and function notation. Functions are presented as models for natural phenomena whenever possible, connecting the abstract, graphical and numerical understanding of a function to concrete applications.

This course deals with the collection, organization, presentation, and analysis of data. It provides students statistical concepts, principles, and method on topics regarding descriptive and inferential statistics such as: frequency distribution and graphs; measure of central tendency and other positions; measure of variation; probability and counting techniques; test of hypothesis; correlation; and regression.

This course is intended for students looking to create a solid algebraic foundation of fundamental mathematical concepts from which to take more advanced courses that use concepts from precalculus, calculus, probability, and statistics. This course will help solidify your computational methods, review algebraic formulas and properties, and apply these concepts model real world situations. This course is for any student who will use algebraic skills in future mathematics courses. Topics include: the real numbers, equalities, inequalities, and equations, graphs, coordinate geometry of straight lines, relations and functions, Simultaneous linear equations, matrices and determinants.

The course is a continuation of math202. It extends the tools and techniques of single variable calculus to functions of several variables and applications in three-dimensional space and beyond. It introduces the theory and application of partial differentiations, multiple integrals, and vector calculus. Other topics are parametric equations, polar coordinates, derivatives and integrals of vector-valued functions, functions of several variables and line integrals. Multivariable calculus finds application in economics, engineering, physics, business, and any field of study where problems involve more than one input variable.

This course focuses on ordinary differential equations (ODEs). It focuses primarily on techniques for finding explicit solutions to linear ODEs. The course will demonstrate the usefulness of ordinary differential equations for modelling physical and other phenomena. Complementary mathematical approaches for their solution will be presented including analytical methods, graphical analysis and numerical techniques. Topics include first order ordinary differential equations, linear differential equations, linear equations with constant coefficients, nonhomogeneous equations, undetermined coefficients and variation of parameters, linear systems of equations; the existence and uniqueness of solutions.

This course is intended for science, mathematics, and engineering students, and anyone needing multivariable calculus. The topics covered include plane vector, polar coordinates, vector and parametric equations, solid analytic geometry, partial differentiation, multiple integration, and introduction to vector calculus.

The focus of the course is on the use and interpretation of statistical procedures used with quantitative methods of research and evaluation. This course aims to give clear and comprehensive view of statistics in relation to studies and research, thus, it will provide tools used for statistical analysis and decision making as applied to educational research setting.

A survey of the history of mathematics from the beginnings through the 19th century, with special attention to primary sources and to the interactions between culture and mathematics. It includes topics on Mesopotamian, Egyptian, and Greek mathematics; non-European mathematics; early Renaissance mathematics; the birth and flowering of calculus; the beginnings of probability theory; and the origin of non-euclidean geometry and the modern concept of number.

This is an introductory course in probability covering axiomatic probability space, discrete and continuous random variables, special distributions, mathematical expectation, conditional probability and independence, multivariate distributions, Laws of Large Numbers, and the Central Limit Theorem.

This is an undergraduate course in Geometry. This course requires students to focus on logical proof and critical thinking when solving problems or evaluating arguments. It enhances the knowledge of the students on points, segments, triangles, polygons, circles, solid figures, and their associated relationships. Inductive and deductive thinking skills are utilized in problem-solving situations, and applications to the real world. It also emphasizes writing proofs to show properties of geometric figures. Basic constructions will also be stressed.

This is the continuation of Modern Geometry I. It includes discussions on spherical geometry, non-Euclidean geometry, projective geometry and other related topics. A number of exercises of different level of difficulty will strengthen students' problem-solving abilities and geometrical intuition. The study of Solid Geometry includes space geometry: basic elements of space and their relations, oblique projection, perpendicular, angle, distance, prism, cylinder, cone, sphere, cross section, polyhedron, affine and projective geometries, Möbius transformations, and hyperbolic geometry.

This introductory course covers error analysis, solutions of linear and nonlinear equations and linear systems, interpolating polynomials, numerical differentiation and integration, numerical approximations of eigenvalues, and numerical solutions of ordinary differential equations.

Methods for first-order differential equations: Taylor's method, Euler methods, Runge-Kutta methods, multi-step methods. Methods for higher-order differential equations: Taylor's, Euler and Runge-Kutta methods.

This course enables students to review articles in scientific journals and present research reports. The goal is for students to know how to research for a topic, prepare and present a research paper, and, at the same time, be an active and critical listener to technical reports presented by others.

This course exposes students to various fields of mathematics through presentations by department of mathematics faculty members on their research or on a mathematical topic on which the faculty members would be willing to supervise a senior project. Class time is also devoted to topics concerned with the senior project, including mathematical writing and presentation, searching mathematical literature, bibliographic citation and style in mathematics. The goal is to help students arrive at a proposal by providing them a range of lively topics in mathematics from which they could choose a suitable topic for their senior thesis, preparing students to communicate mathematics effectively in writing and speaking, providing basic instruction in mathematical typesetting software for writing projects and giving presentations.

Introductory calculus designed for students in health and biological sciences. It is intended to develop the students' understanding of the concepts of mathematics in sciences and to provide experience with its methods and applications. This course will introduce students to the fundamentals of differential and integral calculus. The successful student will develop a thorough understanding of single variable calculus, will appreciate the utility of calculus as a tool to analyze functions that naturally arise in the study of biological systems, and will be able to place these systems in the biological world. This course covers most of the material included in a traditional calculus. The main difference is that the students will be able to apply mathematics in biological sciences. Topics covered are Limits and Continuity, Laws of Differentiation, Applications of Derivatives, Implicit Differentiation, Integrals, Definite Integrals, Logarithmic and Exponential Functions, Partial Differentiation, Applications of Partials Derivatives, Multiple Integration and their Applications.

This course intends to expose students majoring in mathematics to the world of pure mathematics and to build among them a strong foundation on principles and theories on abstract algebra. This course covers groups, subgroups, cyclic groups, permutation groups, abelian groups, normal subgroups, quotient groups and homomorphisms and isomorphism theorems, rings, integral domains, fields, ring homomorphisms, ideals, and field of quotients. It also includes rings of polynomials, fundamental theorem of field theory, extension fields, algebraic extensions, finite fields, geometric constructions, fundamental theorem of Galois theory and illustrations of Galois theory. Moreover, students gain in-depth knowledge on the importance of constructing mathematically correct and concise proofs.

This course is designed to develop the mathematical strengths of the students who are majoring mathematics. Number Theory is the study of numbers and their properties with focus on the set of positive integers. Students will also learn the concepts on the properties of integers, divisibility, algorithm, linear Diophantine equation, congruences, Chinese Remainder Theorem, prime numbers, special functions such as number theoretic, multiplicative, mobius and bracket functions, and quadratic congruences.

This course focuses on the use and interpretation of statistical procedures in quantitative methods of research. It aims to give clear and comprehensive view of statistics in relation to thesis writing. It provides tools used for statistical analysis and decision making as applied to research. Topics include both parametric and non-parametric tests to draw conclusions about a population; test concerning means, proportions, analysis of variance, correlation, regression, chi-square, Wilcoxon Rank Sum Test, Sign-test, Mann-Whitney test, and Kruskal-Wallis test.

This course covers vectors in the plane and in space, vector-valued functions, and the calculus of vector fields. It also includes with Vector mechanics: Statics and Dynamics. Velocity, momentum and moments. Equilibrium and conservation laws. Introduction to vector-valued functions. Differentiation of vector-valued functions. Cartesian tensors and their transformations. Coordinate-free definitions of gradient, curl and divergence. Scalar and vector potential. Notion of orthogonal curvilinear coordinates and bases.

This course is an introduction to topology. It includes topics fundamental to modern analysis and geometry like topological spaces and continuous functions, connectedness, compactness, countability axioms, and separation axioms.

This course is designed to prepare students majoring in mathematics, computer science and engineering for a background in abstraction, notation and critical thinking. It develops students reasoning and proving skills. Topics include: logic, relations, functions, basic set theory, countability and counting arguments, proof techniques, mathematical induction, graph theory, combinatorics, discrete probability, recursion, recurrence relations, elementary number theory and graph theory.

This course focuses on graph theoretical notions and problems, and the use of algorithms, both in the mathematical theory of graphs and its applications. Moreover, the basic theory of graphs of different kinds is developed in detail, especially trees and bipartite graphs. Algorithms that totally or partly solve graph theoretical problems are also presented. Topics include the theory for matchings and Hall's theorem, spanning trees, Menger's theorem, the theory of vertex and edge colouring, Brooks' theorem, Vizing's theorem and an introduction to matroid theory.

This course involves a study of the algebra of complex numbers, analytic functions, elementary complex functions, complex integration, and the residue theorem and its applications. The aim of the course is to teach the principal techniques and methods of analytic function theory. It deals with complex numbers and topology of the complex plane, analytic and harmonic functions, Cauchy-Riemann equations, complex integration, Cauchy's integral formula, power series and Laurent series, zeros and singularities, residue theory with the Cauchy residue theorem, the argument principle and conformal mappings.

course_title: Methods of Teaching Mathematics in Secondary Schools
description: This course is designed to explore various methods, skills, and strategies for teaching mathematics in the secondary classroom. The student through explorations of epistemological foundations of mathematics algorithm as both logical discipline and physical sciences and its pedagogical and philosophical underpinnings, develop a point of view in mathematics teaching. The course offers a review of Liberian viz-a-viz West African Senior Secondary Certificate Examination (WASSCE) mathematics curricula, strategies for teaching mathematics with emphasis on planning using backward design and the several instructional models, and locally available resources for instruction. Performance or Project-based assessment and evaluation of learning is emphasized. Field Experience Required.

math410 is a senior-level capstone seminar that focuses on advanced mathematical topics through independent research, student presentations, and discussions. The course enhances problem-solving skills, mathematical communication, and critical thinking while fostering collaboration and professional development. Students will explore significant mathematical concepts across various fields, present their findings, and engage in meaningful discourse to deepen their understanding and appreciation of mathematics.

Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

This course introduces the fundamentals and principles of kinematics and kinetic; degree of freedom, four bar linkage synthesis, position, velocity, and acceleration as applied in the analyses of machinery, and gear linkages of Machines are also covered.

This is the first part of a two-part lab course which is the backbone of the real industrial environment which helps to develop and enhance relevant technical hand skills required by the technician working in the various engineering industries and workshops. This course intends to impart basic know-how of various hand tools and their use in different sections of manufacturing. Irrespective of branch, the use of workshop practices in day to day industrial as well domestic life helps to dissolve the problems.

This course introduces the principles of internal and external energy analyses, and the mechanisms of heat transfer based on significant temperature difference between systems and bodies involved. The concept of heat transfer by Conduction, Convection, Radiation and the combination of one or more modes are taught. Other practical engineering applications such as boilers, heat exchangers, among others are discussed and analyzed.

This course discusses processes involved in transforming raw materials to relevant engineering components for specific engineering applications. Material processing and conditioning such asraw and bulk material preparation, forging, forming, machining, heat treatment etc. Other processes such as welding and powder metallurgy are also discussed. The working principles and operations of manufacturing machineries and basic equipment are also discussed.

This is the second part of a two-part lab course which is the backbone of the real industrial environment. The workshop experiences would help to build the understanding of the complexity of the industrial job, along with time and skills requirements of the job. Workshop curricula build the hands-on experiences which would help to learn manufacturing processes and production technology courses in successive semesters.

This course will be taken distinctly over a three-month period, during which students will be placed in engineering industries for exposure to engineering workplace and field experiences. Students will be introduced to managerial and standard operation procedures, engineering standards and codes of operations. Interns will be expected to work along with professional Engineers on sites and understand hands-on procedures and other significant details in the day-to-day responsibility of engineers. Interns will be expected to prepare and present reports on their experiences from the industry.

This is the second of a two-part course presents the thermodynamic analysis of steam plant internal combustion engine and other air breathing marine power devices, including gas turbines and air compressors. The course is based on the fundamental thermodynamic concepts and analysis techniques learnt in Thermodynamics class

This course Introduces the basic nature of fluids and instrumentation linked to fluid from an engineering perspective: Fluid static, hydrostatics pressure, flotation hydrometers, barometers, fluid dynamics without friction. Bernoulli's principle, venturi-meters, orifices, weirs notches. Loss of head, hydraulic structures and equipment, cavitation. Hydrology, rainfall, run-off underground water, flow rivers and streams. Laminar and turbulent flows. Hydraulic machines their selection and performance, drainage and plumbing.

This course extends the concepts of continuum, continuity and momentum balances for laminar and turbulent flow with applications and design problems and fluid kinematics (Bernoulli's equation, Langrangian method and Eulerian method, types of fluid flow and types of flow lines). Concepts of compressible and incompressible fluids, modeling and boundary layers are also covered.

This is the first of a two-part course which introduces physical engineering systems with multiple interactive parts working together with a common input instruction for a specific output response. The characteristics of dynamic systems are analyzed, mathematical models are derived based on the system components and characteristic interaction, block diagrams and system loops of information and feedbacks are also introduced for system analyses.

This is the second of a two-part course which begins with the analyses of the system responses in both frequency and time domains, various orders of systems damping characteristics, stability, and errors, among others. Dynamic systems are designed, controlled and analyzed base on different systems characteristics.

This is the first of a two-part course which introduces to hands-on activities and all relevant guidelines and ethics for technical laboratory and workshop procedures. The principles of machinery operations, safety, standard operation procedures and technical workplace regulations.

This is the second of a two-part course which introduces to hands-on activities and all relevant guidelines and ethics for technical laboratory and workshop procedures. The principles of machinery operations, safety, standard operation procedures and technical workplace regulations.

This is the first of a two-part course which introduces students to the theory and practice of machine design as a functional area in mechanical engineering design, by primarily discussing the principles, concepts and basic problems of design of machine elements. The course will review critical materials properties in design, such as stress, fatigue, strength, coefficient of thermal expansion, etc.

course_title: Machine Design 11 (Analysis and Design of Machine Components)
description: This the second part of a two-part course, which focuses on the theory and practice of machine design as a functional area in mechanical engineering design. It primarily discusses the principles, concepts and basic problems of design of machine elements and covers topics such as Energy storing elements and engine components, clutch, brake, bearing and lubrication and pressure vessels design.

This course introduces the theory and practice of CAD/CAM as a functional area in the manufacturing of engineering components. It primarily discusses the principles, concepts and basic problems of computer aided design and manufacturing. It covers topics such as Numerical Control (NC) and programming, Group Technology, computer aided process planning, computer aided quality control and flexible manufacturing system.

This course introduces the students to the theory and practice of production management as a functional area in production/Industrial engineering. It primarily discusses the principles, concepts and basic problems affecting the manufacturing and non-manufacturing firms. It also includes the methods, strategies and application of various economical and mathematical tools in solving the production and operation related problems. It covers the two major areas of production and operations management and topics such as Work study, Plant location and Layout, Scheduling, inventory management etc

course_title: Internal Combustion Engine and Automotive Power Systems
description: This course covers the analyses of air compression and combustion processes of engines. Other concepts on air capacity, engine vibration, kinematics and dynamics of the crank mechanism, air compression cycles, combustion, petroleum and alternative fuels, engine electronics and fuel cells are explored. Automotive emissions, government standards, instrumentation, and laboratory procedures are emphasized.

volume and redirecting the flow in order to transmit power for control and work. The concept of hydraulics circuits followed by the sizing and control of hydraulic cylinders and motors, prime movers, Valves, circuit tracing and component recognition are emphasized. The application of pressured air in pneumatic component sizing and ladder logic and other relevant components are introduced.

This is the first of a two-part course which introduces senior students the fundamentals of engineering solution-based projects. It practically involves students in basic research and problem identification and justification, analysis of probable solutions, and conceptual solutions based on considerable algorithms, equations, design concepts, and material selections to meet performance constrains for the achievement of the project objectives.

This is the second of a two-part course which introduces students to engineering solution-based project implementation for realization of algorithms, equations and design by physically producing scalable prototypes and conducting testing for performance evaluation. Students will be required to analyze results, present final project report and attend a project defense.

This is the first of a two-part course which introduces students to practical specialized interest-based engineering topics and skills developments in exploring knowledge and solutions for gaps in the currently available scientific and engineering approach. It enhances students' ability to undertake Senior Project MENG 505.

This is the second of a two-part course which introduces students to the analysis and evaluation of new solutions for gaps found in existing knowledge and engineering solutions. It also promotes the need to realize ideals through physical designs, simulations and real engineering application, considering internal standards, and others engineering codes and regulations. This course helps students fulfil the requirement of meng506.

This course introduces students to the exploration of possible sustainable energy sources to meet global and local energy demands. It considers the mechanism of energy generations, structural design parameters of machineries, operations and power output calculations.

course_title: Heating, Ventilation and Air Conditioning System
description: The course focuses on mechanism applied and technology employed to regulate the temperature and air quality in a defined space. Systems operating on hydronics and other gases base on the refrigeration cycle are considered for heating, and Mechanical ventilation. Designs of systems and equipment for efficient heat exchange, the respective energy sources and international standards n HVAC are covered.

This course introduces students to the approach of segmenting and subdividing engineering problems and focus elements into smaller parts for end-to-end boundary conditions consideration for effective calculations. Specific phenomena and scientific principles are considered to analyze the distribution of temperature, vibration, and deformation among other conditions that affect the structural integrity of engineering components or systems. Students will also be introduced to the use of engineering software to conduct finite element analyses (FEA) and predict the performance of engineering materials subjected to different types of loads.

This an advanced course on the theory and practice of production management as a functional area in production/Industrial engineering. It discusses the principles, concepts and problems affecting the manufacturing and non-manufacturing firms. It also includes the methods, strategies and application of various economical and mathematical tools in solving the production and operation related problems. It covers the two major areas of production and operations management and topics such as Work study, Plant location and Layout, Scheduling, inventory management etc.

This course introduces students to concepts of leadership and management for application in midwifery practice settings. Principles of time management, leadership styles, budgeting, staffing patterns, personnel evaluations, delegation, the steps of discipline, and healthcare accreditation criteria are discussed and analyzed across diverse practice settings. Students use a variety of professional tools such as in-class forums to debate relevant healthcare issues, the resume, a portfolio, and a template for a project proposal for enhancement of professional development. The clinical part focuses on the student developing their leadership and management style within the midwifery clinical setting. Participation in this clinical course will support competency development in the coordination of patient-centered care, implementation of evidence-based care, interprofessional team collaboration for prioritized clinical decision-making, use of ethics and respect principles in all aspects of midwifery practice, technical skill acquisition dependent on clinical setting, regulatory knowledge related to the scope of practice, and budgetary and health policy impact in the delivery of care to the client seeking midwifery services.

This course is designed to examine current issues in midwifery practice and the influence of societal/environmental trends on the practice of the profession. Emphasis will be placed on the historical, philosophical, ethical, and political aspects of the midwifery profession. These major areas are considered within the context of the global community and macro and micro society with special emphasis on midwifery issues in Liberia.

This course will focus on human development from fertilization to the 8th week of pregnancy. After mastering normal development including implantation, gastrulation, and organogenesis the focus will shift to abnormal development, i.e. congenital anomalies. The final unit will focus on genetic disorders that may affect the care and management provided by the midwife during pregnancy and throughout the lifespan. Students will learn effective genetic and preconception counseling strategies that can be utilized by the midwife.

This is part one of a two-part course. The course explores the steps of the research process, and the framework for critiquing qualitative and quantitative nursing research articles will be presented. Students are expected to develop the ability to read and analyze various midwifery and/or health research articles critically. Students will conduct a review of the literature and critique selections of research articles related to midwifery and healthcare practices. Students are expected to develop the ability to apply research findings to midwifery practice to ensure evidence-based practice. The opportunity to complete a literature review of research articles related to midwifery and to formulate research problems applying the elements of the research process will be provided. Ethical issues involved in midwifery research and various theoretical frameworks for midwifery research will be explored. The midwife's role as a member of a research team and his or her responsibility to the profession in conducting and disseminating midwifery and health-related research will be explored and discussed.

This course reviews the management process as it is applied to the pathologies of the female and male reproductive systems that may occur throughout the lifespan. Emphasis will be placed on both the cultural context in which these conditions occur as well as evidence-based management options, documentation, and shared decision-making. Students will spend one day per week in the clinical setting applying the knowledge and skills being attained in this course.

This course is All first-year courses designed to meet the educational requirements for midwives who are seeking endorsement as bachelor's prepared Registered Midwives. The course builds on the student's background as a Registered Midwife (RM) and prepares the student to think critically about medication selection, dosage, and route of administration for those medications used throughout pregnancy, labor, and immediately postpartum.

This second course builds on the content offered in midw204 and provides the student with a greater understanding of the research process and its application to midwifery practice. Various types of research and research methods, as well as statistical methods, are discussed. Emphasis will be placed on the student developing, writing, and executing an oral presentation of his or her nursing research proposal paper.

This is a final skills refinement clinical course to empower the midwifery students to become competent and compassionate midwives capable of providing women and family centered care across diverse settings. Students will be able to successfully integrate into clinical practice the knowledge, skills and confidence as novice professional midwives to navigate the complexities of professional midwifery practice. They will be able to make meaningful contributions to the health and well-being of women and their family. The focus will be on enhancing professional communication, psychomotor, assessment, safety, organizational, medication administration, and documentation skills.

course_title: Senior Midwifery Practice Seminar (from Theory to Application)
description: Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

This course provides a foundation in teaching and learning that includes the learning theories, principles of adult education, effective teaching strategies, classroom management techniques, and evaluation. The student will learn how to conduct learning needs assessments, write course outcomes, do lesson planning, and conduct formative and summative evaluations.

In this final practicum of the BSc program, students will choose from one of the following areas of interest and work alongside a midwife (or other professional) in that setting. The areas are as follows: 1. Teaching/Curriculum development: CMs, RMs, and Teaching Assistants in BSc program 2. Administration: Ward, Department, or Clinic Manager 3. Leadership: Internship with a governmental organization, nongovernmental organization, or international agency 4. Entrepreneurship: Internship with a small business or corporation whose mission aligns with that of the profession of midwifery 5. Research: Internship In addition to the practicum, students will write a final paper that relates to their practicum. Students will meet with the course instructor independently or in small groups arranged by area of interest at regular intervals throughout the semester for guidance on

course_title: Fundamentals of Nursing II (with Lab and Clinical)
description: This course is a continuation of nurs205. It will introduce the student to complex nursing competencies in the provision of clinical care as it relates to cognitive, psychomotor, and affective domains needed to provide safe evidence-based nursing practice. The student learns more complex knowledge, skills, and attitudes needed for safe, evidence-based nursing care. The student will practice safe nursing care in a variety of situations applying principles of wellness, health promotion, and disease prevention in caring for patients across the lifespan. The student will continue to use the nursing process in formulating a plan of care for patients and their families. This course has laboratory and clinical components. The laboratory allows students to learn and practice skills related to key patient care concepts. The clinical component exposes the student to actual patient care and will provide opportunities to increase competencies in performing nursing care.

This course is the first part of the two-part course on medical and surgical nursing practices. It provides a theoretical foundation and evidence-based care for patients with selected disease conditions. The focus will be on the biophysical and psychosocial concepts in medical-surgical nursing care delivery, covering homeostasis, stress and adaptation, and perioperative concepts. Major medical-surgical dysfunctions covering integumentary, musculoskeletal, digestive, gastrointestinal, urinary, hematologic, and reproductive systems will be discussed within the nursing process framework. The clinical component of Medical-Surgical Nursing I focuses on intermediate nursing care and critical thinking skills within collaborative nursing practice settings. It allows students to develop skills and competencies necessary for nursing care and managing adult patients with medical-surgical diseases and conditions interfering with the integumentary, musculoskeletal, digestive, gastrointestinal, urinary, hematologic, and reproductive systems.

This course is the second part of a two-part course on medical and surgical nursing practices. The course provides a theoretical foundation and evidence-based nursing care of patients with selected medical-surgical dysfunctions including the principles and practices related to chronic illness, disabilities, and rehabilitation. System dysfunctions covering gas exchange, respiratory, cardiovascular, neurologic, sensory, metabolic, endocrine, and immunologic will be discussed within the context of the nursing process framework. Content related to emergency care of patients will be covered. The clinical provides the students with the opportunity to develop the competencies and skills necessary for managing adult patients with medical-surgical conditions/disorders of body systems. The student will utilize the human needs theory, primary health care approach, and nursing process to deliver care to medical-surgical adult patients and their families.

The course provides students with theoretical foundations for promoting mental health across the lifespan. It focuses on the nursing care needs of persons experiencing alterations in social and psychological functioning. Students examine theoretical and research findings as well as issues and trends that influence the planning and delivery of nursing care for persons with common mental health needs and disorders across the lifespan. Students will integrate mental health concepts, principles of human relationships, and interpersonal skills in caring for selected clients in a therapeutic environment within the context of the nursing process as a framework for providing and managing patients with psychiatric mental health nursing care needs. The clinical part of the course enables the student to apply knowledge acquired in the course's theoretical component. The student will apply and develop skills and competencies in the assessment and management of individuals with psychiatric mental health disorders and problems using appropriate interventions. Students are expected to collaborate with multidisciplinary and multisectoral teams involved in the care of individuals with psychiatric mental health disorders.

This is the first part of a two-part clinical obstetrical maternity nursing course that focuses on the childbearing client and family needs from conception through postpartum. This first part provides a foundational understanding of the principles of safe motherhood and basic care to the pregnant and laboring woman and her family. It is also designed to teach the student about reproductive health, family planning, and adolescent sexual and reproductive health. This course helps prepare the student to provide adolescents with family planning counseling and reproductive health care services. Alterations in obstetrical and gynecological health during the reproductive cycle will be addressed. The clinical part of the course is an introductory experience in the delivery of nursing care to mothers, neonates, and families. Students will be introduced to the gynecological care and counseling needs of adolescent, adult, and elderly female patients. Obstetrical conditions and gynecological problems commonly encountered are the focus of this clinical experience. Emphasis will be placed on health promotion and illness prevention during the childbearing phase of life.

This is the second part of a two-part clinical obstetrical maternity nursing course. This course continues to address themes related to the principles of safe motherhood while focusing on the clinical care of the newborn/neonates, Emphasis is placed on disorders of the reproductive system . This guided clinical course builds on the student's knowledge and performance in nurc305. Students will be expected to perform normal and abnormal deliveries and manage obstetric emergencies. A student is expected to be able to conduct a minimum of ten normal deliveries, five abnormal deliveries, and at least five obstetric emergencies. Student/midwife dyads provide students with a vantage point to participate in the deliveries and the management of obstetric emergencies in both urban and rural communities.

This is the first part of a two-part clinical pediatric nursing course. This course is designed to teach the students the role of the pediatric nurse who provides care to children from birth to adolescence. It builds on and assumes knowledge of newborn care, and care of the neonate to age two months. It addresses the care of the well child, health promotion, and growth and development. Emphasis is placed on evidence-based care concerning the use of the nursing process and selected nursing theories in the care of children and adolescents. The clinical part of this course will help the student enhance the skills and knowledge in caring for the well child. The course provides students with evidence-based patient care skills for caring for healthy children, adolescents, and their families in the hospital and community settings.

This is the second part of a two-part clinical pediatric nursing course. This course is designed to teach students acute and emergent nursing care of children from infancy to adolescence utilizing the nursing process. It builds on and assumes knowledge of newborn care and care of healthy children learned in nurc307 Pediatrics Nursing I. Application of growth and development, integration of pathophysiology of childhood diseases, and teaching and counseling families are integral to this course. Emphasis will be placed on the World Health Organization's Integrated Management of Childhood Illnesses (IMNCI) as the primary strategy for managing illness in children under five. This course include guided clinical experience in various settings that serve pediatric and adolescent patients correlates with the required didactic instruction. The students can enhance the skills and knowledge drawn from the fundamentals of nursing clinical experiences while working with sick children and their families in hospital and community settings.

This course focuses on the community as a client. Emphasis is on the nurse's role in addressing the preventative health needs of populations at risk. Current public health problems, epidemiology, trends in healthcare delivery, and community resources are examined. Students analyze the sociocultural, political, economic, ethical, and environmental factors influencing community and global health. Students will perform comprehensive community and family assessments using focused assessment tools. This course involves clinical practicum experience in designated community agencies. Within a variety of community-based settings, students will apply the concepts of family, community, and/or population assessment, evaluate relevant data sources to identify health priorities, examine current health policy as it relates to their community or population of interest, and advocate for the wellness of the physical community and the populations residing within.

This course introduces students to concepts of leadership and management for application in practice settings. Principles of time management, leadership styles, budgeting, staffing patterns, personnel evaluations, delegation, the steps of discipline, and healthcare accreditation criteria are discussed and analyzed across diverse practice settings. Students use a variety of professional tools such as in-class forums to debate relevant healthcare issues, the resume, a portfolio, and a template for a project proposal for enhancement of professional development. This course focuses on the student developing their leadership and management style within the clinical setting. Participation in this clinical course will support competency development in the coordination of patient-centered care, implementation of evidence-based care, interprofessional team collaboration for prioritized clinical decision-making, use of ethics and respect principles in all aspects of practice, technical skill acquisition dependent on clinical setting, regulatory knowledge related to scope of practice, and budgetary and health policy impact of care.

This course is designed to expand students' skills in diagnosing and treating common diseases in Liberia. The students apply previous knowledge and skills of assessment, such as obtaining vital signs, history taking, physical examination, and interpretation of findings, to arrive at a rapid and accurate diagnosis using algorithms and flowcharts. The students utilize summaries of algorithms of rapid diagnostic processes and emergency skills in handling various emergencies when working in low-resource areas of Liberia. In the clinical component of the course, students will integrate knowledge of primary health, health education, nutrition, maternal-child care, prenatal care, family planning, and environmental sanitation to run clinics and health centers. They will learn how to work with community health volunteers to deliver care to individuals, families, and communities. The course teaches the student to function at the clinic level, linking the clinic with the community and working with community-level providers.

This course teaches the student knowledge, skills, and attitudes to perform a comprehensive health assessment through interviewing and physical examination of the patient. The student will learn how to conduct health assessments while taking into consideration the physical, psychological, social, cultural, spiritual, functional, and environmental factors that affect health and illness. The student will learn how to report findings and document them on the medical record. The student will be introduced to the basic principles of adult education as they apply to patient and family teaching. The Lab Focus on developing students' technical proficiency in basic health assessment techniques and skills. Students will practice interviewing, physical examination, and patient/family teaching skills.

This course introduces the professional, ethical, and legal decision-making frameworks used in the profession of nursing. Students will have opportunities to discuss moral, ethical, and legal concepts and responsibilities related to nursing, nurses, patients, and healthcare delivery. Students will explore their values and beliefs and identify their nursing philosophy. In addition, the course will equip students with the necessary skills to deal with moral, ethical, and religious dilemmas. Emphasis will be placed on the principles of professional practice and the regulation of nursing practice.

This course introduces the student to the nursing profession through the acquisition of knowledge of nursing history, code of ethics, values, scope of practice, and the nursing process. The student learns beginning knowledge, skills, and values to provide safe patient care. The content of the course relates to concepts such as communication, vital signs, basic infection control (medical and surgical asepsis), activity and mobility, basic safety and comfort, hygiene, basic nutrition, elimination, general skin care, and selected specimen collection. The Laboratory component focus on developing students' technical proficiency in basic patient care techniques and skills using evidence-based practice and the nursing process.

This course introduces the theoretical background that enables students to provide safe and effective care related to drugs and natural products to persons throughout their lifespans. Students learn to make selected clinical decisions regarding using current, reliable sources of information, monitoring, and evaluating the effectiveness of drug therapy, teaching persons from diverse populations regarding the safe and effective use of drugs and natural products, intervening to increase therapeutic benefits and reduce potential negative effects, and communicating appropriately with other health professionals regarding drug therapy. Drugs are studied by therapeutic or pharmacological class using an organized framework.

course_title: Nursing Care of the Aging Adult
description: This course discusses and highlights the role of the nurse in meeting the physiologic, psychosocial, and spiritual needs of the elderly. Course content includes the normal aging process, the effects of aging on the aging body, and the physiologic, psychosocial, and spiritual needs of the aging adult in the sociocultural context of Liberia. The effects of medications on the aging adult explored. Students will learn to provide safe and effective nursing care to the aging adult patient. Gerontology nursing as an emerging specialty in Liberia, as well as the nurse's required leadership and management skills required in care for aging adults, will be emphasized.

This is part one of a two-part course. The course explores the steps of the research process, and the framework for critiquing qualitative and quantitative nursing research articles will be presented.
