Students are expected to develop the ability to read and analyze various nursing and/or health research articles critically. Students will conduct a review of the literature and critique selections of research articles related to nursing and healthcare practices. Students are expected to develop the ability to apply research findings to nursing practice to ensure evidence-based practice. The opportunity to complete a literature review of research articles related to nursing and to formulate research problems applying the elements of the research process will be provided. Ethical issues involved in nursing research and various theoretical frameworks for nursing research will be explored. The nurse's role as a member of a research team and his or her responsibility to the profession in conducting and disseminating nursing and health-related research will be explored and discussed.
This course is a workshop designed to equip the students with the continuous process of acquiring new knowledge and skills that relate to the nursing capstone.
This course is designed to equip the students with the continuous process of acquiring new knowledge, skills, and professional expectations related to the nursing capstone.

This second course builds on the content offered in nurs312 and provides the student with a greater understanding of the research process and its application to nursing and nursing practice. Various types of research and research methods, as well as statistical methods, are discussed. Emphasis will be placed on the student developing, writing, and executing an oral presentation of his or her nursing research proposal paper.

This course is designed to equip the students with the continuous process of acquiring new knowledge and skills that relate to the nursing courses taken in the previous semesters and facilitates the transition from student nurse to Registered Nurse.

This course introduces students to the concepts, principles, and theories of curriculum development, teaching strategies, the evaluation of learning, as well as educational programs in nursing. Students teach health promotion behaviors to individuals, families, groups, and communities using appropriate teaching-learning techniques and strategies. Students engage in the teaching-learning process by providing teaching in hospitals, health centers, and community as well as grade school settings. The course further exposes students to the role of nurses as educators in the clinical practice arena and schools of nursing.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

course_title: Introduction to Public Administration and Political Science
description: This course focuses primarily on the functional aspects of the three branches of government, a general introduction to the science and art of public administration, the elements, functions and processes of public administration, simple decision making models, financial and personnel administration, organizational theories and leadership concepts. It also underscores the principles of public administration in respect to accomplishing public goals, public service and the community. The course evaluates public administration as academic discipline, analyzing the respective schools of thought, organizational charts and hierarchies, civil servants, spoil and merit systems

This course focuses on the nature, objective and functions of development planning and administration in the third world, with special reference to Liberia. The course includes the rising role of social-economic planning models, implementation and coordination of comprehensive national, inter-regional, andsectorial development programs including the mobilization and allocation of human and financial resources. It also explores the functional aspects of rural, urban, and regional community development.

The study of Administrative Law and Practice involves examination of the legal rules and institutions through which government decision-making is authorized, effected and reviewed. This course further examines the exercise of executive officials and the means by which administrative actions are carried out. It also looks at the merit based procedures, the law labor of Liberia and the functions of the Public Defender.

Students exhibit knowledge and understanding of the administrative behavior, theories and practice. Topics covered social, psychological and behavioral theories of organization; concepts of administrative leadership organization and the individual; emphasis on the use of legal and administrative power in government and public organizations, ecological constraints and the styles used in the exercises of social, legal and administrative power.

The course enables students to articulate a thorough knowledge of public organizational structure, identify key concepts central to organizing and changing public agencies for social needs. The concepts are traced from the historical development of contemporary theories, and projection of trends in the public agencies. Bureaucratic theory underlying contemporary organizational assumptions of man are also explored.

This course is primarily designed for students in Public Administration and those who choose as elective to understand the interconnection and the role the government and International and Local NGO's are playing in bringing the desired services for the citizens of Liberia specifically and the world generally. It underscores the importance of NGO's in complimenting the government in providing basic goods and services especially for a country emerging from a brutal civil war. The heart of this course is the study of how government and NGO's interact and work together within an organized setting.

The course is organized in three sections: a) communication and the organization, b) introduction to basic communication models and c) cybernetic and intra organizational communication processes and problems. This course covers verbal, written, implied, behavioral electronic informal and interpersonal forms of organization communication.

The course is intended to help students demonstrate proficiency in administration management. A comparison is made between the administrative systems of industrial nations and those of the underdeveloped world alongside the political and ecological landscape styles of these two separate worlds.

The course analyzes the personnel problems with emphasis on supervision and management of public and private employees and public organization in an age of change. Special emphasis is made to the Liberian civil service agency and its function in recruitment, examination, selection position classification pay plan, grievances and complains and the retirement system.

This course is designed to enhance students to critically examine the contributions of Multi-National Organizations in the development of Communities in Africa general and Liberia in particular. It underscores the Corporate Social Responsibility of Multinational Organization in up lifting communities that have been deprived of basic needs.

The course looks at management trends in public sector finance- administration, budgetary procedures and techniques, as well as control and monitoring systems with special reference to Liberia. Its analysis is primarily based on the Liberian Government budget as an instrument of economic and social policy and a tool for efficient financial management and coordination. Emphasis is also placed on the four basic phases of the Liberian budgetary process- executive preparation and budget to check on inflation, reverse trade recession, improve the balance of payments and distribute incomes. Furthermore, theories and social consequences of budget decision-making and practices of budgetary process at all levels of government are highlighted. Other critical topics explore include cash management, capital projects management, debt administration, disbursement, funds management and auditing.

This course will provide an introduction to the theory and practice of rural development. It will use examples of topical issues from development and developing nations to illustrate the range of change facing rural areas in Liberia and Africa generally. Upon completion of this course, students will evaluate different approaches to the management of Land, Labor and other natural resources in their respective communities.

The course looks at the role of decentralized local government in the in the planning and political development of local communities. It discusses functions and relationship of various line ministries representatives in the counties with county superintendent and central offices/government.

The focus of this course is on the role of administrators in policy formulation, analysis and decision-making with special emphasis on the study of methods and techniques by which public policies are formulated, analyzed, implemented and evaluated.

course_title: Issues in Liberia and African Development
description: The course integrates the development studies and other related courses offered in other universities. It is cross-disciplinary in its approach and mainly concerns itself with the dynamics and problem which affect the development and modernization process of Liberia in particular and Africa in general.

This course provides experiential learning opportunities for senior students in Public Administration. It also provides practical experience for would be administrators. Each student is required to prepare a detailed report on his/her assigned internship experience with the institution he/she was placed. Another written report is also required from the intern supervisor as 70\% of the supervisor's report will contribute to the student's semester grade.

This course explores the developmental needs of young children, curriculum design, instructional strategies, and assessment methods appropriate for primary schools. Emphasis is placed on creating inclusive, engaging, and supportive learning environments that foster academic and social growth. Exploration of curriculum and programming for students in grade one through sixth grade with emphasis on children's developmental, social, physical, emotional, and cognitive needs as related to appropriate learning experiences.

course_title: Content-Specific Methods for Primary Grades Math + General Methods for Primary Math
description: This course covers the content and process of culturally-relevant mathematics instruction in the primary grades including mathematics teaching principles, mathematical tools, and assessment of student progress within the content learning trajectories of Counting and Cardinality; Comparing, Ordering and Computational Estimating; Adding, Subtracting, Multiplying and Dividing; Composition, Place Value and Multi-Digit Computation; Spatial Visualization and Location; Properties of Shapes; Measurement; Algebraic Reasoning; and Data Analysis.

course_title: Social Studies in Primary Classroom I
description: This course introduces foundational concepts in teaching social studies at the primary school level. It explores key themes such as community, citizenship, culture, and geography, focusing on age-appropriate instructional strategies. Teacher-aspirants will learn to develop engaging lesson plans, incorporate storytelling and interactive activities, and foster critical thinking skills in young learners. Emphasis is placed on the Liberian social studies curriculum grade 1-3, integrating local and global perspectives to help children understand their role in society.

course_title: Social Studies in Primary Classroom II
description: Building on the foundations of Social Studies in Primary Classroom I, this course deepens the exploration of instructional methods for teaching history, civics, geography, and economics. It examines curriculum development, assessment techniques, and the use of technology to enhance learning. Emphasis is placed on the Liberia social studies curriculum from grade 4-6. Special attention is given to culturally responsive teaching, inquiry-based learning, and strategies for fostering social awareness and problem-solving skills in primary grades.

course_title: Practicum in Literacy and Social Studies in Primary Classroom
description: This course introduces teacher-aspirants to the basic knowledge of the Social Sciences which includes sociology, anthropology, political science, history, economics, demography, and geography. It enhances students' awareness of the current social, political, and economic affairs of their society and aims to expose students to the basic concepts of scientific methods.

course_title: Natural and Life Sciences in the Primary Grades
description: This course introduces teacher-aspirants to effective methods for teaching natural and life sciences to children in primary grades. Emergent educators will explore age-appropriate concepts in biology, ecology, and physical science, emphasizing inquiry-based learning and hands-on activities. The course focuses on fostering curiosity, critical thinking, and environmental awareness while aligning contents with developmental stages and the Liberian curriculum standards for general science.

course_title: Practicum in Math and Science for Primary Grades
description: This course focuses on hands-on practical experience in math and science from grade one to six. Teacher-aspirants will work directly with children in these content areas to ground their knowledge and world views with keen emphasis on Liberia's primary curriculum for math and general science.

This course focuses on effective strategies for teaching reading comprehension and writing skills in primary grades. Teacher-aspirants will explore techniques to enhance children's ability to analyze, interpret, and engage with texts, as well as methods to develop clear and cohesive writing. Topics include vocabulary development, critical thinking, writing process approaches, etc. Emergent educators will demonstrate knowledge and understanding to develop literacy at each stage in the primary education classroom, including concepts, materials, and teaching strategies for reading fluency, reading comprehension, and writing and speaking to support diverse students.

course_title: Diagnostic and Prescriptive Advanced Methods for Primary Grades Math
description: This course focuses on advanced strategies for diagnosing and addressing mathematical learning difficulties in primary-grade students. Teacher-aspirants will explore assessment tools, data-driven decision-making, and individualized instructional techniques to support diverse learners. Emphasis is placed on error analysis, intervention strategies, and differentiated instruction to enhance students' conceptual understanding and problem-solving skills. The course also integrates technology and hands-on activities to develop stimulating, student-centered learning experiences.

This course explores the principles and practices of teaching language arts in grade one through sixth grade. It covers key components such as reading comprehension, writing, speaking, listening, and vocabulary development, with a focus on evidence-based instructional strategies. Special topics such as sentences, tenses, subject-verb agreement, paragraphs, the parts of speech, phrases and clauses, capitalization, and punctuation will be explored. Teacher-aspirants will learn to design engaging lessons, assess literacy skills, and support diverse learners. Emphasis is placed on integrating literature, phonics, fluency, and comprehension strategies to foster a love for language and effective communication skills in young learners.

This seminar provides a critical analysis of the current trends, challenges, and best practices in teaching in primary classrooms. Through discussions, case studies, and reflective activities, teacher-aspirants will analyze instructional strategies, classroom management techniques, and working with colleagues in professional setting. The course emphasizes collaboration, problem-solving, and professional growth, preparing teacher-aspirants to apply research-based approaches in real-world primary classroom settings while utilizing the Liberian Teachers Code of Conduct as a cornerstone. Special focus is given to fostering inclusive learning environments and adapting to diverse student needs.

This advanced practicum provides hands-on experience in integrating literacy and creative arts into primary-grade instruction. Teacher-aspirants will apply research-based strategies in teaching reading, writing, drama, music, and visual arts to enhance student engagement and learning. Teacher-aspirants will develop teaching aids to enable students learn reading, phonemic awareness skills, and writing creatively. Emphasis is placed on lesson planning, classroom management, and differentiated instruction to support diverse learners. Through guided practice and reflection, teacher-aspirants will develop innovative teaching approaches that foster creativity and literacy development in young children in primary grades.

course_title: Internship - Teaching in Primary School Setting
description: This semester-long internship provides teacher-aspirants with supervised, hands-on teaching experience in a primary school classroom. The teacher-aspirants will apply instructional strategies, classroom management techniques, and assessment methods in real-world primary school settings. Emphasis is placed on lesson planning, differentiated instruction, and fostering an inclusive and engaging learning environment. Through mentorship, self-reflection, and feedback teacher-aspirants will develop the skills necessary for effective teaching and professional growth. The internship culminates in a comprehensive evaluation of teaching performance and student impact.

This course explores issues concerning the mental, social, physical and moral development of students. It covers fundamental activities in team sports (soccer, volleyball, basketball and athletics). The aim is to promote physical fitness, wellness and self-testing activities.

This course is an intensive and practical continuation of phed101. It focuses on physical fitness, wellness and self-testing activities. The aim is to promote wellness and team sports (soccer, volleyball, basketball and athletics) as important human values and enterprises.

course_title: History, Principles and Foundations of Physical Education
description: This course deals with the meaning, definition and scope of Physical Education including its aims and objectives and its importance in the present era. In addition, it also includes topics on the historical development of Physica1 Education which focuses on medieval period (1000 AD - 1757 AD), British Period (Before 1947), and Physical Education in Liberia and in Greece. The contributions to physical education in Liberia of Institutions or Bodies like YMCA and Armed Forces will also be tackled. More importantly, foundations of Physical Education especially its philosophical foundation on Idealism, Pragmatism, Naturalism, Realism, Humanism, Existentialism and Culture are also incorporated.

This course will focus on health education, health problems, environmental science and natural Resources and related environmental issues. Topics under Health Education are aims, objectives and principles of Health Education, biomedical, ecological and holistic concepts, dimensions of Health and factors effecting Health. Communicable and non-communicable diseases like chickenpox, measles, hypertension, diabetes, and the like under health problems are also included. Other topics are: need and importance of environmental studies, role of school in environmental conservation and sustainable development, natural resources, effects and control measures of air pollution, water pollution, soil pollution, noise pollution, thermal pollution and role of pollution control board.

This course will focuses on major topics such as: scope of Sports Psychology, Individual differences in sports, heredity and environment, dynamics of human behavior, play and theories of Play. Others include, theories of learning, transfer of Learning, personality and sport, characteristics of personality, dimension of personality, motivation, culture, features of culture, effects of culture on people life style. orthodoxy, customs, tradition and Physical Education, festivals and physical Education, Socialization through Physical Education, social group, Primary group and Remote group.

This course focuses on the structures of the human body and how these structures work together. It includes the gross and microscopic structure and function of the human body. Topics include body organization; homeostasis; cytology; histology; and the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous systems, and special senses. It demonstrates an in-depth understanding of principles of anatomy and physiology and their interrelationship. Laboratory works includes dissection of preserved specimens, microscopic study, physiological experiments, computer simulations, and multimedia presentations.

This course focuses on the fundamental movement of human and how its helps in wellness. It also includes mechanical concepts in movement, concepts in human physiology such as angle of pull, reciprocal innervations, Planes and axes, gravity, base, centre of gravity, equilibrium, and line of gravity. It will also discuss concepts in kinematics and kinetics of human such as Newton's Laws of Motion and their application in sports, Factors influencing projectile trajectory, distance and displacement, speed and velocity, acceleration and displacement, angular speed and velocity, angular acceleration, inertia, mass, momentum, and friction.

course_title: Educational Technology and Methods of Teaching in Physical Education
description: This course focuses on educational technology, types of education, teaching technique, types of teaching aids, lesson planning, micro Teaching, meaning of tournament and types of tournaments which includes: Knock-out (Elimination), League (Round Robin), Knock-out cum league, League cum knock-out, Double league, Double knockout, Challenge. Method of drawing Fixtures: Seeding, Special Seeding. Rotation Method, Stair case method and Intramural and Extramural and their importance.

This course include concept and importance of Sports Management, forms of leadership i.e. autocratic, Laissez-faire, Democratic, Benevolent and Dictator etc. financial management in Physical Education and sports in schools such as: Criteria of good budget, Steps of budget making, model budget for a school, procedures for purchases and constructions, sports management in schools, colleges and universities Planning, directing and controlling school, college and university sports programs, duties of official in general, pre, during and post-game in athletics meet, qualities and qualifications of good coach and good official.

Introduction to a variety of ethnic and social dance forms and swimming; and appreciation of their historical and cultural origins. Analysis and refinement of skill requirements; examination of concepts, movement theories, and appreciations; and adaptations for special populations related to modern dance, jazz, ballet, social dance, folk dance, ethnic dance and dance exercise, types of swimming etc.

This course focuses on good health (physical, mental, social), nutrients, malnutrition, and wellness. It required to identify nutrients and the main food (a) Nutrients: protein, carbohydrates, fats, groups; minerals, vitamins, fibre, water. It also includes: Testing blood pressure, heart rate, principles of assessment, prescription and exercise program adherence, physical fitness testing, and basic principles of Exercise program Design.

This course focuses on the testing of fitness in all dimensions but not limited to : Cardio respiratory fitness and designing cardio respiratory, exercise programs, exercise prescription, guidelines and procedures, maximal exercise, test protocols, sub maximal Exercise test protocols, field tests for assessing aerobic fitness, exercise testing for children, aerobic training methods. Designing resistance training programs, types of resistance training, developing resistance training programs, Muscular misconceptions on resistance training, Assessing Flexibility and Designing stretching programs, Laboratory methods for assessing body composition, Field methods for assessing body composition, Designing weight management and body composition programs,

The course focuses on definition of First Aid, DRABC formula which include: (danger, response, airways, breathing and circulation etc.). It also include concepts such as mouth to mouth, mouth to nose respiration, CPR (Cardio Pulmonary Resuscitation). In also include Treatments of wounds such as: laceration, blisters, contusion, strain, sprain, fracture, dislocation and cramps. Cryotherapy will also be discussed under the following: Thermo therapy, Contrast Bath, Whirlpool Bath, Steam Bath, Sauna Bath, etc. Hydrotherapy and Massage Therapeutic exercise also be discussed through: Passive Movements (Relaxed, Forced and passive stretching). Active movements (concentric, Eccentric and static). Free Mobility Exercise for Shoulder, Wrist, Fingers, Hip, Ankle, Foot joints and Neck exercises.

course_title: Analysis of Artistic and Rhythmic Gymnastics
description: Analysis and refinement of skill requirements; examination of concepts, movement theories, judging, history and appreciations; and adaptations for special populations related to stunts, tumbling, floor exercise; apparatus work and object manipulation required of artistic and rhythmic gymnastics. One hour of lecture and two hours of activity per week. Fee required.

Each candidate will be required to undertake three events but not more than two events from any group of Running, Jumping and Throwing events. Track events includes: world championship, para-Olympic etc.; design the 400m and200m track arena, types of tracks events and their phases. Difference between sprint middle and long distance races including relay race and its rules. Field events includes: throwing or putting sectors dimensions and technique such as long jump, high jump, triple jump, hammer throw, discus, shot put javelin etc.

This course is designed to provide an introduction to the team sport. Topics addressed consist of, but are not limited to: history, rules, terminology, and basic skills, techniques, world governing bodies, and tactics associated with participating in, observing, and coaching the sport. Unit 1: Soccer Unit 2: Volleyball Unit3: Basketball Unit 4: Tennis Unit 5: Table Tennis Unit 6: Cricket Unit 7: Kickball (Minor Game) Unit 8: handball

This course introduces students to philosophy, its major themes and some key individuals in ancient, medieval, modern, and contemporary philosophy. Topics include the nature of knowledge, (epistemology), the nature of the things (metaphysics), the nature of right and wrong (ethics), the mind/body problem, free will, atheism and the existence of God. The aim is to help students develop and use their reflective and critical abilities to ask and answer important questions.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

This course introduces students to classical mechanics. Topics include: space and time; straight-line kinematics; motion in a plane; forces and equilibrium; experimental basis of Newton's laws; particle dynamics; universal gravitation; collisions and conservation laws; work and potential energy; vibrational motion; conservative forces; inertial forces and non-inertial frames; central force motions; rigid bodies and rotational dynamics. Lab: An introductory course sequence designed to give science students an understanding of the practical meaning of introductory physics. This course provides the student with the practical understanding of basic concepts, insight and phenomena of physics. This course emphasis classical mechanics and heat. phys102 provides the understanding of the concepts of error analysis and graphical analysis of physical data. It also prepared students to solve real life problems via the application of physical theories. phys102 is non-calculus-based course intended for physics, mathematics, chemistry, biology, civil engineering, mining engineering, electrical engineering, geology, agriculture, agronomy, and forestry majors.

This course is Introduction to Physics II, a continuation of phys101. It emphasizes on waves and electromagnetic energy. Students will start with a study of electromagnetic phenomena; including electrostatics; capacitors; resistors and direct-current circuits; magnetism and magnetic induction; RC, LR, and LRC circuits; waves; electromagnetic waves; and radiation. Students are also introduced to properties and propagations of sound, wave motion, light, electromagnetic spectrum, the nature of light, speed, emission, absorption of light, reflection, refraction, and diffraction of light plus polarization of light; Finally, an overview of the relationship between matter, energy, and light will be presented. All presentations will emphasize the concepts and principles centered on calculus mathematics linked to math201, math202. A one-hour lab period will demonstrate the practical applications of the various concepts taught. Lab: An introductory laboratory course that emphasize the practical aspect of phys106, optics, electricity and magnetism. It is a continuation of the first Laboratory course. The course covered Laboratory report writing , instrumentation , graphics and interpretation of physical constants derived from experiments.

course_title: Basic Electronics and Circuit Theory (with Lab)
description: This course introduces students to basic electronics and circuit theory principles. Courses taught are direct current (D.C) circuit, sources of electromotive force (e.m.f). Resistor combinations. Kirchoff's laws. Network analysis and circuit theorems. Mesh current methods, Node-voltage , Thevein and Norton theorem, superposition principle. Alternating Current (A.C), sinusoidal waveforms. Root-mean-square (RMS) and peak values. Power, Resistance, Inductance, and Capacitance in a.c circuit. Impedance and admittance. Series and parallel RLC circuits. Q- Factor, Resonace, and the transformer. Electronic, Filters, amplifiers and transistors. Bipolar junction and Field Effect Transistor (F.E.T). Equivalent circuits. Feedback, Oscillators, Signal generators. Semiconductors (devices and characteristics). The p-n junction, simple diodes, photodiodes and Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs). Lab: A second course sequence that is designed to prepare students effectively to conceptual and practice, the understanding of scientific phenomena in proving theories and principles are paramount. The students will be trained to work independently on real-world problem based on fundamental principles of physics; being able to solve realistic problems using scientific theories and laws and learning to communicate technical information effectively. To achieve these goals, this course requires an in-depth understanding of the concept of Heat and Electricity. Physics 211L will emphasize the theoretical and experimental approach to physics, emphasizing the cares to be considered; kinds of errors, error analysis, and laboratory report writing. Most of the experiment will include Bimetallic strip for thermal expansion, Heat Capacity of Water, Boiling and Cooling rate of water, Simple Electric Circuit connection, Resistors in Series and Parallel connections in an electric circuit, Soldering electric or electronics elements in a panel, and Solenoid.

Previously General Physics II, this course highlights the core areas of electricity and magnetism. Specific contents include electric field and forces, electric potential, calculating electric potential, current and resistance, electric circuit, and Ohm's law, Resistance combinations. Conductors and insulators. DC circuits (current, voltage and resistance). Analysis of DC circuits. AC circuits (voltages and currents applied to capacitors, inductors, and resistors. Step-Up and Step-Down transformers. Also include Magnets and Magnetism (ferromagnetism, paramagnetism, diamagnetism), magnetization and demagnetization. Magnetic field. Lorentz force, Biot-Savart's and Ampere's laws. Magnetic dipoles, dielectrics. Energy in magnetic field, electromotive force (emf), electromagetic induction. Self and mutual inductances. Lab: This Laboratory course is designed for physicists and engineers to have practical knowledge around optics and magnetism. The experiments will assist the students to interpret fundamental principles and laws in Physics under these concepts. The designed experiments are but not limited to demonstration of Laws of Reflection in Mirror and Lenses, Laws of Refraction in transparent Glass block and Lenses, and in Water, Magnification of images, demonstration of Snell's laws using triangular glass Prism, Diffraction of light waves, Dispersion of White Light using triangular glass Prism, demonstration of magnetic force and magnetic field using bar magnetic and iron fillings.

This course is designed as an elective course for students interested in the area of geophysics and explorations. The introductory courses taught include; the solar system, the Earth's history, the Earth's interior, Seismicity and Earthquake zones. The Earth's gravitational field and gravitational potential. Figure of the Earth, the Earth's magnetic field. Others are Rock magnetism, Polar Wandering, Continental drift, Plate Tectonics, Seafloor spreading, Volcanoes, Rock cycle, Glaciations, Groundwater, Heat flow, and other case studies.

The course is the first level of classical mechanics and begins with the key mathematical principles -- vector arithmetic and calculus. Key themes include Kinematics, Newtonian Mechanics, Dynamics of Particles, Oscillation, Theory of Relativity, Conservation of Energy, Conservation of linear and Angular Momentum, The dynamics of Rigid Bodies, Lagrange equation, Hamilton equation.

The course advances upon phys301 and discusses in-depth topics such as Dynamics of Systems of Particles, Motion of Rigid Bodies in Three Dimensions, Lagranian Mechanics and Dynamic of Oscillating System.

The course teaches concepts of modern Physics such as special relativity, relativistic momentum, mass and energy, theory of relativity, wave-particle duality and measurement of reality, atomic structure and statistical view. Students will be required to study in-depth the principle of Lorentz, Eistein, Bohr, and Zeeman to modern Physics.

The course analyses the statistical principles of thermodynamics beginning with temperature with focus on work, heat, ideal gases, reversibility and irreversibility. The first, second and third laws of thermodynamics will be fully studied. Concurrently, the course will bring to bed the practical applications of the principles of statistical mechanics, thermal properties of solid and chemical equilibrium.

course_title: Mathematical Method in the Physical Sciences
description: The course is purely mathematical in design, reflecting infinite series, complex number, linear algebra, multiple integrals, vector analysis, Fourier analysis and transforms, differential equations, integral transforms and coordinate transformation as well as special functions.

The course advances upon phys411 and studies in-depth magnetic forces and fields. Core areas of concentration include, among others, displacement of current, electromagnetic waves, momentum and radiation pressure, electric dipole radiation, sinusoidal electromagnetic waves and electromagnetic spectrum. The course advances upon Gauss' Law of Magnetism with a focus on Ampere Law and Maxwell Equations and Electromagnetic waves.

Beginning with a review of mathematical analysis of vector, the course explores discussions on electric charge and field, electric potential and electric current. Coulomb's and Gauss's laws of electromagnetism are emphasized.
The course uses physical applications and geometry to study not only geometric and physical optics but also the Fourier optics, atmosphere optics and modern optics.

The course is the first level of classical mechanics and begins with the key mathematical principles -- vector arithmetic and calculus. Key themes include Kinematics, Newtonian Mechanics, Defects in Newtonian Mechanics, Dynamics of Particles, Oscillation, Theory of Relativity, Galilean relativity. The speed of light, inertia frames and the concept of an observer. The principle of Eistein's special theory of relativity. Lorentz transformation, Time-dilation, and length contraction. Transformation of velocities. Doppler's Effect. Relativistic of energy and momentum. Conservation of Energy, Conservation of linear and Angular Momentum, The dynamics of Rigid Bodies, Lagrange equation, Hamilton equation.

The course advances upon phys311 and discusses in-depth topics such as Dynamics of Systems of Particles, motion of Rigid Bodies in Three Dimensions, Lagranian Mechanics and Dynamic of Oscillating System.
The course uses physical applications and geometry to study not only geometric and physical optics but also the Fourier optics, atmosphere optics and modern optics.

course_title: Mathematical Method in the Physical Sciences
description: The course is purely mathematical in design reflecting infinite series, complex number, linear algebra, multiple integrals, vector and scalar field analysis. Vector operators; divergence, gradient, and curl. Divergence theorem, Stoke's theorem. Linear algebra and functional analysis, Fourier analysis. Transformations in linear vector spaces and matrix theory. Differential equations, integral transforms and coordinate transformation as well as special functions (Dirac-Delta functions). Fourier series and Fourier transform are taught. Applications of Laplace transform, Poisson theorem, Green theorem, and Schrödinger Equations in solving electromagnetic field problems are taught.

The course analyses the statistical principles of thermodynamics beginning with temperature with focus on work, heat, ideal gases, reversibility and irreversibility. The first, second and third laws of thermodynamics will be fully studied. Concurrently, the course will bring to bear the practical application of the statistical mechanics, thermal properties of solid and chemical equilibrium. Entropy.

The course focuses on general principles of optics such as image formation by mirrors (plane and curved), lenses, and optical instruments. General characteristics of waves to include displacement, amplitude, wavelength, period and frequency are emphasized. Other features of wave are reflection, total internal reflection, mirage formation, refraction, deviation, interference, superposition of waves are taught. The course also includes sound waves and its associated characteristics such as standing waves, travelling waves, longitudinal and transverse waves which are taught in-depth. Musical sound; sources, intensity and sound levels with special mention of tuning fork, resonance, Doppler Effect, and speed of sound in different media (solid, liquid and gases) and their properties. The wave equations (2-D and 3-D equations),Wave energy and power, Phase and group velocities, Echo and Beats are taught. Nature and propagation of light. Reflection and refraction at plane and spherical surfaces, and internal reflection. Scattering of light. Dispersion, Huygen's principle (interference and diffraction) are all emphasized.

The course advances in phys317 with more emphasis on light waves by using physical applications and geometry to study concepts such as of Prism, angle of Deviation, Spherical refracting surface, Snell's Law, refractive index, light polarization. Diffraction, Young's slits Experiment: Coherence and Non-coherence, Double --slit Interference, Multiple-Slit Interference, Michelson's Interferometer, Doppler's Effect for light, Speed of light, Fourier transform spectroscopy, Lloyd's mirror Fraunhofer diffraction. The diffracting grating and spectrometer. Thgin films, Diffraction and Scattering. Polarization and polarization states. Unpolirized and partially polarized light. Brewster's angle. Polarizing beam splitters. Photometry and light spectrum analysis. Resolution of optical instruments. Transmission lines, Wave guides and optical cavities. Lasers (rate equation, steady state operation, threshold and efficiency are also taught to enable the student advance in communication fields.

Gravitational force, gravitational potential, gravity field of the Earth, figure of the Earth, reference Ellipsoid, equivalent surface layer, global gravity anomalies, gravity measurement and reduction; isostacy, isostatic models and corrections; anomaly separation and enhancement, interpretation of gravity anomalies; depth and total mass estimates; applications of gravity method in mineral exploration, groundwater and geologic mapping. Basic concepts of magnetism, geomagnetic field and magnetic anomalies, magnetic survey, and instruments, magnetic data acquisition and reduction, interpretations and applications of magnetic data.

course_title: Electrical and Electromagnetic Methods of Geophysics
description: Rock electrical Resistivity and Conductivity, the vertical Electrical Sounding, Limitation of Resistivity method. Iso-apparent resistivity maps, Resistivity surveying, electrode arrays, Electrical resistivity Tomography, Electrical Self Potential (SP) survey, Induced Polarization (IP). Fundamentals of electromagnetism, Depth of penetration of electromagnetic fields, subsurface conductivity and electromagnetic responses are emphasized. Principles of electromagnetic surveys, Types of electromagnetic surveys, Ground Electromagnetic survey, Airborne electromagnetic survey, and Downhole electromagnetic surveying.

History of solar observations alongside solar activity and its effects are introduced. Magnetic fields of the Earth and other planets. Earth's magnetosphere, inter-planetary medium. Observing the magnetosphere and interplanetary medium. Space weather, Aurora. Solar and magnetic storm. Introduction to astronomy, structure and evolution of the universe, galaxies, stars, and sun. Evidence of the Big Bang theory, the solar system, gravitation and the planets. Astronomical observations at all wavelengths. Cosmic rays, basic cosmology. Hydrogen spectral lines and Doppler's effect. Structure and formation of the Earth's ionosphere, ionospheric observation techniques (space and ground techniques). Ionosphere as medium of communication, its layers and variability, latitudinal stratification and seasonal differences ionospheric dynamics (at low, equatorial, medium, and high latitude). Factors that affect the ionosphere. Ionospheric models.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

course_title: Methods of Teaching Physics in Secondary Schools
description: This course explores a variety of instructional strategies and methods for effectively teaching physics in secondary school settings. Students will learn how to engage, convey complex concepts, and foster a deep understanding of physics principles. The course covers traditional and modern teaching methods, including lectures, demonstrations, laboratory work, inquiry-based learning, problem-solving, the use of technology, flipped classrooms, cooperative learning, project-based learning, and differentiated instruction. Emphasis is placed on practical application, classroom management, and creating an inclusive learning environment that caters to diverse student needs. The course offers a review of Liberian viz-a-viz West African Senior Secondary Certificate Examination (WASSCE) physics curriculum. Performance or Project-based assessment and evaluation of learning is emphasized. *Field Experience Required:*

This seminar course engages students in extensive discussions about the teaching methods and techniques in physics. Through research, presentations, and discussions, students will demonstrate and reflect on the practices of teaching physics in secondary schools. Emphasis is placed on critical thinking, problem-solving, and effective scientific communication among high school students and colleagues.

The course focuses primarily on every aspect of Nuclear Physics, highlighting the concept of the nucleon and the properties of the nuclei, nuclear binding energy, radioactive decay, nuclear reaction, nuclear fusion fundamental forces, and quarks. Practical applications of Nuclear Physics such as number conservation and particle accelerators and detectors will be studied

This course offers students hands-on experiences of teaching physics contents in high school classrooms and labs. Through supervised internships, students will apply physics concepts in real-world settings, develop problem-solving skills, and explore career opportunities. The course includes mentor guidance, reflective discussions, and a final presentation or report on the internship experience.

Beginning with a review of mathematical analysis of vector, the course explores discussions on electric charge and field, electric potential and electric current, Coulomb's law, Gauss's laws of electromagnetism, EM Induction Faraday's law, Lenz's law, Biot-Savart's law are emphasized. Electrostatic and Magnetostatics. Magnetization and magnetic susceptibility. Laplace's equation and boundary value problems. Multipole expansions. Electromagnetic waves in dielectric

The course advances upon phys411 and studies in-depth magnetic forces and fields. Core areas of concentration include, among others, displacement of current, electromagnetic waves, momentum and radiation pressure, electric dipole radiation, sinusoidal electromagnetic waves and electromagnetic spectrum. The course advances upon Gauss' law of Magnetism with a focus on Ampere's Law, Maxwell Equations and electromagnetic waves. Polarization of EM waves, A.C circuits. Maxwell's equations. Lorentz covariance and special relativity. Gauss theorem in dielectrics. Poisson's equations. Uniqueness theorem, Solenoid, Motors, Dynamo, Generators, Poynting vectors are studied in-depth.

Students must have offered phys318 and are expected to study in-depth about wave function in relation to Properties of atoms, time evolution of Schrodinger's equation and the hydrogen atom, the energies of the hydrogen atom states, orbital angular momentum and magnetism, spin angular momentum and magnetism. The hydrogen atom and wave functions, Stern-Gerlach experiment. Identical particles and Pauli exclusive principle. Multi-electrons atoms, Spectroscopy, and the periodic table, characteristics of x-ray spectrum, x-ray and numbering of the elements. Approximation method , Variational methods and WKB approximation for band states and tunnelling. Time-independent perturbation theory. The fine structure of hydrogen, Creation and Annihilation operators. External fields, Zeeman and Stark Effects in hydrogen. Lasers and masers light.

The course is a continuation of phys413 designed to enable the students advance in theoretical physics. The course contents include Time-independent and Time-dependent perturbation theory, scattering theory, elastic potential scattering. Green's function and Partial wave method, Symmetries in quantum mechanics, Rotations, space --time reflections and parity. Selection rules for atomic transitions. Emission and absorption of radiation, Selection rules for hydrogen. Other areas of emphasis include Description and interpretation of selected phenomena from each of atomic physics, molecular physics, solid-state physics, and nuclear physics using quantum mechanical models. Relativistic wave equation, The Klein- Gordon equation, The Dirac equation, Chirality, Lorentz invariance and non-relativistic limit are to be well taught.

The course focuses primarily on every aspect of Nuclear Physics, highlighting the concept of the nucleon and the properties of the nuclei, nuclear binding energy, radioactive decay, nuclear reaction, nuclear fusion fundamental forces and quarks. Practical application of Nuclear Physics such as number conservation and particle accelerators and detectors will be studied.

Physics of solar radiation and the Earth's atmosphere as it focuses on the interaction between the Sun's radiation, and the Earth's atmosphere. The course aims to understand the physical processes that govern the transport and scattering of solar radiation in the Earth's atmosphere. Essential of climate change and weather pattern as the behaviour of solar radiation in the atmosphere is a critical factor in determining the temperature and weather conditions on the Earth's surface. Concepts of the Sun, its composition, energy transfer, its interaction with the Earth's atmosphere are emphasized. The students would be exposed to the physics of the atmosphere, its structure and composition, and management of the Earth's resources. Spectrum of the solar radiation in order of increasing wavelength, temperature and pressure variation with altitude are taught.

Magnetic data: Qualitative and Quantitative analysis of magnetic data. Forward and Inverse modelling, interpretation pitfalls. Gravity data: Forward and Inverse modelling, interpretation pitfalls. Electromagnetic data: profile interpretation, gridding of electromagnetic data, pseudo-section interpretation; Depth and Skin depth estimations, interpretation pitfalls. Electrical Resistivity data: resistivity Depth Sounding, resistivity profiling and mapping, 2-D Tomography, interpretation pitfalls. Practical applications of some relevant software. Radiometric data: introduction to radiometric methods, Ratios of Radiometric data; Ternary Radiometric map; Radiogenic Heat Estimation, interpretation pitfalls. Seismic data: basic Earthquake, exploration data analysis and interpretation.

The course is designed for students majoring in Communication Physics and Space Physic, and advances of phys332. The outline includes; Introduction and brief history of satellite communications. Satellite orbits, satellite configurations, satellite launching and launching vehicles, space environment, satellite bus subsystem. Types of propagation and effects, impacts of propagation and effects on satellite communication links. Propagation path loss, propagation models. Sources of error in satellite communication. VSAT systems, LEO's and non-geostationary satellites. DBS, TV and radio, satellite navigation, Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS), Global Positioning System (GPS), GLONASS, BEIDOU. Some regional satellite navigation systems.

This course aims at introducing students to the history, basic theories, and research methods in psychology. This course aims at ensuring that students will have basic knowledge of some psychological theories underlying behavior and also be able to apply what they have learnt to everyday life.

course_title: Introduction to Public and Community (Theory and Practice)
description: Math 108 chsc201, chsc202 This introductory course introduces students to the various disciplines and professionals of the public health field. It introduces the profession of public health as a theoretical science and a practical science. It provides topics on the history of public health, core functions and tools of public health, and the legal and ethical basis of public health at the local, national, and global levels as applied in the policies and practice. Community health concepts and the determinants of health and diseases are also introduced. The concept of community entry and community engagement in community public health activities is covered and emphasized. The course includes fieldwork trips that will expose students to public health settings where they will work on projects and will complete in their daily functions as public health professionals. The basic theories and skills of personal and community health promotion within a public health context is explored, using a broad multi-disciplinary perspective. Selected health topics will be highlighted (including stress, nutrition, self-esteem, substance abuse, violence, and sexuality) with emphasis on individual and group behaviors, and the implications for personal and community health. The ethical and legal rights of all individuals, groups and communities as it relates to health and welfare be discussed.

Students are introduced to the concepts and methods of bio-statistical data analysis. Topics include descriptive statistics, probability, standard probability distributions, sampling distributions, point and confidence interval estimation, hypothesis testing, power and sample size estimation, one- and two-sample parametric and non-parametric methods for analyzing continuous or discrete data, and simple linear regression.

The course introduces students to Liberia's health information system, here students collect, analyze, and interpret available data at the county level and learn how to put epidemiological health information into its proper context. Diseases that have high frequency and are severe and which are preventable or controllable are given priority.

This course is designed to provide students with an overview of the contributions of the social and behavioral sciences to public health. Topics include the identification of major behavioral, psychosocial, and cultural factors that influence public health; a discussion of leading social and behavioral theories used to understand and modify these underlying factors, and the basic processes involved in planning, implementing, evaluating, and disseminating effective prevention programs. This course addresses behavioral change at the various stages, involvement with counseling, mediation, prayers, spirituality, and traditional practices.

This course is designed to cover the important concepts in epidemiology and their application in epidemiological research. The emphasis is on measures and quantitative techniques, proper interpretation, and explanation of quantitative measures and results. The course covers screening and diagnostic tests and epidemiologic study designs. In screening and diagnostic tests, the validity, sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values of screening tests are presented and under epidemiologic study designs observational cohorts' studies, cross-sectional studies, case-control studies, and clinical trials are discussed. Ethical issues related to epidemiological study designs are also included in the course. If surveys or other epidemiological studies are going on in the country, students will be encouraged to visit and or participate.

This course covers the life stage of community-based programs from inception, implementation, and sustainability. It applies theoretical concepts from the social and behavioral sciences, health education, and health communication to the planning, design, and evaluation of community-based interventions. Characteristics of theory-based interventions are discussed, critiqued, and assessed for relevance to the needs of the students who will have the opportunity to apply these ideas to their work.

course_title: Introduction to Environmental and Occupational Health
description: This course involves a survey of major topics of environmental health: sources, routes, media, and health outcomes associated with biological, chemical, and physical agents in the environment, effects of agents on disease, issues related to water quality, sanitary disposal of human excreta, proper siting of wells and latrines(i.e. is how far should a well be from a latrine, vice versa), students should be able to determine how much water in the well to determine how much chlorine to put in that well, air quality, food safety, and land resources. Current legal frameworks, policies, and practices associated with environmental health and intended to improve public health are discussed.

course_title: Monitoring and Evaluation of Public Health Programs
description: This course is designed to teach students the four stages of a monitoring and evaluation (MandE) system: planning, data collection, making data usable, and using data for decision-making to help organizations, including public health agencies, reflect on and strengthen their plans.

course_title: Foundation of Family, Maternal and Child Health
description: The course consists of maternal and reproductive health, child health, and family planning. It focuses on these three related areas. The health of the mother, including issues related to reproduction, and the health of the child including nutrition, nutrition and traditional malpractices, breastfeeding, hygiene, family planning, child spacing, and having children when parents are prepared to receive them are all covered. Students are required to visit antenatal, family plannings, and pediatric clinics to participate in growth monitoring activities.

In this course, students are taught the basic concepts and principles, methods, and techniques of research that enable them to understand the research process. This puts them in a position where they would be ready to participate in research or carry out their research. As part of the course requirements, students, with the help of the course faculty select a research topic as a prerequisite to research proposal development in the first semester of the final year.

course_title: Introduction to Health Policy and Management
description: Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

This course covers the life stage of community-based programs from inception, implementation, and sustainability. The course applies theoretical concepts from the social and behavioral sciences, health education, and health communication to the planning and design of community-based interventions. Characteristics of theory-based interventions are discussed, critiqued, and assessed for relevance to the needs of the students who will have the opportunity to apply these ideas to their work.

This course considers the relationship between environmental factors and the occurrences of disease in human populations and provides an overview of the collection, monitoring, and interpretation of such environmental data.

course_title: Gender, Race, Ethnicity, and the Social Determinants of Health
description: This course examines gender, racial, and ethnic inequities in society. It also examines the factors contributing to said inequities, which are associated with demographic, scientific, clinical, economic, social, political, ethical, and legal issues.

The course helps students to understand epidemiological patterns, etiology, and risk factors of infectious diseases as they occur in populations, rather than in individual patients. Familiarity with epidemiological terminology and biostatistics is required.

This course covers simple and multiple linear regressions, ANOVA (Analysis of Variance), ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance), model-building procedures, and diagnostics with applications in health research.

This course provides an overview of preparedness strategies, emergency planning, and assessment of hazards and resources. Studies include in-depth planning and analytical framework, hazard/vulnerability analysis, and management.

This course provides an overview of basic frameworks for understanding national and global health issues and the improvement of health at a population level. This includes cross-cutting issues underlying the strategies and organization for the delivery of health care and population health services regionally, nationally, and globally. Approaches to regional and global cooperation to address health issues that cross national borders and/or require consistent multinational approaches for successful identification, interventions, and confinement will be discussed. Health issues of national and international concerns such as viral hemorrhagic fevers, cholera, Guinea worm, Polo, Buruli ulcer, and other such diseases/ conditions will be explored.

430 This course comprises field work at approved Public Health establishment sites for at least a period of 8 weeks (320 hours). Focus on strengthening competence in public health skills through practical experiences. The concepts presented via coursework are integrated and assimilated through an internship, which provides an opportunity for each student to apply his or her knowledge in a practice setting. Internships are individually tailored to assure competence in general and to meet student goals and the needs of the agencies involved. The internship is done and completed from June to August at the end of the student's sixth semester . Each student is to be visited at least twice during the 8 weeks of field activities. The internship and the special project must be approved by the student's assigned faculty before registering. The students are to report to their supervisors weekly to assess progress on data collection, analysis, and interpretation.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

This course looks at the integration of trending public health topics and issues. The topics discussed here are of national and international concerns; the list is dynamic and may vary from time to time. The list may include psychological behaviors, social issues, issues of defaulters in treatment courses, challenges of disease control in Liberia, issues of safe domestic water and good sanitation systems, rape, Ebola, and related issues

course_title: Health and Social Welfare Policy, and Management
description: The course focuses on ethical issues in health-care policy and management as well as the regulatory framework concerning social welfare policy and management in the country. Topics include patient informed consent, privacy, medical malpractice, resource allocation at the national, ministry, and county levels (equity), and control of health hazards. The course also examines the legal environment concerning the establishment and control over health-care facilities, licensure of health professionals, drug production, and administration.

The course introduces students to health information systems, their structure, components, and uses in decision-making, policy, and planning. It concentrates on health information systems and subsystems, data resources, and common problems related to health information systems. It explores information needs and the selection of proper indicators and ways to enhance their use in decision-making and planning. It examines routine and non-routine data collection methods and introduces the advantages of available software possibilities to support management activities.

This course covers microeconomic and macro analyses of the structure of healthcare and economic incentives facing healthcare providers, patients, and hospitals. Emphasis is placed upon efficiency goals of healthcare policy, and the use of economic analysis in the design of such policies. It is divided into modules for efficiency and effectiveness of materials and topics presentation: Module 1: The Foundation of Economics; Module 2: Introduction to Health Economics Module 3: Major Tasks of Economics and Health; Module 4: Health Economics in Liberia and Module 5: Application of health economics to access issues in Liberia.

This course covers the management processes and roles of public health professionals; leadership structures, decision-making processes, and health service organizations; policy issues and resource utilization and control within public health agencies including county health teams (CHTs) and the Ministry of Health (MoH). It also addresses human resources management and public health trends.
Project reports are prepared and presented to the Public Health Faculty at a seminar. These reports are completed during the last four weeks of the semester.

In this course, students learn how to use the computer to analyze epidemiological data. Commonly used software such as the District Health Information System (DHIS) statistical packages for social scientists (SPSS) and EPI INFO will be introduced.

The course helps students to understand epidemiological patterns, etiology, and risk factors of chronic diseases as they occur in populations, rather than in individual patients. Students will also be introduced to contemporary data about the burden of chronic noncommunicable diseases in Liberia and West Africa. Familiarity with epidemiological terminology and biostatistics is required.

course_title: Research Survey Methods in Public Health
description: This course provides advanced level to the design, analysis, and interpretation of sample surveys. Types of sampling covered include simple random sampling, stratified random sampling, systematic sampling, cluster sampling, and multi-stage sampling. Methods of estimation are described to estimate means, totals, ratios, and proportions. Development of sampling designs combining a variety of types of sampling and methods of estimation, and a detailed description of sample size determinations to achieve goals of desired precision at least cost will be covered.

This course covers essential computer-based techniques for a public health researcher, including data entry, editing, management, sub-sample selection, data encryption, and an introduction to Epi info, SPSS, and/or SAS will be covered.
This course studies the treatment technologies for water and wastewater. Emphasis is given to treatment technologies appropriate for developing countries.

This course provides a review and analysis of the Liberia Environmental Protection Agency and Liberian laws and regulations about the pollution, regulation, and protection of the air, water, and environmental issues in general.

This course considers the relationship between environmental factors and the occurrences of disease in human populations and provides an overview of the collection, monitoring, and interpretation of such environmental data.

course_title: Data Collection, Analysis, and Oral Defense
description: This course introduces the students who have done Research Methodology and Proposal Development to the basic steps in collecting, analyzing, and interpreting data. During this course, with the help of the assigned research supervisor, students can structure questionnaires and/or other instruments used in the collection, analysis, and interpretation of data. The application of ethical consideration in the collection, analysis, and interpretation of data involving animals and human subjects is maintained. Finally, an oral defense is made in front of fellow specialists and academicians thus completing the research work.

This course is an introduction to reproductive/prenatal epidemiology and its application in maternal and child health. It examines prenatal and family planning issues and emphasizes factors that affect reproductive, pregnancy, and infant health outcomes on a national and global level.

course_title: Public Health Capstone and Internship Registration Only

description: This course is the second part of a 2-part research course. It involves the development of a full research proposal containing an introduction to the study, a review of relevant literature, methodology, and oral defense. Ethical issues in general research are also covered in this course.

The course emphasizes the concepts of disease surveillance and integrated disease surveillance and response (IDSR), the objectives of IDSR, and how International Health Regulations (IHR) can be achieved through IDSR. It describes how counties can use the approach to strengthen surveillance and response with support from the Liberian Ministry of Health (MOH). It provides explanations of the specific objectives of IDSR in the Liberian context as well as the core functions of integrated disease surveillance and response (IDSR) with a key focus on the functions of surveillance at all levels (community, health facility, district, county and national). It demonstrates how IDSR promotes rational use of resources by integrating and streamlining common surveillance activities such as detection, reporting, analysis, and taking appropriate actions. Detailed information is given on the priority diseases, conditions, and events currently under surveillance in the African Region. The role of the laboratory in confirming the existence of specific diseases is highlighted in line with case definitions.

The course addresses today's global health security concerns and is a critical part of protecting global health. It emphasizes the need for all countries to have the ability to detect, assess, report, and respond to public health events. Key aspects of the course include knowledge of Epidemiology, surveillance, informatics, laboratory systems, and other essential disciplines.

This course provides an introduction to empirical research and a variety of research approaches upon completing the course, participants will be able to locate, understand, evaluate, and interpret qualitative as well as quantitative educational research and use these skills to identify possible Thesis or Project topics. Additionally, these students will learn how to collect data based on different data collection methods, construct these tools, and pilot them before they become ready for use. Moreover, students will learn to write a comprehensive research proposal which will be defended and submitted at the end of the semester. It will consist of the first three chapters of a research paper - Chapter 1 (Background of the Study), Chapter 2 (Review of Related Literature and Studies) and Chapter 3 (Methods of the Study).

This course is a continuation of science research methods (resm411) indicative of a thorough understanding and application of scientific research. Therefore, it will permit students to engage in full time research work under supervision. Empirical research design (laboratory design) will be of primordial significance, other forms of research designs descriptive, meta analytical research designs among others will be useful.
