This course engages students into in-depth discussions on major social theories that are employed in social research, counseling, and other modes of human and social services. In this light, students encounter issues and problems that are multifarious within the Liberian context. Topics covered include issues and problems that are economic, cultural, political, ecological, anthropological, psychological, legal and ethical in scope.
Placeholder support entry for a referenced course code that currently has no course-description definition in the six college curriculum files. Fill with the canonical definition.

The background and inception of sociology; the principal pioneers of sociology; sociological imagination, creativity and awareness. Understanding basic theories of sociology (Functionalism, Conflict, and Symbolic Interactionism). The value of social research and sociological techniques for examining society, including field studies and social surveys, instruments for obtaining information from surveys, participant and non-participant observation, focus groups, interviews, historical and documentary sources, ethical concerns, etc. Social structure, institutions, social functions, social system, social change, and other sociological concepts and phrases are used to analyze society and culture.

The nature and functions of social institutions. Understanding the characteristics and roles of social institutions from a comparative standpoint; systems of marriage, family, and kinship; religion and varying ways in which they are expressed; Political Organizations: upholding law and order under divergent political frameworks; formal bureaucracy and organizations; Current social concerns include issues with urban and rural environments, bribery and corruption, ethnicity, population and development, and more.

The course is designed to familiarize students with social work. The course examines various definitions, basic ideas, and the essential ideals of social work. The duties and responsibilities of social workers, the code of ethics for professionals, and moral conundrums in social work are covered in detail. Students who complete this course will gain fundamental interviewing skills that will help them work effectively with stakeholders in an efficient manner.

This course deals with reviewing and tracing the evolution and origin of the African Culture; the African environment, diversity and similarities of its people. It also discusses worldviews of Africans; Religious belief systems: the supernatural, witchcraft, sorcery, magic, etc.; Cultural norms, values and practices; Marriage practices, family, lineage and clan systems, kinship and descents; Land tenure systems and modes of economic production, distribution and exchange; Traditional political institutions; Law, religion and social control in African societies. Westernization and domination of Africa and Africa's adaptations and responses by Europeans and Americans and the impact of same on African societies and cultures from specific case studies of selected groups/societies in Eastern, Western, Northern, and Southern African regions.

The main objective of this course is to familiarize students with a range of analytical perspectives on the investigation of poverty and inequality. By the end of the course, students will have a better grasp of the ways that resources are dispersed both within and between societies, as well as how different groups are impacted by this distribution process. In light of this, the course will introduce students to the study of poverty and inequality through a comparative analysis of sub-Saharan Africa, Europe and the United States. Students will learn about a variety of socioeconomic issues in each of the above stated regions through readings and lectures, such as the effects of colonialism, unemployment, crime, pollution, prejudice, migration, etc.

The course examines the evolving field of gender and development by tracking changes in paradigm in this policy area over time, especially through important ideas like empowerment and gender mainstreaming, as well as by concentrating on particular fields like education, employment, and reproductive health. It also closely examines the gendered aspects of global economic transformation in addition to international development. The course discusses analytical approaches to development and sociological perspectives on gender. It examines the political arena of gender and development, highlighting conflicts both inside and across institutions and centers on the issue of empowerment, emphasizing employment and education.

This course discusses family as a fundamental social structure, and the relationship between marriage and family, the importance of descent groups, descent systems, kinship; succession and inheritance; Marriage: forms and procedures; stability of marriage, divorce, and its effects. It also reviews the types of extended family in comparative perspectives comparing Liberia and other countries in Africa. It explores family from a theoretical standpoint: conflict and functionalist viewpoints; important family-related studies; family from a theoretical standpoint: conflict and functionalist viewpoints; important family-related studies; Social transformation and the family: the rise of the nuclear family and its effects on decision-making, inheritance, authority, power, and member welfare. It also dives into discussions about family lifestyles in modern nations including cohabitation, single parenthood, mixed families, gay couples, single parents, and unmarried people.

Social Stratification is the study of social divides according to class, rank, status, power, and a variety of other criteria. This course combines theoretical readings, empirical works, lectures, class debates, and activities in order to investigate the issue of inequality in sub-Saharan Africa. How theories of stratification connect to current affairs and ongoing concerns about inequality in various regions of the world will also be covered in lectures and class discussions. Students will explore how stratification functions in the modern world and investigate three theories of stratification: Weberian, Post-Marxist, and Functionalist and the reasoning behind each theoretical viewpoint.

This course gives a historical overview of the concept of rural sociology. It discusses the fundamental framework of rural communities; population trends, family structures, politics, religion, health, education, and the economy, rural communities and migration; rural communities and social change. It discusses rural agents, the comparison between rural and urban populations; district assemblies; NGOs/Civil Society Organizations; faith-based organizations; and community-based organizations.

This course discusses sociological concepts as they relate to urban society; theoretical stances; the fundamental framework of urban life; urban growth; industrialization and urbanization. It discusses urbanism, religion, politics, economics, family, health, and education; their urban social relationships, and their integrative roles. Urban social issues including waste management, drug misuse, prostitution, crime, juvenile delinquency, and over-crowding are also key points of discussion in this course. It also discusses industrial relations theories such as the human relations model, social action model, Marxist model, and systems theory; factors affecting industrial relations patterns; causes of disputes and how to resolve them. It also reviews trade unions: their goals, development, and expansion; Union Organization; unions and the government; trade unions in developing nations, particularly in Africa; disparities between the social traits, development, and functions of trade unions in the developed and developing worlds.

This course is designed to provide students with a comprehensive understanding of current social problems both in Liberia and globally. By employing a variety of sociological viewpoints and the impact of Liberian cultural aspects on social problems, the course investigates the various levels of social problems. We'll also look at the difference between social issues and social problems and the different programs and policies put in place to address them. Various sociological ideas and perceptions of criminal and deviant behavior will be examined in this course. This entails examining historical and cross-cultural concepts of and responses to deviance, as well as critically assessing the connection between defining patterns of criminal and deviant behavior in society and societal perceptions of deviance. Drug usage, rape, violence, sex work, terrorism, teenage culture, and occupational crime are among the subjects to be discussed.

This course focuses on the conceptualization and historical analysis of Globalization. I t exposes students to the forces behind globalization: ICTs, multinational corporations, international organizations and financial institutions, neoliberalism---market and democracy, etc. Issues of globalization, such as imperialism and westernization; the influence of globalization on employment in nation-states, the shift in the global economy, and state capabilities; the effects of globalization on third-world societies, including the state, economic systems of production, consumption habits, and cultural norms; The effects of international migration, labor activity, and power in the Third World, opportunities and contending issues, will also be discussed.

This course will examine positivist and phenomenological philosophical schools; Sociological structuralism; conflict and consensus methods; Neo-Functionalism Marxism and Neo-Marxism: cultural evaluations of contemporary societies, Habermas' theories, and critical theory; Marxism with an emphasis on the past; Macro and the argument between micro or structure and agency; the description of the situation and sociological interpretation traditions; the social creation of reality; theories of exchange and rational choice; feminist theories, and their proponents will be discussed.

Sociology of Law is the sociological study of the relationship between society and the law, as well as how social variables impact and are influenced by the law. The course discusses topics like social norms and law in traditional and modern societies. It discusses major classical studies on the Marxist/Conflict, Functionalist, Durhkemian, and other approaches to the study of Law. It also discusses social control and deviance in society; Conflict resolution techniques, trial by ordeal, arbitration, adjudication, mediation, etc. The organization and functions of the police, judiciary, prisons, and human rights institutions, as well as punishment and corrections, comprise the justice administrative system. The program will provide students with the skills needed to critically examine, analyze and evaluate the interplay between socio-legal theory and practice, explore social policies, and the problems with social policies implementation at the national, international and global levels.

This course attempts to explain significance of social research in sociology and the social sciences. By means of conversations on both qualitative and quantitative research methods, students will be encouraged to develop into reflective learners in order to comprehend the ever-changing social environment. Writing research proposals, including data collection techniques, designing questionnaires and interview guides, observing participants, using key informants, conducting content analysis, holding focus groups, discussing the benefits and drawbacks of the data collection techniques, etc. ethical issues; methods for analyzing and presenting data; citing sources, segmenting, formatting, revising, and more will be addressed during the course.

The various definitions of concepts like "community" and "development”, as well as the responsibilities that planners and associated professionals might play in assisting communities in their development, will all be covered in this introduction to the area of community development. The fundamentals of this course has a strong emphasis on fairness and social justice, and students will be asked to consider some of their personal experiences and viewpoints. The main ideas, approaches, and procedures of community development will be examined in class discussions, along with how they have been used historically and currently. We will look at the social and physical elements that (dis)connect communities, planning strategies that promote or hinder growth, the importance of various types of capital, and the difficulties in striking a balance between community empowerment and professional expertise. The course will explore the physical and social factors that (dis)connect communities, planning practices that spark or impede development, the privileging of different forms of capital, and the challenges of balancing professional expertise with community empowerment.

This course is intended to serve as a resource for senior students as move on with the writing and research phases. Seminars and lectures will be conducted at various points during the semester to offer guidance, assistance and direction. The course will also include guest presenters who will give lectures and share their personal research-related challenges and setbacks. Our mutual obstacles are writing and research, and this course aims to help you overcome both. This is a supervised student research work which begins first semester of the senior year. Students are required to submit their topics for supervisor's approval before proceeding for the vacation, data collection and fieldwork placement.

Social policy analysis, the nature and limits of social policy, welfare state conceptions, the social policy framework, and social policy are the main topics of this course. It provides a summary of both society and personal responsibility and looks at the connection between social policy and fundamental human needs. In order to examine social concerns of poverty, development, health, education, housing, and social welfare, the course will assist students in appreciating social needs, social problems, and social work perspectives.

course_title: Organization and Administration of Sport and Tournament
description: This course will study the fundamental skills of sports administration including planning, organizing, staffing, coordinating, and budgeting in public, not-for-profit, and for profit sectors

This course will provide students with an overview of sport management and leadership concepts and practice. Course content will represent a variety of sport management concepts, segments, theories, and applications. Topics include the history of sport management, youth, community and scholastic sport and recreation, professional and international sport, the relevance and practice of managerial and leadership concepts within sport, typical settings and opportunities for sport leaders, diversity and ethical issues in sport, sustainability practices in sport, as well as a discussion of sport management education and research. Topics will be presented, explored and measured through a combination of lectures, discussions, readings, videos, guest speakers, individual and group activities, quizzes and exams.

After successful completion of this course, students will be able to develop a sound philosophy of marketing; understand the evolution of sport marketing theories; identify the major theories related to consumer behavior and market segmentation; identify contemporary issues and trends in sport marketing; increase competence of scientific inquiry in sport marketing; and create a marketing plan for a sport organization.

course_title: Construction and Management of Sports Facilities
description: Students are exposed to the theory and practice of sports facilities construction. Emphasis will be laid on construction of Athletics tracks and all field events facilities such as Shot-put, Javelin, Discus-Hammer (throwing areas), Long Jump, High Jump, Triple Jump areas. And Soccer, Volleyball and basketball

course_title: Sports Law, Ethics and Contemporary Issues in Sport
description: After successful completion of this course, students will be able to understand fundamental legal concepts relevant to tort law, contract law, and constitutional law; identify potential liability situations in the supervision, management and conduct of sport, recreation and physical activity (including situations regarding discrimination in terms of race, sex, and disability); and design and develop strategies for limiting liability. This class forces students to analyze the increasingly complex legal and ethical issues facing leagues, teams, event organizers, participants, athletes, entertainers, communities and others. In addition, students will consider the role of public policy and ethical decision--making. Students will learn the tools of legal analysis and problem solving, including issue spotting and the application of legal principles to disputes. Students review several broad issues related to sports law before turning to consider the legal issues routinely faced by sports managers. The class also considers Alternative Dispute Resolution (“ADR”) and the distinctions among arbitration, mediation and litigation.

course_title: Leadership And Personnel Management in The Sports
description: This course examines issues of management and organizational behavior within the broad context of the sports industry, with specific reference to issues of staffing, motivation, and communication. Introduces leadership theory, as well as the tools and techniques for its practical applications, including how to most effectively apply leadership theory on a daily basis within a sports organization.

A familiarization with the major components of bodies that govern sports competition. The bodies include recreational associations, The LFA, The CAF, Major sporting Organizations in Liberia and the world. Comparative studies will be conducted to establish the relationship between these bodies and how they affect each other.

course_title: Sports Administration and Event Planning and Management
description: This course studies the intricacies involved in the management and leadership of sports programs. Specific management techniques, administration techniques, and theories will be studied to provide the foundation for effective leadership and supervision of sports programs. Methods for designing systems and programs and sporting events within the context of sports administration are studied to give application relevance.

Explores issues related to sports from several perspectives-those of individual athletes, coaches, sports psychologists, trainers, sports managers, and that of the larger society. Issues discussed include goal setting for peak performance, motivation, competitive stressors, visualization, burnout and drug abuse, as well as sociological issues of race and gender as they relate to stereotyping, participation, and opportunity structure.

course_title: Sports Media, Social Networking and Brand Communication
description: After successful completion of this course, students will be able to explain the role of print media (newspapers and magazines), electronic (radio, network television and cable television) and new media (Internet and related digital and social interactive technologies) in escalating the interest, coverage, and importance of big-time college and professional sport, as well as niche-market sport. Students will be able to define the roles of media planners, producers, sports information directors, sports journalists, and specialized media-related companies dedicated to marketing, producing, and disseminating commercialized sport in North American and global contexts.
Same ase phed291.

This course provides an examination of organizational behavior from the perspective of the practicing manager. An understanding of group and individual behavior as related to the goals of organizations is explored. Critical factors such as developing successful communication, motivation, leadership, culture, and a working organizational structure are scrutinized. Additional topics covered include conflict resolution, power and politics, ethics in organizations, personality, and decision making.

Introduces the sport management student to financial challenges, financial statements, financial planning, and related issues within sport organizations, Introduction to Sport Finance and Budgeting, Sources of Revenue in Sports, Types of Budgets in Sport Organizations, Expenditure in Sport Organizations, Sponsorship and Fundraising, Legal and Ethical Considerations in Sport Finance.Etc

course_title: Legal and Ethical Issues in Sport
description: This course provides an overview of legislation, legal actions and ethical concerns in sport industry

course_title: Planning a Competition and Training Program
description: This course Identify the needs of Futsal athletes/teams. Devise a yearly plan appropriate to the Futsal athlete/team's needs .Develop appropriate communication skills to ensure the needs of the team, and the situations that arise, monitor team performances and provide appropriate tactical corrections it also includes practice methods, development of skill in games, supporting players and evading defenders to penetrate defenses, understanding and employment of blocks, feints, dummy runs etc.

Having identified a club, the student will assist with daily work office, administration, and logistics, assist with setups of team activation, warm-up and gym areas, and administer sports science measurements using GPS, HR Monitors if available, and athlete wellness reporting. Work with existing staff to analyze historical data looking at trends that can impact daily actions. Maintain cleanliness and upkeep of the Sports Performance environment, ensuring areas are kept at the highest professional standard. Demonstrate the ability to perform critical analysis by critiquing then making changes in their execution of the soccer skills of dribbling, shooting, and passing. Examine how participation in soccer can help them to meet national physical activity guidelines. Demonstrate knowledge of basic soccer techniques and systems. Demonstrate knowledge of basic soccer rules and strategies. Demonstrate the ability to dribble, pass, and shoot a soccer ball using the correct technique. Demonstrate higher order thinking by demonstrating knowledge of strategies for small group and full team soccer play. Develop soccer skills that will contribute to their physical, social and emotional wellness. Demonstrate the ability to write an analysis of their own physical activity behavior patterns. After the session a portfolio will be developed on his/her activities back up with a former report from the team management team to the department for evaluation.

An understanding of group and individual behavior as related to the goals of organizations is explored. Critical factors such as developing successful communication, motivation, leadership, culture, and a working organizational structure are scrutinized. Additional topics covered include conflict resolution, power and politics, ethics in organizations, personality, and decision making.

This course examines the history of Liberia's political system, county structure, and current issues and trends that are evolving in the Liberian society. It also explores questions about marriage, the family, employment, leadership, war and justice. Students will learn to critically evaluate the relevance of these issues to their individual situations.

Students are required to demonstrate teaching in human Kinetics (PHED) for 8 weeks in any accessible school. During the eight weeks, coaching lessons in any one of the games from year I and II should be demonstrated. Each lesson is of 45 minutes duration. Out of 10 lessons, 5 lessons are to be conducted in school. For the remaining 5 lessons, 4 lessons should be conducted for internals and 1 for the external examination. Both the classroom lessons and coaching lessons have to be incorporated.

This course focuses on the study of the major phyla of the animal kingdom. Classification, morphology, distribution, life history, ecology and economic importance of the invertebrate phyla of the animal kingdom are studied. Microscopic study and dissections of typical examples of the various phyla are stressed.

This course is a continuation of General Zoology. It focuses on the Protozoans and comparative study of the major invertebrate phyla with detailed study of the classification and morphology (anatomy and histology). Laboratory work includes dissection of the earthworm, snail, cockroach and crayfish, study of slides. Students should have a general knowledge of the more common invertebrate animals.
